title: Introduction to Multi-Modal AI
description: Explore how AI systems learn to see, hear, and create — from CLIP and vision-language models through diffusion-based image generation, speech understanding, and multi-modal agents that perceive and act in the world.

lessons.title: What is Multi-Modal Learning
lessons.content:
# What is Multi-Modal Learning?

A lot of “intelligence” is really *translation*.

You look at a scene and turn it into words (“a dog chasing a frisbee”). You read words and imagine a picture. You hear a siren and infer “an ambulance is nearby.” **Multi-modal learning** is the ML version of that: building models that can *process multiple kinds of data* and, crucially, *connect them*.

## Modalities: different “senses” of data

A **modality** is a type of input signal with its own structure:

- **Text**: a sequence of discrete tokens
- **Images**: 2D grids of pixels (often turned into patch tokens)
- **Audio**: waveforms / spectrograms over time
- **Video**: images over time (plus audio)

Single-modal models are great specialists (a language model for text, a vision model for images). Multi-modal models aim to be *bilingual* (or multilingual) across these data types.

## The core problem: how do we “meet in the middle”?

To combine modalities, we typically do two steps:

1. **Encode** each modality into vectors (embeddings).
2. **Fuse** those vectors so the model can reason across them.

A useful mental model: text tokens and image patches both become *a sequence of vectors*. Once everything is vectors, Transformers can start doing what they do best—attention over sequences.

## Fusion strategies (how modalities get combined)

There isn’t one “right” fusion method. The design choice is *where* and *how* the modalities interact.

| Strategy | Idea | What it’s good at | Common downside |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Early fusion** | Mix modalities near the input; run joint self-attention over everything | Learning tight token↔patch alignment; unified reasoning | Can be expensive; requires careful training to avoid one modality dominating |
| **Late fusion** | Process each modality separately; combine near the end (e.g., concatenate pooled features) | Simplicity; modularity | Weaker fine-grained grounding (less “word points to region” behavior) |
| **Cross-attention fusion** | Keep a strong backbone (often an LLM) and *inject* visual info via cross-attention layers | Powerful language generation grounded in vision; flexible | More architectural complexity; needs good alignment between encoders |

*Table note: synthesized from common VLM design patterns (adapted from Lilian Weng’s VLM overview).* 

### Cross-attention, pictured

Cross-attention is like giving the language model a “visual memory” it can query while generating.

![VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention to balance visual and linguistic signals (Adapted from Lilian Weng).](/api/wiki-images/multimodal-fundamentals/images/lilianweng-posts-2022-06-09-vlm_010.png)

## Alignment: making “dog” and *that region* mean the same thing

Even if you can fuse vectors, you still need **alignment**: the model should place related concepts from different modalities into compatible representations.

A classic alignment objective is contrastive learning: given a batch of (image, caption) pairs, train the model so each image embedding is closest to its matching caption embedding (and far from mismatches). Once you have this shared space, you can do things like **zero-shot classification** by comparing an image to text prompts.

Here’s the vibe in code (conceptually):

```python
# Pseudocode: compute similarity between an image and candidate text labels
img_vec = image_encoder(image)
text_vecs = [text_encoder(f"a photo of a {label}") for label in labels]

pred = argmax([cosine_similarity(img_vec, t) for t in text_vecs])
```

## A concrete system example: text-to-image models are multimodal

Text-to-image generation is a great “you can’t fake it” demo of multimodality: the model must map language into visual structure.

![Stable Diffusion: text encoder feeds numeric representations to image generator (Adapted from Alammar).](/api/wiki-images/multimodal-fundamentals/images/jalammar-illustrated-stable-diffusion_003.png)

Notice the modularity: a text encoder turns words into vectors, and the image generator uses those vectors as conditioning.

## Summary

**Multi-modal learning** builds models that can understand and connect different data types (text, images, audio, video). The key engineering choices are how you **fuse** modalities (early, late, cross-attention) and how you ensure **alignment** so concepts match across modalities.

## Recommended Reading

- [Generalized Visual Language Models](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2022-06-09-vlm/) — Clear taxonomy of how modern vision-language models fuse modalities.
- [Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020) — The core idea behind contrastive image-text alignment and zero-shot transfer.
- [Interacting with CLIP (Notebook)](https://github.com/openai/CLIP/blob/main/notebooks/Interacting_with_CLIP.ipynb) — Practical similarity scoring between images and text.
- [The Illustrated Stable Diffusion](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-stable-diffusion/) — Intuitive walkthrough of a multimodal generative system.
lessons.summary: Multi-modal learning trains models to process and connect multiple data types—like text and images—so they can ground language in perception and reason across modalities. The main design choices are how to fuse modalities (early, late, cross-attention) and how to align their representations so related concepts match across inputs.

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing cross-attention fusion mechanisms; use to contrast with prefix-based approaches and explain how complementary gates prevent catastrophic forgetting of linguistic knowledge.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualGPT showing a visual encoder feeding into a GPT-style decoder via cross-attention, with the self-resurrecting activation unit (SRAU) gating mechanism that balances visual information and pre-trained linguistic knowledge using complementary gates B_vis and B_lan.

image_metadata.caption: Transformers as the central model-definition hub across ML frameworks. (HuggingFace)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this when introducing the Transformers library for the first time, especially when explaining why a student should learn Transformers rather than a framework-specific API. Also useful when discussing MLOps tooling choices.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the Transformers library as a central 'pivot' or hub connecting model definitions to downstream frameworks. It likely depicts Transformers at the center with spokes or arrows pointing outward to training frameworks (Axolotl, Unsloth, DeepSpeed, FSDP, PyTorch-Lightning), inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TGI), and adjacent modeling libraries (llama.cpp, mlx).

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion: text prompt in, generated image out. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on Stable Diffusion to anchor the discussion in a familiar, tangible example before introducing architecture
image_metadata.description: A text-to-image example showing a text prompt input on the left and the resulting AI-generated image output on the right, illustrating the basic input/output interface of Stable Diffusion

image_metadata.caption: img2img: Stable Diffusion accepts both image and text inputs. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing text-to-image to contrast the two modes and prompt discussion about how image information is incorporated alongside text
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating image-to-image (img2img) generation in Stable Diffusion, showing both an input image and a text prompt feeding into the system to produce a modified output image

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion: text encoder feeds numeric representations to image generator. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first explaining that Stable Diffusion is not monolithic — use as a scaffold before drilling into each component individually
image_metadata.description: A high-level system diagram showing two main blocks: a 'Text Understanding' component (text encoder) on the left receiving a text prompt, and an 'Image Generator' component on the right producing the output image, with an arrow showing information flow between them

image_metadata.caption: Image generator internals: information creator then decoder. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into the image generator and introduce the concept of latent space processing
image_metadata.description: A diagram expanding the image generator block into two sub-stages: (1) Image Information Creator and (2) Image Decoder, with text embeddings flowing in from the text encoder on the left

image_metadata.caption: Image information creator: UNet + scheduler iterate in latent space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why increasing steps improves quality, or when students ask what the 'steps' parameter does in generation interfaces
image_metadata.description: A diagram highlighting the Image Information Creator component, showing it operates iteratively over multiple steps (the 'steps' parameter) in latent space, with a UNet and scheduler inside, before passing output to the image decoder

image_metadata.caption: Three neural networks in Stable Diffusion: CLIPText, UNet, Autoencoder. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary diagram after introducing each component individually, or as a reference when students need to connect component names to their roles
image_metadata.description: A complete architecture diagram labeling all three neural network components of Stable Diffusion: CLIPText (text encoder), UNet (diffusion in latent space), and Autoencoder Decoder (pixel image painter), with their connections shown

image_metadata.caption: Tensor shapes through Stable Diffusion: 77×768 → 4×64×64 → 3×512×512. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students are ready for technical depth — ideal when discussing why latent diffusion is faster than pixel-space diffusion, or when connecting to code implementations
image_metadata.description: A detailed tensor-level architecture diagram showing the exact data shapes flowing between components: text embeddings (77×768), noise latent tensor (4×64×64) into UNet+Scheduler, processed latent (4×64×64) into Autoencoder Decoder, and final image (3×512×512)

image_metadata.caption: Diffusion: random noise latent iteratively refined using text embeddings. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of diffusion — use to explain why the process is called 'diffusion' and how text guides the denoising trajectory
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step diffusion process diagram showing how a random noise latent array is progressively refined over multiple steps using text embeddings and the UNet, with each step producing a less noisy latent until the final array is decoded into an image

image_metadata.caption: UNet applied repeatedly: each step refines the latent toward the prompt. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the UNet's role in the diffusion loop, especially to clarify the relationship between the number of steps and the UNet's repeated application
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple UNet processing steps in sequence, with each step taking the current latent and text embeddings as input and outputting a refined latent, illustrating the repeated application of the UNet across diffusion steps

image_metadata.caption: Denoising steps visualized: structure emerges from noise step by step. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the key intuition-building moment when teaching diffusion — particularly effective when students ask 'what is actually happening at each step?' or when discussing the steps parameter
image_metadata.description: A visual sequence of decoded latent arrays at multiple diffusion steps (e.g., steps 1, 2, 4, 10, 20, 50), showing the progression from pure visual noise to a recognizable, detailed image, demonstrating how structure emerges during denoising

image_metadata.caption: Forward diffusion: clean image (step 0) to pure noise (step 4). — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when first explaining how diffusion models are trained — show it before discussing the noise predictor to establish what the model is learning to reverse
image_metadata.description: A sequence of 5 images showing a photograph progressively transformed from clean (step 0) to pure noise (step 4), illustrating the forward diffusion process used to generate training examples

image_metadata.caption: One training example: original image + noise = noisy image at step t. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of forward diffusion and before explaining the noise predictor architecture, to bridge 'how noise is added' with 'what the model learns'
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single forward diffusion training example: an original image plus a generated noise sample combined to produce a noisy image, with the noise amount labeled, forming one (noisy_image, noise, step) training tuple

image_metadata.caption: Many noisy training examples train the U-Net noise predictor. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use after explaining individual training examples to show how they aggregate into a full training regime; ideal before discussing the training loss or optimization step
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple training examples fed into a U-Net noise predictor, illustrating how a dataset of (noisy image, noise level) pairs is used to train the model to predict the noise component

image_metadata.caption: U-Net training step: predict noise, compute loss, update weights. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present when transitioning from 'what training data looks like' to 'how the model is optimized'; useful for students with ML background to connect diffusion to familiar training paradigms
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single U-Net training step: a noisy image and timestep are input, the U-Net predicts noise, the predicted noise is compared to the actual noise via a loss function, and gradients update the model weights

image_metadata.caption: One denoising step: subtract predicted noise to recover a cleaner image. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the 'generating images' section, immediately after training is explained, to show how the trained model is used in the opposite direction
image_metadata.description: A diagram of one denoising step during inference: a noisy image and timestep are fed into the trained noise predictor, which outputs a predicted noise slice that is subtracted from the noisy image to produce a slightly cleaner image

image_metadata.caption: Chained denoising steps progressively reconstruct an image from noise. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the single denoising step diagram to extend the concept to a full generation loop; useful for motivating later discussion of faster samplers like DDIM
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a second denoising step chained after the first, illustrating that the output of one denoising step becomes the input of the next, progressively reducing noise across multiple iterations

image_metadata.caption: Full image generation: iterative denoising from pure noise to final image. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining individual denoising steps; ideal for summarizing the generation process before introducing text conditioning or latent diffusion
image_metadata.description: A full end-to-end image generation diagram showing the complete reverse diffusion pipeline: starting from pure random noise, applying many sequential denoising steps via the noise predictor, and arriving at a final generated image

image_metadata.caption: Autoencoder compresses images to latent space where diffusion operates. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from basic diffusion to Stable Diffusion's specific architecture; use to motivate why latent diffusion is faster before showing the full latent diffusion pipeline
image_metadata.description: A diagram of an autoencoder showing an input pixel image compressed by an encoder into a smaller latent representation, then reconstructed back to pixel space by a decoder, with the latent space highlighted as the site of diffusion

image_metadata.caption: Forward diffusion applied to compressed latents, not raw pixels. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the autoencoder diagram to demonstrate how forward diffusion is adapted to latent space; bridges the autoencoder concept with the diffusion training process
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the forward diffusion process applied to latent representations rather than pixel images: an image is encoded to a latent, noise is progressively added to the latent across timesteps, generating latent-space training examples

image_metadata.caption: Complete LDM architecture: forward/reverse latent diffusion with text conditioning. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as the master architecture diagram after all individual components have been introduced; ideal for synthesis discussions or as a reference when students ask how text prompts influence generation
image_metadata.description: A two-path diagram (mirroring Figure 3 from the LDM paper) showing the forward process (image → encoder → noisy latents) and reverse process (noisy latents → denoising U-Net → decoder → generated image), with conditioning inputs (text prompts) feeding into the U-Net

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion architecture: latent diffusion with text conditioning. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing the full Stable Diffusion pipeline, after students understand basic diffusion, to show how pixel-space diffusion is replaced by latent-space diffusion with conditioning.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion) showing the forward diffusion process on compressed latents via an autoencoder encoder, the noise predictor (UNet) operating in latent space, the reverse process via the autoencoder decoder, and the text conditioning pipeline feeding into the noise predictor.

image_metadata.caption: ClipText encodes text prompts into token embeddings for image conditioning. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how text prompts are converted into a form the UNet can use; ideal after introducing the overall architecture and before explaining cross-attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a Transformer language model (ClipText, a GPT-based model) taking a text prompt as input and producing token embeddings, which are then fed into the image generation process as conditioning vectors.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP trains on 400M image-caption pairs crawled from the web. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the CLIP training explanation, before introducing the contrastive loss, to establish what kind of data CLIP learns from.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of a sample image-caption dataset used to train CLIP, showing paired examples of images and their descriptive text captions, representing the 400-million-pair web-crawled training corpus.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 1: encode image and caption, compare embeddings via cosine similarity. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the first step in a three-step walkthrough of CLIP training, immediately after presenting the dataset diagram.
image_metadata.description: Step 1 of CLIP training: an image and its caption are each passed through their respective encoders (image encoder and text encoder) to produce embeddings, which are then compared using cosine similarity — shown to be low at the start of training.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 2: update encoders so matching image-caption embeddings become similar. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as step 2 in the CLIP training walkthrough, directly after the initial encoding and similarity measurement step.
image_metadata.description: Step 2 of CLIP training: the image and text encoders are updated via backpropagation so that the embeddings of a matched image-caption pair become more similar, illustrating the gradient update step.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 3: trained encoders align matching image-text pairs in embedding space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final step of the CLIP training walkthrough to summarize what the trained model achieves and why negative examples are necessary.
image_metadata.description: Step 3 of CLIP training: after repeated training across the dataset with large batch sizes, the encoders produce aligned embeddings where semantically matching image-text pairs have high cosine similarity, and negative (non-matching) pairs have low similarity.

image_metadata.caption: UNet inputs: noisy latent, timestep, and text token embeddings in latent space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from explaining CLIP embeddings to explaining how those embeddings are used inside the UNet; bridges the text encoder and the denoising network.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the inputs to the Stable Diffusion UNet noise predictor: a noisy latent image, a timestep value, and encoded text token embeddings — all operating in latent space rather than pixel space.

image_metadata.caption: Training dataset: noisy latents paired with text embeddings and target noise. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the noise predictor is trained with text conditioning, to ground the abstract conditioning concept in a concrete data format.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the training dataset for the text-conditioned noise predictor, showing triplets of (noisy latent image, encoded text embedding, target noise) used to train the UNet to predict noise conditioned on text.

image_metadata.caption: Baseline UNet noise predictor: noisy latent and timestep inputs, no text. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing the text-conditioned UNet to establish the baseline; use as a 'before' in a before/after comparison when explaining cross-attention addition.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a diffusion UNet without text conditioning, showing its inputs (noisy latent image and timestep) and output (predicted noise), with internal ResNet blocks visible — serving as a baseline before text conditioning is added.

image_metadata.caption: UNet internal ResNet block layers before text-conditioning attention is added. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when diving into UNet internals, after the high-level UNet diagram, to prepare students for understanding where attention layers are inserted for text conditioning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the internal layers of the UNet noise predictor with ResNet blocks, illustrating the sequential processing steps within the encoder and decoder paths of the UNet before attention layers are added.

image_metadata.caption: Text-conditioned UNet: cross-attention layers inject text embeddings between ResNet blocks. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the text-free UNet, when explaining how text prompts influence image generation — use it to highlight the structural addition of cross-attention layers and why ResNet blocks alone cannot process text.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a UNet noise predictor modified for text conditioning, showing ResNet blocks interleaved with cross-attention layers that receive text embeddings as input, with labeled inputs (noisy latent + timestep + text tokens) and outputs (predicted noise).

image_metadata.caption: Step-by-step data flow through text-conditioned UNet layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the static architecture diagram (Image 1), when students need to understand the sequential processing order — particularly useful when discussing why text influence accumulates across network depth.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step walkthrough diagram of the text-conditioned UNet noise predictor, illustrating the sequential flow of data through alternating ResNet and cross-attention layers, showing how text token representations are repeatedly merged into the evolving latent representation at each stage.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'joint text-image training' paradigm; use to contrast how image regions are embedded analogously to word tokens in BERT.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text tokens and visual region embeddings (each summing tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss how researchers isolate the contribution of each design choice.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, pre-training stages).

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM encodes image patches as bidirectional prefix; text is decoded autoregressively. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SimVLM; use to contrast bidirectional vs. causal attention and explain why images are encoded as prefix tokens.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a bidirectional prefix encoder (cross-attention) and a text decoder with causal (unidirectional) attention, illustrating the prefix language model design.

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to reinforce why both the objective choice and data diversity matter in vision-language pre-training.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants such as different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM) and data mixtures (image-text only vs. combined with text-only C4 data).

image_metadata.caption: CM3 applies causal masking to interleaved text and image tokens from HTML pages. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting CM3 with simpler joint-training models; use to explain how masking strategy enables both generation and infilling in a multimodal context.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the causally-masked multimodal modeling (CM3) approach, showing how HTML-structured web content with interleaved text and image tokens is processed with a causal masking strategy that allows both generation and infilling.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM remains fixed, enabling few-shot VQA. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' approach; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain the motivation for freezing LM parameters.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training where only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) is updated to produce image embeddings compatible with a frozen GPT-style LM; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image embeddings prepended to text.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen LM outperforms fine-tuned variant on VQA; blind baseline shows LM prior strength. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion on why fine-tuning the LM hurts performance and what the blind baseline reveals about LM priors.
image_metadata.description: Two bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants (Frozen, Frozen-scratch, Frozen-finetuned, Frozen-train-blind) on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right), trained on Conceptual Captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix vectors via a lightweight network. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to show how a mapping network replaces direct vision encoder training and discuss the trade-off between transformer vs. MLP mapping.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings, a lightweight mapping network (transformer or MLP) translating them into k prefix vectors in GPT-2 embedding space, and a frozen GPT-2 generating captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap's image prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's mapping network; use as an intuition-building example to show that learned visual prefixes are not arbitrary but carry semantic meaning.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', etc.).

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SRAU gating mechanism to illustrate why balancing linguistic and visual signals matters empirically, especially in low-data regimes
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning metrics across data-scarce conditions

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT-2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing VC-GPT to illustrate the architectural solution to catastrophic forgetting before presenting the self-ensemble logit combination formula
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a language decoder (GPT-2), and a self-ensemble module combining vision-language fused logits with language-only logits

image_metadata.caption: Self-ensemble module significantly boosts VC-GPT captioning performance. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the self-ensemble formula to demonstrate its empirical importance via the 'VC-GPT w/o SE' ablation row
image_metadata.description: Results table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics

image_metadata.caption: MERLOT learns spatial and temporal video-language alignment via two objectives. (Zellers et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MERLOT to contrast image-only vs. video-based pretraining and to explain why temporal reordering is a useful self-supervised objective
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching training in MERLOT; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and a temporal frame reordering task on shuffled video segments

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo injects visual features into a frozen LM via Perceiver and cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Flamingo section to establish the overall architecture before diving into the gated cross-attention details
image_metadata.description: High-level overview diagram of Flamingo showing interleaved image/text inputs processed by a frozen vision encoder and Perceiver resampler, with cross-attention layers inserted between frozen LM layers to inject visual features

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer enables stable visual integration. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Flamingo overview to explain the specific mechanism preventing destabilization of the pretrained LM during multimodal training
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how a tanh-gated cross-attention output is added to the frozen LM's feed-forward output with a learnable scalar gate initialized to zero

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo performance scales with both model size and few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's few-shot capabilities to illustrate that both model scale and number of examples matter, and to motivate why large-scale pretraining is valuable
image_metadata.description: Line plots showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot to 32-shot) across multiple vision-language benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on many tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining Flamingo's training to contextualize the claim that it outperforms fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks without fine-tuning, prompting discussion of generalization vs. specialization
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo variants (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning

image_metadata.caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives in one architecture. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa to contrast its from-scratch dual-objective training with the frozen-component approaches of Flamingo and VC-GPT
image_metadata.description: Architecture overview of CoCa showing a unimodal image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch

image_metadata.caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to diverse downstream tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining CoCa's pretraining to illustrate how the same model handles recognition vs. generation tasks differently through pooler design
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained components are adapted to downstream tasks (image classification, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries

image_metadata.caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss sum (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is applied in practice.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including dual-loss computation with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.

image_metadata.caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC magic-score decoding formula to contextualize how well the approach performs relative to supervised baselines and other unsupervised methods.
image_metadata.description: A results table comparing image captioning performance of MAGiC and other models on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised methods and standard metrics (CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: PICa n-shot VQA pipeline: image → caption → GPT-3 prompt → answer. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to illustrate how image captions bridge vision and language models, especially when contrasting with end-to-end trained VQA systems.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how PICa performs n-shot Visual Question Answering at inference time: an image is converted to a caption/tags, few-shot examples are selected and formatted as a prompt, and GPT-3 generates the answer.

image_metadata.caption: PICa-Full vs. PICa-Base on OK-VQA: impact of example selection and ensembling. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how PICa improves over a random baseline, to motivate why similar-example retrieval and ensembling matter in few-shot LLM prompting for VQA.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing PICa-Base (random in-context examples) vs. PICa-Full (similar example selection + multi-query ensembling) on the OK-VQA benchmark, showing performance gains from each improvement.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models: composing VLM, LM, and ALM via language prompts. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students a visual anchor for the concept of language-mediated model composition before diving into specific task examples.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework, showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation to solve multimodal tasks without additional training.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models captioning: VLM fills prompt template, LM generates caption. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the image captioning application of Socratic Models, to walk students through each step of the VLM→prompt→LM chain before presenting quantitative results.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step illustration of how Socratic Models generate image captions: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, fills a language prompt template, and a causal LM generates the final caption.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models captioning vs. ClipCap on 100 COCO examples. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the approach's strengths and limitations in numbers, especially when comparing training-free vs. fine-tuned methods.
image_metadata.description: Table or chart comparing image captioning performance of Socratic Models against other models (including ClipCap and supervised baselines) on 100 random COCO test examples, using standard captioning metrics.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models for egocentric video: ALM + VLM + LM chained via language. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when illustrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to demonstrate how audio, video, and language models can be composed for complex real-world tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Socratic Models pipeline for egocentric video understanding: audio is processed by ALM (speech-to-text), video frames by VLM (image-to-text), outputs are combined into a language prompt, and LM generates activity summaries and answers questions.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF uses human feedback as a reward signal to optimize language models. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of an RLHF lesson when introducing why standard loss functions and metrics like BLEU/ROUGE are insufficient for capturing human preferences in language generation.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the high-level concept of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), showing how human feedback is used as a reward signal to optimize a language model beyond simple next-token prediction loss.

image_metadata.caption: Pretraining produces the base LM used to generate reward model training data. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the RLHF pipeline stages, specifically before discussing reward model training, to ground students in the prerequisite step of having a pretrained base language model.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the language model pretraining stage in the RLHF pipeline, showing how a base language model is trained before being fine-tuned with human feedback, serving as the starting point for reward model data generation.

image_metadata.caption: A reward model assigns scalar scores to text, encoding human preferences for RL training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining pretraining and before introducing the RL fine-tuning stage, when students need to understand how human preferences are operationalized as a numeric reward for policy optimization.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the reward model training stage in the RLHF pipeline, illustrating how a preference/reward model is trained to assign scalar scores to text outputs, with context on model size choices used by OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepMind.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT model. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the conceptual motivation for the KL constraint; use to transition students from intuition to formal understanding before discussing the InstructGPT paper's notation.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing it as the sum of two terms: the expected reward from the reward model and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy from straying too far from the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based solutions to LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the hallucination section, before presenting the InstructGPT empirical results, to create a productive tension between Schulman's hypothesis and the actual findings.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF worsens hallucination despite improving overall human preference. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Schulman's proposed fix (image 3) to highlight the gap between theory and practice, and use to spark discussion about evaluation trade-offs in LLM alignment.
image_metadata.description: A chart or figure from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit worse hallucination rates compared to SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Two complementary strategies for high-quality human annotation data. (Weng 2024)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on human data quality to orient students before diving into specific methods; use as a running reference to locate each new technique within the overall framework.
image_metadata.description: A two-branch diagram showing two complementary approaches to achieving high data quality: one branch focused on improving rater quality (e.g., better annotators, training, guidelines) and another on aggregation/modeling techniques (e.g., majority vote, weighted aggregation, learned models).

image_metadata.caption: Quality assurance as a feedback loop over annotation quality attributes. (Daniel et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing how to operationalize data quality standards; useful before explaining specific QA mechanisms like inter-annotator agreement or rater audits.
image_metadata.description: A structured diagram illustrating the quality assurance process for crowdsourced annotation, showing how quality attributes feed into a quality model, which in turn drives quality assurance actions that loop back to improve the annotation process.

image_metadata.caption: Non-expert AMT workers match experts in MT evaluation when spammers are down-weighted. (Callison-Burch 2009)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the wisdom-of-the-crowd concept and when discussing how to handle low-quality annotators; pairs well with explaining weighting schemes for aggregating noisy labels.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Bar chart comparing expert-expert vs. expert-non-expert agreement rates on MT ranking tasks under different weighting schemes (uniform, weighted by experts, weighted by non-experts), with a 1/3 chance baseline marked. (Right) Bar chart comparing BLEU scores of translations from AMT non-experts vs. LCD professional translators across multiple MT systems.

image_metadata.caption: Expert–non-expert annotation agreement varies substantially across conversation topics. (Wang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing sources of rater disagreement and the descriptive annotation paradigm; effective for motivating why demographic and topic metadata should be collected alongside labels.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap or scatter/bar chart showing Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients between non-expert crowdworker annotations and expert (Trust & Safety professional) annotations, broken down by conversation topic, revealing high variance across topics.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of root causes for rater disagreement in human annotation. (Zhang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the rater disagreement section; revisit when explaining disagreement deconvolution and the descriptive vs. prescriptive annotation paradigms.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical taxonomy tree diagram categorizing causes of rater disagreement into major branches such as task/item difficulty, annotator characteristics (identity, knowledge, values), and stochastic/random errors, with leaf-level examples for each.

image_metadata.caption: Four architectures for learning from multi-annotator labels on a shared BERT encoder. (Davani et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to model annotator disagreement computationally; use to motivate the multi-task architecture before presenting its F1 and uncertainty estimation results.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing four model setups for handling multi-annotator labels: (1) single model trained on majority vote, (2) single model trained on individual labels, (3) multi-task model with one head per annotator sharing a common encoder, and (4) a variant thereof — all built on a shared BERT backbone.

image_metadata.caption: Jury learning aggregates predictions from demographically specified annotator 'jurors.' (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from passive disagreement modeling to active jury composition; effective for discussions on fairness and whose values a model should reflect.
image_metadata.description: A conceptual illustration of the jury learning framework: individual annotators with demographic characteristics are treated as 'jurors'; a practitioner specifies a jury composition; the model samples predictions from each juror and aggregates them into a final verdict.

image_metadata.caption: DCN architecture jointly embeds comment text, annotator identity, and demographics for jury learning. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the jury learning concept when students need to understand the technical implementation; useful for students with a deep learning background who want architectural details.
image_metadata.description: A detailed neural network architecture diagram of the Deep & Cross Network (DCN) used for jury learning, showing parallel input streams for comment text (processed by a pre-trained language model), annotator embedding, and group/demographic embedding, which are combined through cross and deep layers before a final prediction head.

image_metadata.caption: Jury composition significantly shifts model predictions compared to an annotator-agnostic baseline. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the jury learning architecture to show its practical impact; effective for motivating discussions on fairness and representation in annotation pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Bar or line charts comparing jury learning against an annotator-agnostic baseline across multiple jury compositions (e.g., all-male, all-female, balanced, random), showing differences in predicted toxicity rates or F1 scores, demonstrating that jury composition meaningfully shifts model outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Influence function estimates closely match leave-one-out retraining on MNIST. (Kohn & Liang 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing influence functions as a data quality tool; use to justify why the approximation is trustworthy before discussing its application to detecting mislabeled or low-quality training examples.
image_metadata.description: A scatter plot comparing influence function estimates of the effect of removing each training point against actual leave-one-out retraining results on 10-class MNIST, showing strong linear correlation and validating the closed-form approximation.

image_metadata.caption: Data map of SNLI training set partitioned by confidence and variability. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the Data Maps method (Swayamdipta et al. 2020) — show it to anchor the abstract concepts of 'confidence' and 'variability' to a concrete visual partition of a real dataset before discussing how these regions relate to data quality.
image_metadata.description: A 2D scatter plot (data map) of the SNLI training set using a RoBERTa classifier, with axes for 'variability' (x-axis) and 'confidence' (y-axis). Points are colored by region: easy-to-learn (high confidence, low variability), ambiguous (high variability), and hard-to-learn (low confidence, low variability). Density contours show clustering of samples in each region.

image_metadata.caption: Label-flipped points shift to low-confidence regions, validating data map mislabel detection. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the data map figure (Image 1) when explaining how the hard-to-learn region relates to mislabeling — use it as empirical validation of the Data Maps hypothesis before moving to other detection methods.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side or overlaid data map scatter plot showing how data points shift in the confidence-variability space after their labels are artificially flipped. Originally high-confidence, low-variability (easy-to-learn) points migrate toward low-confidence, slightly higher-variability regions after label flipping, visually confirming that mislabeled samples appear in the hard-to-learn region.

image_metadata.caption: Threshold samples calibrate AUM cutoff to separate mislabeled from clean data. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the AUM method and the threshold-sample trick — show it after defining the margin formula to make the threshold-setting mechanism concrete before presenting experimental results.
image_metadata.description: A diagram or plot illustrating how artificially inserted 'threshold samples' (with known incorrect labels) are used in the Area Under the Margin (AUM) method to set a decision boundary separating mislabeled from correctly labeled samples. Shows AUM score distributions for clean, mislabeled, and threshold samples, demonstrating that threshold samples help calibrate the cutoff.

image_metadata.caption: AUM filtering vs. other methods on CIFAR-10/100 with varying label noise rates. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining both AUM and NCV methods — use it to compare methods empirically and prompt discussion about which approach is most practical and why performance varies with noise rate.
image_metadata.description: A bar or line chart comparing test error on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks under varying levels of randomly mislabeled training data, contrasting AUM-based data filtering against other methods such as NCV, MentorNet, and standard training with noisy labels.

image_metadata.caption: INCV algorithm iteratively removes mislabeled samples via cross-validated label agreement. (Chen et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from NCV to INCV — show it to make the iterative refinement process concrete, especially when students ask how the method handles high noise rates where a single pass is insufficient.
image_metadata.description: A pseudocode or flowchart of the INCV (Iterative Noisy Cross-Validation) algorithm, showing the iterative process: splitting data into halves, training on each half, identifying 'clean' samples whose labels match cross-validated predictions, removing likely noisy samples, and repeating until convergence.

lessons.title: Modality Representations & Embeddings
lessons.content:
# Modality Representations & Embeddings

A multimodal model has a deceptively simple job: take wildly different inputs (pixels, waveforms, words) and turn them into vectors that can “meet” in the same geometric space. The trick is that **each modality needs its own front-end**—an encoder that speaks its native language—before we can compare or combine them.

## The big picture: encoders + a shared space

Think of a **shared embedding space** as a common coordinate system. If an image of a “golden retriever” and the text “a golden retriever” land near each other in that space, you can do useful things:

- retrieve images given text (and vice versa)
- do zero-shot classification by comparing an image embedding to many text label embeddings
- feed visual/audio features into a language model after converting them into “token-like” vectors

A canonical pattern is:

1. **Modality-specific encoder** → produces features in its own internal dimension
2. **Projection layer** → maps those features into a shared dimension (e.g., 512, 768, 1024)
3. **Training objective** → shapes the geometry (often contrastive)

![CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_004.png)

## Images: Vision Transformers and patch embeddings

A **Vision Transformer (ViT)** treats an image like a sequence—similar to text—by chopping it into fixed-size patches.

- Start with an image of shape `(H, W, C)`.
- Split into patches of size `(P, P)`.
- Flatten each patch into a vector of length `P*P*C`.
- Apply a learned linear layer to get a **patch embedding** of size `D`.

Now you have `N = (H/P)*(W/P)` tokens, each `D`-dimensional, and a transformer can run self-attention over them.

Why patches? They give you a scalable “tokenization” of vision, and transformers are very good at learning relationships across tokens.

## Audio: from waveform to mel spectrogram

Raw audio is a 1D waveform, but most modern systems first convert it into a time–frequency picture.

A **mel spectrogram** is a 2D representation:

- x-axis: time (frames)
- y-axis: frequency bins on the mel scale (closer to human perception)
- values: energy

Once audio is a 2D grid, you can encode it with CNNs or transformer-style patching (patches over time×frequency), very similar in spirit to images.

## Projection layers: the “adapter plug” between worlds

Even if your image encoder outputs 1024-d vectors and your text encoder outputs 768-d vectors, you can still align them by learning small **projection layers**:

- image features `v ∈ R^{d_img}` → `W_img v ∈ R^{d_shared}`
- text features `t ∈ R^{d_txt}` → `W_txt t ∈ R^{d_shared}`

These layers are often just linear (sometimes with a nonlinearity), and they matter more than they look: they let you keep strong pretrained encoders “as-is” while learning just enough to make them comparable.

```python
# Minimal sketch: projecting two modalities into a shared space
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F

class Projector(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d_in, d_shared):
        super().__init__()
        self.proj = nn.Linear(d_in, d_shared, bias=False)

    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.proj(x)
        return F.normalize(x, dim=-1)  # cosine-friendly

img_proj = Projector(d_in=1024, d_shared=512)
txt_proj = Projector(d_in=768,  d_shared=512)
```

## Contrastive alignment: CLIP-style, SigLIP-style

To *shape* the shared space, a common approach is **contrastive learning**:

- take a batch of matching pairs (image, caption)
- pull matched pairs together
- push mismatched pairs apart

This is the core idea behind CLIP-like systems, and variants like **SigLIP** adjust the exact loss formulation but keep the same geometric goal: aligned modalities should be neighbors.

## DINOv2: strong visual embeddings before multimodality

Not every embedding model is multimodal. **DINOv2** is a great example of a *vision-only* model trained to produce high-quality image representations via self-supervision.

Why it matters here: in many multimodal stacks, you start with a powerful pretrained vision encoder (often ViT-based), then add a projection layer and multimodal training on top. Strong unimodal embeddings make the “bridge” easier to learn.

## Summary

Multimodal systems work because they respect the fact that each modality needs a specialized encoder (ViT + patch embeddings for images, mel spectrogram pipelines for audio). Projection layers then map these modality-native features into a shared embedding space, where contrastive objectives (CLIP/SigLIP-style) can align meaning across modalities.

## Recommended Reading

- [Multimodality and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)](https://huyenchip.com/2023/10/10/multimodal.html) — Clear overview of multimodal building blocks and training patterns.
- [Contrastive Representation Learning](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-05-31-contrastive/) — Intuition and objectives for learning embedding spaces.
- [Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020) — The core CLIP idea: align image/text embeddings at scale.
- [Processing images for language models (VLM overview)](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2022-06-09-vlm/) — How visual tokens/features get fused into language models.
lessons.summary: Each modality needs a specialized encoder to turn raw signals into meaningful features (e.g., ViT patch tokens for images, mel spectrogram features for audio). Projection layers then map these features into a shared embedding space, where contrastive training (CLIP/SigLIP-style) aligns semantics across modalities.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CLIP's capabilities and the concept of zero-shot classification, before explaining the architecture that makes it possible.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating CLIP's zero-shot image classification capability, showing how text prompts (e.g., 'a photo of a dog') are matched against image embeddings to classify images without task-specific training.

image_metadata.caption: Full 2D CNN architecture with two stacked convolutional layers. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a CNN introduction lesson as a motivating overview before breaking down individual components
image_metadata.description: A 2D convolutional neural network architecture diagram showing two stacked convolutional layers applied to a 9x5 input grid, illustrating the full modular CNN pipeline

image_metadata.caption: Naive fully-connected network applied to 9 audio samples. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for convolutional layers — use as the 'before' case before presenting the convolutional alternative
image_metadata.description: A diagram of 9 evenly-spaced 1D audio sample inputs connected to a fully-connected layer, representing the naive baseline approach to audio classification

image_metadata.caption: Neuron group A detecting local features in a 1D audio signal. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why convolutions exploit local structure and translational symmetry in sequential data
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single neuron group A applied to a local window of 1D audio samples, illustrating the concept of local feature detection with a shared filter

image_metadata.caption: 1D convolutional layer A (window=2) feeding into fully-connected layer F. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing the convolutional layer operation — use to explain the sliding window and weight sharing mechanism
image_metadata.description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A with a window of 2 points slides across 9 input samples, feeding into a fully-connected layer F

image_metadata.caption: 1D convolutional layer A with window size 3 over 9 inputs. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the 2-point window example to illustrate how window size is a tunable parameter in convolutional layers
image_metadata.description: A 1D convolutional layer diagram where neuron group A uses a window of 3 points, feeding into a fully-connected layer, showing a slightly larger receptive field

image_metadata.caption: Two stacked 1D convolutional layers A and B with shared weights. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how deep CNNs are built by stacking convolutional layers, emphasizing the hierarchical abstraction benefit
image_metadata.description: A diagram of two stacked 1D convolutional layers: neuron group A feeds into neuron group B, which then connects to a fully-connected layer, illustrating layer composability

image_metadata.caption: 1D CNN with Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture for translation invariance. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing max-pooling — use to explain why pooling is inserted between convolutional layers and what invariance it provides
image_metadata.description: A 1D CNN architecture diagram showing a convolutional layer, followed by a max-pooling layer, followed by another convolutional layer, illustrating the Conv-Pool-Conv pattern

image_metadata.caption: Single 2D convolutional unit A scanning a patch of an image. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from 1D to 2D convolutions — use to make the extension to image data concrete and intuitive
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single 2D convolutional unit showing neuron group A looking at a small 2D patch of an input image grid, illustrating the 2D receptive field concept

image_metadata.caption: 2D convolutional layer A applied to a 9×5 image input. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching 2D CNNs for image data — use to explain how a single convolutional layer processes an entire image via sliding patches
image_metadata.description: A full 2D convolutional layer diagram showing neuron group A sliding over a 9x5 input grid, producing a feature map output connected to a fully-connected layer

image_metadata.caption: Full 2D CNN: Conv-MaxPool-Conv architecture on a 9×5 image. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a capstone diagram when summarizing 2D CNN architecture — use to consolidate understanding of all components working together on image data
image_metadata.description: A 2D CNN architecture diagram showing a 9x5 input processed by two stacked 2D convolutional layers with a 2D max-pooling layer in between

image_metadata.caption: Standard conv layer: parallel neurons share input, learn distinct features. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the internal structure of a convolutional layer, specifically to explain what happens inside a single sliding-window application
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the standard convolutional layer module A as a set of parallel neurons all receiving the same input region and computing different features simultaneously

image_metadata.caption: Network-in-Network: stacked neuron layers inside each conv module A. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining standard conv layers, when discussing architectural innovations or the concept of increasing representational power within a local receptive field
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Network-in-Network (NIN) Mlpconv variant of module A, showing multiple stacked layers of neurons inside A rather than a single parallel layer, enabling higher-level feature extraction per region

image_metadata.caption: AlexNet architecture: 5 conv + 3 FC layers across two GPUs. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching landmark CNN architectures or motivating why depth and scale matter; use as a case study after explaining individual layer types
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the AlexNet (Krizhevsky, Sutskever, Hinton 2012) deep convolutional neural network, showing 5 convolutional layers with pooling and 3 fully-connected layers split across two GPU streams

image_metadata.caption: AlexNet ImageNet results: correct and near-miss classifications shown. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing model evaluation metrics or building intuition about CNN performance; effective for motivating why top-5 accuracy is a useful metric alongside top-1
image_metadata.description: Sample classification results from AlexNet showing input images alongside the network's top predicted categories, including cases where errors are visually understandable

image_metadata.caption: AlexNet first-layer filters: edge detectors and color patterns emerge from training. (Krizhevsky et al., 2012)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining what CNNs actually learn, or when motivating why convolutional architectures are well-suited to visual data; powerful for dispelling the 'black box' misconception
image_metadata.description: Visualization of learned first-layer filters from AlexNet, showing one group of neurons that learned oriented edge detectors (black and white) and another group that learned color/texture detectors (color contrasts and patterns)

image_metadata.caption: 1D conv layer: output y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) via sliding window. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When formalizing the convolution operation mathematically; show alongside the equation y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) to bridge diagram and formula
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a 1D convolutional neural network showing input nodes x_n connected through sliding module A to output nodes y_n, illustrating the formal relationship y_n = A(x_n, x_{n+1}, ...) with the convolution window sliding along the sequence

image_metadata.caption: 2D conv layer: y_{n,m} computed from a 2D patch of inputs. (Olah, 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after the 1D convolution diagram, when generalizing to 2D inputs like images; pair with the 2D index notation equation to reinforce the connection
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a 2D convolutional neural network showing a 5x5 (or similar) input grid connected through a 2D sliding module A to a 2D output grid, illustrating the formal 2D convolution relationship y_{n,m} = A(x_{n,m}, x_{n+1,m}, ...)

image_metadata.caption: Regular fully-connected neural network; scales poorly to image inputs. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing why CNNs are needed — use this to contrast with convolutional layers and motivate local connectivity
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a fully-connected (regular) neural network with multiple hidden layers, showing neurons as circles connected by edges across layers, illustrating the dense connectivity pattern

image_metadata.caption: ConvNet pipeline: INPUT→CONV→RELU→POOL→FC for CIFAR-10. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of CNN architecture discussion as an overview; revisit when explaining each layer type in detail
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a ConvNet pipeline showing the transformation of a 3D input image volume through CONV, RELU, POOL, and FC layers, with each stage depicted as a 3D block shrinking spatially but varying in depth

image_metadata.caption: Depth column: local 2D receptive field, full depth connectivity. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining local connectivity in Conv layers, especially to contrast spatial locality with full depth connectivity
image_metadata.description: 3D diagram of a convolutional layer input volume with a highlighted column of neurons showing local 2D spatial connectivity (receptive field) but full depth connectivity, illustrating the asymmetric connectivity principle

image_metadata.caption: Single neuron: weighted inputs summed and passed through activation. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students need a refresher on the basic neuron computation before explaining how filters in a Conv layer perform the same operation over local patches
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single neuron model showing weighted inputs being summed and passed through an activation function, representing the basic computational unit used in convolutional layers

image_metadata.caption: Stride controls filter step size and output spatial dimensions. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the three hyperparameters (depth, stride, zero-padding) that control output volume size in a Conv layer
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the effect of stride on the spatial arrangement of output neurons, showing how a filter slides across a 1D or 2D input with different stride values, producing different output sizes

image_metadata.caption: Parameter sharing: one filter per depth slice applied across all positions. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CNNs reduce parameter count via weight sharing; use to contrast with the 105M parameter count of a fully-connected first layer
image_metadata.description: Visualization of parameter sharing in a convolutional layer, showing that all neurons in a single depth slice share the same filter weights, with different depth slices using different filters (shown as colored weight maps)

image_metadata.caption: Pooling layer reduces spatial size per depth slice, preserving depth. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Pooling layer to illustrate its effect on volume dimensions before discussing the MAX operation in detail
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a pooling layer operating on a 3D input volume, illustrating how it reduces spatial dimensions (width and height) independently per depth slice while preserving depth

image_metadata.caption: Max pooling: 2×2 filter, stride 2 selects maximum from each region. (cs231n)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the max pooling operation in detail; the numerical example makes the abstract operation concrete and easy to verify by hand
image_metadata.description: Illustration of max pooling operation on a 4x4 input grid divided into 2x2 regions, showing how the maximum value is selected from each region to produce a 2x2 output, with specific numbers as examples

image_metadata.caption: Triplet loss: anchor pulls positives close, pushes negatives apart. (Schroff et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing triplet loss, immediately after presenting the loss formula, to help students map the mathematical terms (anchor, x+, x-, epsilon) to a spatial diagram.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing triplet loss with an anchor sample, one positive sample (same class, pulled closer), and one negative sample (different class, pushed apart), with margin epsilon illustrated as the minimum required distance gap.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive, triplet, and lifted structured loss compared by pair usage. (Song et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from triplet loss to lifted structured loss, to motivate why using all pairs in a batch is beneficial over single-pair or single-triplet approaches.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of three loss functions — contrastive loss, triplet loss, and lifted structured loss — using graph diagrams where red edges connect similar pairs and blue edges connect dissimilar pairs, showing how each method uses increasingly more pairwise information per training step.

image_metadata.caption: False negative sampling bias degrades contrastive learning performance. (Chuang et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the problem of false negatives before presenting the debiased contrastive loss formulation, to build intuition for why correction is needed.
