image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the memory bank limitation; use to explain how MoCo's queue enables reuse of recent mini-batch representations and why momentum updates stabilize training.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of MoCo (Momentum Contrast) showing a query encoder and a momentum-updated key encoder feeding into a queue-based dictionary, with arrows indicating how query and key representations are compared via InfoNCE loss.

image_metadata.caption: SwAV swaps cluster codes between views instead of direct contrastive comparison. (Caron et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how SwAV avoids explicit negative pairs while still preventing collapse; use the side-by-side layout to highlight what changes relative to instance contrastive learning.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison diagram of SwAV versus standard contrastive instance learning, showing how SwAV replaces direct pairwise comparison with prototype/code assignment and a swapped prediction loss between two augmented views.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP maximizes similarity for N correct image-text pairs in an N×N matrix. (Radford et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when introducing multimodal contrastive learning; the matrix diagram is ideal for explaining the batch-level pairing objective before discussing zero-shot transfer results.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of CLIP's contrastive pre-training showing an N×N similarity matrix between N image embeddings and N text embeddings, with diagonal entries (correct pairs) maximized and off-diagonal entries (incorrect pairs) minimized.

image_metadata.caption: NumPy pseudocode for CLIP's symmetric contrastive training objective. (Radford et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the CLIP architecture diagram when students need to understand the exact training procedure; particularly useful for students with programming backgrounds who benefit from code-level clarity.
image_metadata.description: NumPy-style pseudocode for the CLIP training algorithm, showing image and text encoder forward passes, L2 normalization, cosine similarity matrix computation, and symmetric cross-entropy loss calculation.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive, triplet, and lifted structured loss compared by pair usage. (Song et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from triplet loss to lifted structured loss, to motivate why using all pairs in a batch is beneficial over single-pair or single-triplet approaches.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of three loss functions — contrastive loss, triplet loss, and lifted structured loss — using graph diagrams where red edges connect similar pairs and blue edges connect dissimilar pairs, showing how each method uses increasingly more pairwise information per training step.

image_metadata.caption: False negative sampling bias degrades contrastive learning performance. (Chuang et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the problem of false negatives before presenting the debiased contrastive loss formulation, to build intuition for why correction is needed.
image_metadata.description: Visualization illustrating sampling bias (false negatives) in contrastive learning, showing how samples that are semantically similar to the anchor can be incorrectly treated as negatives, and the resulting performance degradation.

image_metadata.caption: Debiased contrastive learning yields better-separated t-SNE clusters. (Chuang et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the debiased contrastive loss formula, as evidence that the correction leads to qualitatively better learned representations.
image_metadata.description: t-SNE 2D visualization comparing learned representations with and without debiased contrastive learning, showing that debiased training produces better-separated, more coherent clusters per class.

image_metadata.caption: Pseudo-code comparing NCE, debiased, and hard-negative contrastive losses. (Robinson et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining all three loss variants mathematically, as a synthesis step to help students see how the equations translate into code and how the methods relate to each other.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block showing implementations of three objectives side by side: standard NCE loss, debiased contrastive loss, and hard negative sampling objective, with M=1, written in Python-style code.

image_metadata.caption: SimCLR: two augmented views trained to share embeddings via contrastive loss. (Chen et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing SimCLR as the canonical example of augmentation-based contrastive learning, to give students a complete architectural overview before diving into loss details.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimCLR showing: an input image augmented into two views, each passed through an encoder f(·) and projection head g(·), with the NT-Xent contrastive loss applied to the projected embeddings to maximize agreement between the two views.

image_metadata.caption: SimCLR training algorithm: augment, encode, project, compute NT-Xent loss. (Chen et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the SimCLR architecture diagram, when students need to understand the training procedure in detail or are preparing to implement it.
image_metadata.description: Formal algorithm pseudocode for SimCLR detailing the minibatch sampling, augmentation, forward pass through encoder and projection head, pairwise similarity computation, and NT-Xent loss calculation steps.

image_metadata.caption: Barlow Twins pushes cross-correlation matrix of twin outputs toward identity. (Zbontar et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Barlow Twins as an alternative to SimCLR, to highlight the architectural similarity but conceptual difference in the objective function.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of Barlow Twins showing two distorted image views fed through twin networks, producing embeddings whose cross-correlation matrix is computed and pushed toward the identity matrix via the Barlow Twins loss.

image_metadata.caption: Barlow Twins PyTorch pseudo-code: cross-correlation toward identity matrix. (Zbontar et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Barlow Twins architecture diagram and loss formula, for students who want to understand implementation details or compare with SimCLR's algorithm.
image_metadata.description: PyTorch-style pseudo-code for the Barlow Twins algorithm, showing network forward passes, cross-correlation matrix computation along the batch dimension, and the invariance plus redundancy-reduction loss terms.

image_metadata.caption: BYOL: online network learns from momentum target network without negative pairs. (Grill et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting BYOL with SimCLR to highlight that negative pairs are not strictly necessary, and to explain the role of the asymmetric predictor and EMA target in preventing representational collapse.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of BYOL showing an online network (encoder, projector, predictor) and a target network (encoder, projector only) with stop-gradient on the target, polyak-averaged weight updates, and the MSE loss between online predictor output and target projector output.

image_metadata.caption: Instance discrimination pipeline with memory bank for negative samples. (Wu et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining why computing all negative samples per batch is expensive and how a memory bank addresses this; use before introducing MoCo as the motivating predecessor.
image_metadata.description: Training pipeline diagram for instance-level contrastive learning showing how image embeddings are L2-normalized and compared against a memory bank of stored representations to compute a non-parametric softmax loss.

image_metadata.caption: CURL applies momentum-contrast contrastive learning to RL observations. (Srinivas et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing applications of contrastive learning beyond standard vision benchmarks, or when illustrating how MoCo-V2 ideas generalize to RL observation spaces.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of CURL showing two augmented views of a reinforcement learning observation being passed through a query encoder and a momentum key encoder, with contrastive loss computed between their embeddings.

image_metadata.caption: DeepCluster iteratively clusters features and uses assignments as pseudo-labels. (Caron et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from pairwise contrastive methods to clustering-based self-supervised methods; use to motivate why SwAV was designed to avoid DeepCluster's costly offline clustering phase.
image_metadata.description: Flowchart of the DeepCluster iterative pipeline: a CNN extracts features, k-means clustering assigns pseudo-labels, and those labels supervise the next round of CNN training in a loop.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive + bag-of-words encoding yields large data efficiency gains for CLIP. (Radford et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing CLIP's training objective design choices; show after the architecture diagram to justify why contrastive loss outperforms predictive captioning in terms of sample efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Line graph comparing data efficiency (ImageNet accuracy vs. number of training examples seen) across different pre-training objectives and text encoding strategies, showing that bag-of-words + contrastive objective achieves the best efficiency.

image_metadata.caption: Supervised contrastive learning uses all same-class samples as positives. (Khosla et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from self-supervised to supervised contrastive methods; use the visual contrast to prompt discussion of how leveraging labels changes what counts as a 'positive' example.
image_metadata.description: Comparison diagram of self-supervised versus supervised contrastive loss formulations, showing that supervised contrastive learning treats all same-class samples as positives (not just augmented pairs), while self-supervised only uses augmented views of the same image.

image_metadata.caption: Three cutoff augmentation strategies for text contrastive learning. (Shen et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing text-specific augmentation techniques for contrastive learning, especially to contrast with image augmentation methods and show how NLP adapts the concept.
image_metadata.description: Schematic diagram showing three text augmentation strategies: token cutoff (randomly masking individual tokens), feature cutoff (masking entire feature dimensions), and span cutoff (masking contiguous spans of tokens) applied to a sentence.

image_metadata.caption: SimCSE uses dropout masks (unsupervised) or NLI labels (supervised) for contrastive pairs. (Gao et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how contrastive learning is applied to sentence embeddings, particularly to illustrate the elegance of using dropout noise as augmentation versus explicit label-based supervision.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating SimCSE's two modes: unsupervised (same sentence fed twice through encoder with different dropout masks to form positive pairs) and supervised (NLI entailment pairs as positives, contradiction pairs as hard negatives).

image_metadata.caption: SimCSE achieves strong STS benchmark results across 7 datasets. (Gao et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimCSE's method, to ground the theoretical discussion in concrete performance numbers and motivate why the approach matters.
image_metadata.description: Results table comparing SimCSE (unsupervised and supervised variants) against baselines including BERT, SBERT, BERT-flow, and BERT-whitening across 7 STS benchmark datasets, reporting Spearman correlation scores.

image_metadata.caption: Sentence-BERT siamese architecture with classification and regression heads. (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing supervised sentence embedding methods, to show how BERT is adapted into a siamese architecture and how different training objectives (classification vs. regression) are applied.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Sentence-BERT showing siamese network setup: two BERT encoders sharing weights process sentence pairs, pooled representations are combined and passed to either a softmax classification head (for NLI) or a regression head (for STS).

image_metadata.caption: SBERT outperforms prior baselines on 5 of 7 SentEval tasks. (Reimers & Gurevych, 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SBERT's architecture, to show its real-world performance gains and introduce SentEval as a standard evaluation framework for sentence representations.
image_metadata.description: Performance table of Sentence-BERT on the SentEval benchmark, comparing SBERT against baselines (InferSent, USE, BERT CLS, etc.) across multiple NLP tasks including STS, sentiment analysis, and textual entailment.

image_metadata.caption: BERT-flow maps anisotropic BERT embeddings to an isotropic Gaussian space. (Li et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the geometric properties of pre-trained language model embeddings and methods to improve them without task-specific fine-tuning.
image_metadata.description: Illustration showing BERT-flow's approach: a normalizing flow model maps the anisotropic, non-uniform BERT sentence embedding space to a smooth, isotropic Gaussian distribution, visualized as a transformation from a clustered/skewed space to a uniform spherical distribution.

image_metadata.caption: Pseudo-code for Whitening-k: SVD-based sentence embedding normalization. (Su et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the whitening operation as an alternative to BERT-flow for correcting anisotropic embeddings, especially for students who need to implement or understand the mathematical procedure.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block for the Whitening-k operation, showing steps to compute mean and covariance of sentence vectors, perform SVD decomposition of the covariance matrix, compute transformation matrix W, apply whitening transformation, and optionally reduce to k dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Quick-Thought learns sentence embeddings by predicting context sentences contrastively. (Logeswaran & Lee, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing context-based contrastive objectives for sentence embeddings, to show how predicting surrounding sentences provides a self-supervised training signal.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Quick-Thought vector learning: given a sentence, the model uses two encoders f and g to encode candidate sentences, and learns to classify which candidate is the true context sentence versus negative samples, without a softmax output layer.

image_metadata.caption: IS-BERT maximizes mutual information between local token and global sentence representations. (Zhang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching mutual information-based contrastive objectives, to show how the local-global MI maximization principle from vision (DIM) transfers to sentence-level NLP tasks.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of IS-BERT (Info-Sentence BERT) showing how a global sentence representation from BERT's [CLS] token is contrasted with local token-level representations using mutual information maximization via a discriminator network.

image_metadata.caption: IS-BERT SentEval results comparing MI-based training to supervised baselines. (Zhang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining IS-BERT's mutual information objective, to contextualize its performance gains and compare unsupervised MI-based methods against supervised approaches like SBERT.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison table of IS-BERT on the SentEval benchmark against baselines including InferSent, USE, SBERT, and others, showing scores across supervised and unsupervised settings on multiple NLP tasks.

lessons.title: Visual Instruction Tuning
lessons.content:
# Visual Instruction Tuning

Vision-language models feel magical because they do something we don’t usually get “for free” in ML: they let a language model *talk about pixels* in an open-ended way. Not just “cat vs dog,” but “What’s weird about this chart?” or “Rewrite this sign in polite French.”

![VLMs handle diverse tasks from captioning to document understanding. (HuggingFace Blog)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huggingface-co-blog-vlms_001.jpg)

## From “image features” to “visual tokens”

A modern **vision-language model (VLM)** typically starts with a strong **vision encoder** (often a ViT-like model) that turns an image into a grid/sequence of vectors. You can think of these vectors as **visual tokens**: each one summarizes a patch or region.

But there’s a mismatch:
- the vision encoder outputs vectors in its own feature space (say 1024-d)
- the LLM expects token embeddings in *its* space (say 4096-d)

So we need a translator.

## The LLaVA-style recipe: frozen experts + a small bridge

A very common architecture (popularized by systems like **LLaVA**) is:

1. **Freeze** a pretrained vision encoder.
2. **Freeze** a pretrained LLM.
3. Train a small **projection layer** (often called a **projector**) that maps vision features into the LLM’s embedding dimension.
4. Then do **visual instruction tuning**: fine-tune (some of) the system on instruction-following data that includes images.

The key idea is pragmatic: don’t relearn vision or language from scratch—just learn how to *connect* them, then teach the connected system to follow multimodal instructions.

![Projector aligns image features with text embeddings for the language decoder. (HuggingFace Blog)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huggingface-co-blog-vlms_005.jpg)

### What the LLM actually “sees”

After projection, the image becomes a sequence of embeddings that can be inserted into the LLM’s context, like:

```
<User>
<image_tokens>
What is the main object on the table?
<Assistant>
...
```

No special magic is required inside the transformer: self-attention can now mix text tokens and projected image tokens in one sequence.

## Visual instruction tuning: teaching the *behavior*

Once the model can ingest images, it still might behave like a captioner (“A table with stuff…”) rather than an assistant.

**Visual instruction tuning** fixes that by training on examples where the *desired output is instruction-following*: answering questions, explaining, comparing, extracting structured text, refusing unsafe requests, etc. This is the multimodal analogue of instruction-tuning for chat LLMs.

Conceptually:
- pretraining gives you *knowledge and representations*
- instruction tuning gives you *helpful interface behavior*

## Practical wrinkles: resolution and token budgets

Images aren’t naturally “one size fits all.” Higher resolution helps with small text and fine details, but it also creates more patches → more **visual tokens** → more compute and context usage.

Many VLMs therefore use strategies like:
- fixed resolution (simple, predictable)
- **dynamic resolution** / adaptive tiling (spend tokens where detail matters)

This is one reason document understanding (tables, receipts, screenshots) can be harder than “natural images”: you often need more pixels *and* more tokens.

## Where GPT-4V fits in

Systems like **GPT-4V** demonstrate the end goal: a single assistant that can reason over images as naturally as it reasons over text—often producing text as an intermediate representation (e.g., turning a table image into LaTeX).

![GPT-4V use cases illustrating multimodal reasoning and interaction. (Huyen Chip)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_002.png)

## Summary

Visual instruction tuning is the step that turns “an LLM that can technically ingest image embeddings” into “a helpful assistant that follows multimodal instructions.” Architectures like LLaVA make this efficient by freezing strong pretrained components and learning a small projector plus instruction-following behavior on image-text data.

## Recommended Reading
- [Multimodality and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)](https://huyenchip.com/2023/10/10/multimodal.html) — Big-picture map of multimodal systems and active research directions.
- [Vision Language Models Explained](https://huggingface.co/blog/vlms) — Clear overview of VLM building blocks and model landscape.
- [Processing images with language models (VLM survey)](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2022-06-09-vlm/) — Architectural patterns for fusing vision into pretrained language models.
- [Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.14198) — A key milestone in prompting-based multimodal few-shot learning.
lessons.summary: Visual instruction tuning connects a pretrained vision encoder to a pretrained LLM (often via a small projector) and then trains the combined system to follow image-grounded instructions. This turns raw visual token ingestion into assistant-like behavior for tasks like VQA, document understanding, and open-ended image chat.
lessons.video_title: State of GPT" (covers fine-tuning landscape including LoRA/PEFT in context)

image_metadata.caption: VLMs handle diverse tasks from captioning to document understanding. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a VLM lesson to motivate why these models matter and what problems they solve before introducing technical details
image_metadata.description: A visual overview of VLM capabilities, likely showing icons or examples of tasks such as visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and web page analysis

image_metadata.caption: Projector aligns image features with text embeddings for the language decoder. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into how image and text representations are actually merged, especially when explaining the projector training stage
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram labeled 'Structure of a Typical Vision Language Model' showing how image features from the encoder are projected into the text embedding space via a multimodal projector and combined with text tokens before being fed to the language model decoder

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V use cases illustrating multimodal reasoning and interaction. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on multimodal AI to build intuition and motivation before introducing technical content.
image_metadata.description: A showcase of GPT-4V use cases demonstrating multimodal capabilities, likely showing examples where the model processes images alongside text to answer questions, describe scenes, or perform reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP vs. Flamingo: contrasting architectures, objectives, and capabilities in vision-language AI.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation exercise after covering both CLIP and Flamingo, or as a review tool before moving to newer LMM architectures.
image_metadata.description: Comparison table or diagram contrasting CLIP and Flamingo across key dimensions: task type (classification/retrieval vs. text generation), architecture, training data, and capabilities, serving as a summary of both models.

image_metadata.caption: Adapter architectures for LMMs reduce trainable parameters while preserving pretrained knowledge. (Source: Chunyuan Li, CVPR 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the landscape of efficient multimodal training methods, after introducing BLIP-2 as a motivating example of parameter efficiency gains
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing multiple adapter-based architectures for large multimodal models, showing how different works position adapter layers between frozen vision encoders and language models to reduce trainable parameters

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V converts a data table image into LaTeX code, a text-intermediate multimodal output.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use alongside the CM3 diagram when explaining text-based intermediate outputs; this example grounds the concept with a familiar, practical tool (GPT-4V)
image_metadata.description: Screenshot or example output showing GPT-4V generating LaTeX code from an input image of a data table, demonstrating how a vision-language model can produce structured text markup that reconstructs the original visual content

image_metadata.caption: FP8 bit layout: E4M3 vs E5M2 formats showing sign, exponent, and mantissa. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing quantization formats, specifically when explaining why different FP8 variants (E4M3 vs E5M2) exist and how bit allocation determines representable range (-448 to 448 for E4M3) versus precision.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the FP8 floating point format, showing the bit-level breakdown of sign, exponent, and mantissa fields for both E4M3 and E5M2 variants, with labeled bit positions and their roles in representing floating point numbers.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA adapters enable fine-tuning of frozen 4-bit quantized base models. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how 4-bit or 8-bit quantized models can still be fine-tuned, particularly when discussing RLHF use cases where multiple adapters (reward model, value policy) are trained on a single quantized base model.
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF illustrating the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) mechanism, showing how small trainable adapter matrices are added on top of a frozen base model, with the animation likely depicting the forward pass through frozen weights and the parallel low-rank update path.

image_metadata.caption: PCA illustrates low-rank approximation: the core idea behind LoRA. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this before explaining LoRA math — use it to build intuition that high-dimensional data/matrices often have a lower-dimensional structure that captures most information.
image_metadata.description: A PCA/SVD visualization showing how a high-dimensional dataset can be approximated by a lower-dimensional representation, illustrating the conceptual foundation of low-rank decomposition with axes and projected data points.

image_metadata.caption: Standard finetuning updates full weight matrix W with ΔW. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing LoRA decomposition — students need to understand the standard update rule to appreciate why decomposing ΔW saves parameters.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of standard full finetuning showing a weight matrix W being updated to W' = W + ΔW, with input x and output h, illustrating how the full weight update matrix is applied during conventional finetuning.

image_metadata.caption: Separating ΔW from W enables the LoRA low-rank substitution. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the standard finetuning diagram, as a bridge to LoRA — emphasize that this separation is what allows ΔW to be replaced by a low-rank product.
image_metadata.description: Alternative formulation diagram showing h = Wx + ΔWx, where the original frozen weight W and the update ΔW are kept separate and both applied to input x, with outputs summed.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA decomposes ΔW into two small matrices W_A and W_B. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: This is the central diagram — show it when formally introducing LoRA after the motivation diagrams, and return to it when discussing parameter counts and storage savings.
image_metadata.description: Core LoRA architecture diagram showing the original frozen weight matrix W alongside two small trainable matrices W_A (A×r) and W_B (r×B) whose product approximates ΔW, with rank r much smaller than A or B.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA matches or beats full finetuning on NLP benchmarks. (LoRA paper, via Raschka 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LoRA mechanism, when students ask 'but does it actually work?' — use it to discuss trade-offs between efficiency and performance.
image_metadata.description: Annotated table from the original LoRA paper comparing ROUGE scores of LoRA against full finetuning, adapter methods, prompt tuning, and prefix tuning across multiple NLP benchmarks, showing LoRA often matches or exceeds full finetuning.

image_metadata.caption: Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format used for LoRA-LLaMA finetuning. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the computational benchmarks section, before discussing training results — students need to understand the data format to contextualize the experiments.
image_metadata.description: Example of the Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format, showing the structure of a training example with instruction, input, and output fields used for instruction finetuning of LLaMA.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Instruct 4-step pipeline generates instruction data automatically. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students ask where the Alpaca training data comes from, or when discussing data efficiency and the cost of creating instruction-following datasets.
image_metadata.description: Four-step Self-Instruct pipeline diagram showing how instruction-following datasets are automatically generated: (1) seed tasks, (2) instruction generation via LLM, (3) instance generation, (4) filtering and post-processing.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, especially to contrast single-head vs multi-head attention and explain the concatenation + projection step.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson or when transitioning from individual components to the full model, as a reference map students can return to.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the positional encoding formula to give students an intuition for what the encoding looks like and why different positions produce distinguishable patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions, with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white).

image_metadata.caption: RoPE encodes position as rotation in 2D subspaces of the embedding. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching relative positional encodings, especially after sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs rotational approaches and explain RoPE's advantages for long-context tasks.
image_metadata.description: Visual illustration of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position index.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer-XL caches prior segment states to extend attention span. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and how Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism solves it.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training phases for vanilla Transformer (left) and Transformer-XL (right) with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Transformer-XL to demonstrate how compressive memory further extends context, and when discussing tradeoffs between memory size and temporal range.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory types: regular memory (recent past activations) and compressed memory (older activations compressed by a compression function), with attention attending to both.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing whether compression loses important information, as empirical evidence that the model attends to compressed memory and it is not wasted.
image_metadata.description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right) in the Compressive Transformer, demonstrating that compressed memory receives meaningful attention despite being older.

image_metadata.caption: SPALM combines recurrent short-term and k-NN long-term memory via gating. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, especially to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and explain how retrieval-augmented memory works.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM architecture showing how short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL-style cache) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce the final output.

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches scratch-trained Memorizing Transformer performance. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing practical deployment of memory-augmented Transformers, particularly to motivate fine-tuning as a cheaper alternative to full retraining.
image_metadata.description: Line chart comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds a negative linear distance bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, especially to contrast with RoPE and learned positional encodings, and when discussing context length extrapolation strategies.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) showing how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.

image_metadata.caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
image_metadata.description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear attention complexity. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Linformer after explaining the quadratic cost of standard attention; use the right panel to motivate why linear approximations matter at scale (e.g., L > 1024).
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders attention computation via kernel features, avoiding the quadratic attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RFA kernel approximation formula φ(x)·φ(y) ≈ exp(x·y); use it to explain why reordering (K^T V first) is valid and how it achieves linear complexity.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computes the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reorders operations using kernel approximation φ(·), computing key-value products first to avoid the quadratic matrix, enabling linear-time attention.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer's FAVOR+. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ after explaining the general random feature approach; use to justify why orthogonal and positive features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error for Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing lower variance with orthogonal sampling; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower approximation error for softmax kernel estimation.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize Transformer training in RL. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing GTrXL as a solution to Transformer training instability in RL; use to highlight the specific architectural differences (gate placement, norm reordering) before discussing empirical RL results.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm before residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using the reordered layer norm, enabling stable training for RL.

image_metadata.caption: Vision Arena ranks VLMs via anonymous human preference voting. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to select or compare VLMs, especially to contrast human-preference evaluation against automated benchmark metrics
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Vision Arena leaderboard interface, showing a side-by-side anonymous model comparison UI where users submit an image and prompt and vote on preferred outputs

image_metadata.caption: Open VLM Leaderboard ranks models across benchmarks with size and license filters. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching model selection criteria, especially when students need to choose a VLM for a project with specific size or licensing constraints
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Open VLM Leaderboard showing a ranked table of vision language models with scores across multiple benchmark metrics, with filtering options for model size and license type

image_metadata.caption: VLM architecture: image encoder, projector, and text decoder stacked sequentially. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing VLM architecture, especially before or alongside explaining LLaVA's training procedure to ground the text description in a visual schema
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a typical vision language model showing the sequential pipeline: image encoder → multimodal embedding projector → text decoder, with annotations indicating which components are frozen vs trained during pretraining

image_metadata.caption: BLIP-2 architecture: frozen image encoder, Q-Former bridge, and frozen LLM. (Li et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing BLIP-2 to explain the modular three-stage architecture before discussing training objectives or benchmarks; also use it when contrasting BLIP-2's efficiency against larger models like Flamingo.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of BLIP-2 showing the three-component pipeline: a frozen pre-trained image encoder on the left, a lightweight 12-layer Querying Transformer (Q-Former) in the middle acting as a bridge, and a frozen large language model (LLM) on the right, with arrows indicating the flow of visual information through the system.

image_metadata.caption: Multimodal AI in healthcare: combining images, text, and signals. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for multimodal AI — specifically when arguing that some use cases are impossible with a single modality.
image_metadata.description: A visual showing multimodal AI applications in healthcare, likely depicting how different data types (medical images, text reports, audio) are combined for clinical tasks such as diagnosis, radiology, or patient monitoring.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V performing OCR and document processing on an image. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining classification as a multimodal task type, particularly to contrast fixed-class outputs with more flexible generative approaches.
image_metadata.description: An example of GPT-4V performing document processing or OCR, showing the model reading and interpreting text within an image, such as a scanned document or form.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CLIP's capabilities and the concept of zero-shot classification, before explaining the architecture that makes it possible.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating CLIP's zero-shot image classification capability, showing how text prompts (e.g., 'a photo of a dog') are matched against image embeddings to classify images without task-specific training.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP's dual-encoder architecture maps images and text to a shared space. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining CLIP's architecture in detail, after motivating why a shared embedding space is useful.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of OpenAI's CLIP model, showing the dual-encoder structure: an image encoder (ResNet or ViT) and a text encoder (Transformer), both projecting inputs into a shared embedding space.

image_metadata.caption: Classifier vs. language model objectives: fixed classes vs. flexible text generation. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP moved away from both classifier and language model objectives toward contrastive learning.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting a classifier objective (predicting one of N fixed classes) versus a language model objective (generating free-form text), illustrating their respective output spaces and limitations.

image_metadata.caption: Multiple valid captions for one image motivate contrastive over generative objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately before introducing contrastive learning, to build the intuition for why predicting exact captions is problematic.
image_metadata.description: An illustration showing multiple different human-written captions for the same image (from Flickr30K), demonstrating that a single image can be validly described in many ways.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP's contrastive training: maximize diagonal similarity in an N×N pair matrix. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the central visual when explaining CLIP's contrastive objective — use it to walk through the N×N matrix and the dual classification framing.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how CLIP's contrastive learning works: for a batch of N (image, text) pairs, an N×N similarity matrix is computed, and the model is trained to maximize similarity along the diagonal (correct pairs) and minimize it off-diagonal.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP training pseudocode: symmetric contrastive loss over image-text embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the similarity matrix diagram (image 8) for students ready to go deeper — ideal for those with a programming background or studying model implementation.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for CLIP's contrastive training loop, showing how image and text embeddings are computed, normalized, the similarity matrix is formed, and the symmetric cross-entropy loss is calculated.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive learning gives CLIP a 12x efficiency gain over language model objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the contrastive objective to reinforce its practical advantage with data — useful when students ask 'why does this matter?'
image_metadata.description: A chart or graph showing the training efficiency improvement of CLIP's contrastive objective compared to the language model objective baseline, illustrating the reported 12x efficiency gain.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP achieves strong zero-shot classification via contrastive learning efficiency. (Radford et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP uses contrastive loss instead of a language model objective, and when discussing zero-shot transfer performance benchmarks.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or performance comparison showing CLIP's image classification accuracy as a strong zero-shot baseline, likely compared to supervised models across multiple datasets, illustrating the 12x efficiency improvement from contrastive vs. language model objectives.

image_metadata.caption: unCLIP uses CLIP embeddings to condition image generation via a diffusion decoder. (Ramesh et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from CLIP as a classification/retrieval tool to its role enabling generative models, especially when explaining DALL-E 2's architecture.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2), showing how CLIP's joint image-text embedding space is used in a generative pipeline: a text encoder produces embeddings, a prior maps text to image embeddings, and a diffusion decoder generates images.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo engages in multi-turn visual conversations using few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Flamingo section to motivate why the architecture is needed, before diving into technical components.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot or illustration of multi-turn conversations with Flamingo, showing the model responding to image-based questions and prompts, demonstrating its few-shot visual question answering and dialogue capabilities.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo combines a frozen vision encoder with a language model via cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing Flamingo's architecture, as the anchor diagram for all subsequent component-level explanations.
image_metadata.description: High-level architecture diagram of Flamingo showing its two main components: a frozen vision encoder (CLIP-like) that produces visual features, and a large language model (Chinchilla) augmented with cross-attention layers to condition text generation on visual inputs.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo trains on four datasets totaling 2.1B+ image-text pairs with tuned weights. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's training data strategy, particularly to contrast dataset scale with CLIP and to explain the role of dataset weights in the loss function.
image_metadata.description: Table or diagram summarizing Flamingo's 4 training datasets: ALIGN and LTIP (image-text pairs), VTP (video-text pairs), and an interleaved image-text dataset (LTIP/M3W), including dataset sizes and training weights.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo predicts next text tokens conditioned on both text and visual inputs. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo extends a standard language model to handle visual inputs, before introducing Perceiver Resampler and GATED XATTN-DENSE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how Flamingo's language model (Chinchilla) generates text tokens conditioned on both preceding text tokens and visual tokens, showing the modified autoregressive text generation process with visual conditioning.

image_metadata.caption: Perceiver Resampler compresses variable visual features into 64 fixed visual tokens. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo handles variable numbers of image/video features, specifically to illustrate the role of Perceiver Resampler before discussing GATED XATTN-DENSE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of Flamingo's Perceiver Resampler module, showing how variable-length visual features from the vision encoder (images or video frames) are compressed into a fixed set of 64 visual output tokens via cross-attention with learned latent queries.

image_metadata.caption: GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inject visual context into frozen LM layers via gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Perceiver Resampler to complete the picture of how visual tokens are integrated into text generation, emphasizing the ablation result showing its necessity.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of Flamingo's GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inserted between frozen Chinchilla LM layers, showing how cross-attention allows LM layers to attend to visual tokens, with a gating mechanism controlling the contribution of visual information.

image_metadata.caption: ImageBind aligns six modalities into one shared embedding space using images as anchor. (Girdhar et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing future directions in multimodal AI, specifically to illustrate how the field is moving toward unified representations across more than two modalities.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of ImageBind showing a unified embedding space where six modalities (images, text, audio, depth, thermal, IMU) are all aligned together using image as the binding modality, enabling cross-modal retrieval and reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: LaVIN uses lightweight adapters to connect frozen vision and language encoders.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing efficient multimodal training strategies, specifically to contrast with Flamingo's heavier train-from-scratch approach
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LaVIN, showing how a frozen pretrained language model and vision encoder are connected via lightweight adapter modules to enable instruction-following with minimal training from scratch

image_metadata.caption: Further adapter designs for LMMs from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 2 as a continuation of the adapter landscape overview, before transitioning to multimodal output generation
image_metadata.description: Second diagram from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial showing additional adapter-based LMM architectures, likely detailing specific design choices such as where adapters are inserted and how modalities are aligned

image_metadata.caption: CM3 generates HTML markup as intermediate output, enabling text and image webpage synthesis.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the two strategies for multimodal output (text intermediate vs. multimodal tokens), use this to illustrate the text-based intermediate approach
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating CM3's approach to multimodal output generation, where the model produces HTML markup as an intermediate text representation that encodes both text content and image references, which are then compiled into full webpages

image_metadata.caption: LMMs generate interleaved text and images using tagged multimodal tokens. (Koh et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the text-intermediate approach (CM3, GPT-4V), introduce this to contrast with the multimodal token strategy, as described by Caiming Xiong
image_metadata.description: Diagram from 'Generating Images with Multimodal Language Models' (Koh et al., 2023) showing how an LMM interleaves text token generation and image generation/retrieval in a single output stream using tagged multimodal tokens

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'jointly training with image and text' approach; use to explain how visual regions are embedded analogously to word tokens before entering the transformer.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text token embeddings and visual region embeddings (each composed of tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss which pre-training strategies matter and how researchers validate architectural decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, with/without pre-training).

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM uses image patches as prefix tokens with bidirectional encoder and causal decoder. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting SimVLM with VisualBERT; use to explain how the prefix LM paradigm differs from full bidirectional models and why it suits image-conditioned text generation.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a ResNet convolutional stage into prefix tokens fed into a bidirectional encoder, followed by a causal (autoregressive) text decoder that generates output text.

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to discuss why mixing data modalities and choosing the right pre-training objective are critical design decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants: different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM), with/without image-text data, and with/without text-only data.

image_metadata.caption: CM3 tokenizes images and HTML text into a unified causal masked sequence model. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing CM3 as an alternative multimodal approach; use to contrast discrete image tokenization (VQVAE-GAN) with patch-based methods and explain how web-scale HTML data enables multimodal learning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the CM3 causally-masked multimodal modeling approach, showing how HTML web pages with embedded images are tokenized (images via VQVAE-GAN into discrete tokens), masked, and processed by a causal language model to reconstruct masked spans.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM stays frozen during training and inference. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

image_metadata.caption: Frozen outperforms fine-tuned LM on VQA, showing value of preserving pre-trained knowledge. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix tokens via a small trainable network. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to highlight how CLIP's rich visual representations reduce the burden on the mapping network and enable training with very few parameters.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing: CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings → lightweight mapping network F (transformer or MLP) → k prefix embedding vectors → frozen GPT-2 language model generating captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words, showing visual-linguistic alignment. (Mokady et al. 2021)

image_metadata.description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', 'outdoor').

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals per layer. (Chen et al. 2021)

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing why pre-trained LM adaptation is preferable to training from scratch, especially in low-data regimes
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning performance metrics

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)

image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a self-ensemble module combining language decoder logits and vision-language fused logits, with GPT2 as the language decoder

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT self-ensemble module significantly boosts captioning metrics on MS COCO. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the importance of the self-ensemble module in VC-GPT and how to interpret standard image captioning evaluation metrics
image_metadata.description: Performance table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics

image_metadata.caption: MERLOT uses contrastive and temporal reordering objectives for video-language pretraining. (Zellers et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing video-based vision-language models and explaining how temporal structure in videos provides additional training signal beyond static images
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram of MERLOT training: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and temporal frame reordering task on YouTube video segments

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo connects CLIP and a frozen LM via Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Flamingo as a case study in efficiently connecting pretrained vision and language models, especially before discussing the gated cross-attention mechanism in detail
image_metadata.description: High-level architecture overview of Flamingo showing a pretrained vision encoder (CLIP), a Perceiver Resampler producing fixed visual tokens, and gated cross-attention layers interleaved with frozen LM layers, accepting interleaved image-text input

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer preserves pretrained LM behavior at initialization. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific mechanism Flamingo uses to safely inject visual information into a frozen LM, particularly the role of the learned gate initialized to zero
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how visual tokens attend to language hidden states with a learned tanh gate that initializes to zero to preserve pretrained LM behavior

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo performance improves with both model scale and number of few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how Flamingo leverages in-context learning at test time and why model scale matters for few-shot multimodal performance
image_metadata.description: Line or bar charts showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (e.g., 3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot, 4-shot, 8-shot, 32-shot) across vision-language benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing few-shot prompting versus fine-tuning paradigms, and when discussing the breadth of tasks a single vision-language model can handle
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) of different sizes against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning

image_metadata.caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives with split text decoders. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa and explaining how combining contrastive and captioning losses in one model captures both discriminative and generative vision-language representations
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of CoCa (Contrastive Captioner) showing a shared image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch

image_metadata.caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to recognition and understanding tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how CoCa handles diverse downstream tasks and the role of attention poolers as lightweight task adapters
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained model is adapted to downstream tasks (visual recognition, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries

image_metadata.caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss combination (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is implemented in practice.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including the dual-loss setup with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.

image_metadata.caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC decoding mechanism to contextualize its practical limitations — specifically when discussing the trade-off between no-training convenience and performance ceiling.
image_metadata.description: A results table comparing MAGiC image captioning performance against other methods on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised approaches and standard metrics (BLEU, CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: PICa n-shot VQA: image captions + few-shot examples prompt GPT-3. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to explain how vision tasks can be solved by converting images to text and leveraging large language models without fine-tuning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how PICa performs n-shot Visual Question Answering at inference time: an image is converted to a caption/tags, then combined with n few-shot example (image-caption, question, answer) triplets as a prompt fed into GPT-3.

image_metadata.caption: PICa-Full vs. PICa-Base on OK-VQA: impact of example selection and ensembling. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the two PICa improvements to give students concrete evidence of how each design choice contributes to the +8.6 point SoTA improvement on OK-VQA.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing PICa-Base (random in-context examples) vs. PICa-Full (similar example selection + multi-query ensembling) against other methods on the OK-VQA knowledge-based VQA benchmark.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models: language as interface composing VLM, LM, and ALM. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students the architectural overview before diving into specific task examples.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation, with arrows depicting the multimodal prompting chain.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models captioning: VLM fills prompt slots, LM generates caption. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific image captioning application of Socratic Models to illustrate how VLM-to-LM chaining works step by step.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step illustration of the Socratic Models pipeline for image captioning: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, these fill a structured language prompt, and a causal LM generates the final caption.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models vs. ClipCap on COCO captioning: training-free performance gap. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the discussion with benchmark results and highlight the cost-performance trade-off of training-free approaches.
image_metadata.description: Table comparing image captioning performance (CIDEr, SPICE, CLIP-S metrics) of Socratic Models against ClipCap and other baselines on 100 random COCO test examples, with training-free vs. trained model distinctions.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models for egocentric video: chaining ALM, VLM, and LM via language. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when demonstrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to illustrate how audio, video, and language models can be chained for complex activity understanding tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Socratic Models pipeline for egocentric video understanding: audio is processed by ALM (speech-to-text), video frames by VLM (image-to-text), outputs are combined into a structured prompt, and an LM generates activity summaries and answers questions.

lessons.title: Cross-Attention & Fusion Architectures
lessons.content:
# Cross-Attention & Fusion Architectures

When you build a multimodal model, you’re really solving a *routing* problem: **when the model is generating text, which parts of the image should influence the next token—and how strongly?**

The workhorse for this is **cross-attention**: one stream (say, text) asks questions, the other stream (say, vision) provides evidence.

## From self-attention to cross-attention
In self-attention, tokens attend to *other tokens in the same sequence*. In **cross-attention**, the roles split:

- **Queries (Q)** come from the *consumer* stream (often the language model’s hidden states).
- **Keys/Values (K,V)** come from the *provider* stream (often image features from a vision encoder).

So each text position can “look up” the most relevant visual features before predicting the next word.

![Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/multi-head-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_005.png)

A subtle but important point: cross-attention is not just concatenating modalities. It’s *selective reading*—different words can consult different image regions.

## The efficiency problem: images are long sequences
A ViT-style encoder might produce hundreds (or thousands) of patch tokens. If every LM layer cross-attends to all of them, compute and memory balloon.

Fusion architectures are mostly different answers to:

> “How do we give the LM enough visual information **without** making it attend to an enormous visual sequence everywhere?”

## Perceiver-style compression: fixed visual tokens
One popular trick is to compress variable-length visual features into a small, fixed set of “visual memory” tokens using learned latents (a Perceiver-like resampler).

![Perceiver Resampler compresses variable visual features into 64 fixed visual tokens. (Adapted from Alayrac et al.)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_017.png)

Now the LM cross-attends to (say) 64 tokens instead of 576 patches—much cheaper, and it standardizes the interface for images *and* videos.

## Gated cross-attention: don’t overwhelm the language model
Even with compression, injecting vision everywhere can destabilize a strong pretrained LM. **Gated cross-attention** adds a learned “volume knob” so the model can decide *how much* visual context to mix in.

Conceptually:

\[
\text{hidden} \leftarrow \text{hidden} + g \cdot \text{CrossAttn}(Q=\text{hidden}, K,V=\text{vision})
\]

Where \(g\) is a learned gate (often per layer, sometimes per token).

![GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inject visual context into frozen LM layers via gated cross-attention. (Adapted from Alayrac et al.)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_018.png)

This is the core idea behind Flamingo-style fusion: keep the LM mostly intact, and *carefully* splice in visual evidence.

## Q-Former (BLIP-2): a small “translator” between vision and language
BLIP-2 takes a different angle: freeze a strong image encoder and a strong LLM, then train a lightweight module in between called a **Q-Former**.

Think of Q-Former as a set of learned **query tokens** that repeatedly cross-attend to the image and distill it into a compact set of embeddings that the LLM can consume. The LLM doesn’t need to understand raw patch tokens; it receives a small, curated “visual summary” produced by the Q-Former.

### Minimal pseudo-code for cross-attention fusion
```python
# text_h: [T, d] language hidden states
# vis:    [V, d] visual tokens (patches or resampled latents)

attn = softmax((text_h @ vis.T) / sqrt(d))   # [T, V]
vis_ctx = attn @ vis                         # [T, d]

# gated injection
text_h = text_h + gate * vis_ctx
```

## A quick comparison of fusion patterns

| Pattern | What attends to what? | Why it’s used | Typical tradeoff |
|---|---|---|---|
| Early fusion (concat tokens) | self-attn over (text+vision) | simple, expressive | expensive; can blur modality roles |
| Cross-attn in LM layers | text queries vision K/V | selective grounding | can be costly if V is large |
| Perceiver resampler + cross-attn | text queries small visual latents | fixed compute per image/video | compression may drop fine detail |
| Q-Former (BLIP-2) | learned queries attend to vision; LLM consumes summary | few trainable params; strong reuse of frozen models | summary bottleneck must be well-trained |

*Table synthesized from common VLM designs (adapted from the referenced model docs/posts).* 

## Summary
Cross-attention is the clean mechanism that lets one modality “consult” another at the moment it matters. Modern fusion architectures (Perceiver resampling, gated cross-attention, Q-Former) are largely about controlling *bandwidth*: how much visual information flows into the language model, and how efficiently.

## Recommended Reading
- [BLIP-2 (Transformers docs)](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model_doc/blip-2) — Practical view of Q-Former bridging frozen vision encoders and frozen LLMs.
- [Multimodality and Large Multimodal Models](https://huyenchip.com/2023/10/10/multimodal.html) — Clear architectural overview of Flamingo, Perceiver resampling, and gated cross-attention.
- [Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.14198) — The original design motivations for interleaved vision-text and gated fusion.
- [Vision Language Models Explained](https://huggingface.co/blog/vlms) — A friendly map of the VLM landscape and common building blocks.
lessons.summary: Cross-attention lets text representations selectively read from visual representations, enabling grounded generation without forcing full early fusion. Architectures like Perceiver resampling, gated cross-attention, and BLIP-2’s Q-Former mainly differ in how they control the bandwidth and stability of vision-to-language information flow.
