image_metadata.description: High-level architecture overview of Flamingo showing a pretrained vision encoder (CLIP), a Perceiver Resampler producing fixed visual tokens, and gated cross-attention layers interleaved with frozen LM layers, accepting interleaved image-text input

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer preserves pretrained LM behavior at initialization. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific mechanism Flamingo uses to safely inject visual information into a frozen LM, particularly the role of the learned gate initialized to zero
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how visual tokens attend to language hidden states with a learned tanh gate that initializes to zero to preserve pretrained LM behavior

image_metadata.caption: CAM vs. GETAM: transformers capture full object shapes, not just discriminative regions.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on weakly-supervised segmentation to motivate why transformer-based attention maps are preferable to CNN CAMs.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of CNN-based CAM activation maps versus GETAM (transformer-based) activation maps on multiple object images, showing that CAM highlights only discriminative regions while GETAM captures full object shapes.

image_metadata.caption: Naive CAM transplantation to ViT fails; a new approach is required.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why a new method is needed for ViT-based segmentation, after introducing the CAM concept and before presenting GETAM.
image_metadata.description: Comparison of three CAM variants applied to ViT: CAM (add) which adds the [class] token to patch tokens, CAM (ignore) which ignores the [class] token, and CNN baseline CAM (ResNet38), showing that naive transplantation of CNN CAM to ViT produces poor results.

image_metadata.caption: Class-agnostic attention (left) vs. class-wise gradient maps (right) in GETAM.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the two components of GETAM (attention map and gradient map) to illustrate what each component contributes before explaining their combination.
image_metadata.description: Grid of visualizations showing class-agnostic attention maps (leftmost column) and class-wise gradient maps (rightmost column) for multiple images, demonstrating that gradient maps retain clear object-specific shapes while attention maps show broader candidate regions.

image_metadata.caption: GETAM combines class-agnostic attention and gradient maps inside each transformer block.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the GETAM formulation in detail, as the primary architectural reference diagram for the method.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of GETAM generation within a single transformer block, showing how the class-agnostic attention map (A_CLS^i) and the class-wise gradient map (∇A_CLS) are computed and combined via element-wise multiplication to produce the GETAM output.

image_metadata.caption: Successive transformer blocks focus on different object regions, enabling better coverage.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the successive attention aggregation component of GETAM, to justify why multiple transformer blocks are combined rather than using a single layer.
image_metadata.description: Grid of attention maps from successive transformer blocks for two sample images (one with multiple people), showing how different layers focus on different object regions (foreground vs. background people) without low-level noise.

image_metadata.caption: Element-wise multiplication concentrates attention; matrix multiplication introduces noise.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during the ablation study discussion or when justifying the element-wise multiplication design choice in GETAM.
image_metadata.description: Distribution plots comparing class-wise attention maps produced by element-wise multiplication versus matrix multiplication (dot product) fusion methods, showing that element-wise multiplication concentrates the distribution while matrix multiplication introduces noise.

image_metadata.caption: Label completion: saliency masking for salient objects, activation mining for non-salient ones.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the label refinement pipeline after GETAM, to illustrate how pseudo labels are improved for both salient and non-salient objects.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel diagram illustrating the activation-aware label completion module: (a) all objects located via activation maps, (b) saliency constraint providing accurate shapes for salient objects but suppressing non-salient ones, (c) high activation mining recovering suppressed background objects.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-step WSSS (a) vs. proposed single-stage transformer framework (b).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the overall framework to contrast it with prior multi-stage methods and motivate the single-stage design.
image_metadata.description: Conceptual comparison diagram showing (a) multi-step WSSS methods requiring multiple stages and networks versus (b) the proposed single-stage transformer approach trainable end-to-end in one pass.

image_metadata.caption: GETAM (d) captures better object shapes than CNN CAM (b) or ViT Grad-CAM (c).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during the experimental results section to support quantitative claims with qualitative visual evidence of GETAM's superiority.
image_metadata.description: Qualitative comparison grid showing activation maps from four methods on the same images: (a) original image, (b) CNN-based CAM, (c) ViT-based Grad-CAM, (d) GETAM, demonstrating GETAM's superior object shape capture and noise suppression.

image_metadata.caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus (yellow) with low-res periphery (red).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing attention mechanisms, before any math, to ground the abstract concept in human perceptual experience.
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on the nose, other ear, and eyes — illustrating selective visual attention at varying resolutions.

image_metadata.caption: Words attend to other words with varying weights in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention (Shiba Inu) example, when transitioning from image-based intuition to language-based attention motivation.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weights visualized as colored arcs or highlights showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: source sentence compressed into one fixed vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, just before introducing the attention mechanism as a solution.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing an encoder RNN processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which a decoder RNN then uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: decoder dynamically attends to all encoder states.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when formally introducing the Bahdanau (2015) attention mechanism, after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism in an encoder-decoder architecture, showing alignment scores computed between decoder hidden states and all encoder hidden states, with weighted context vectors fed into the decoder at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Alignment matrix: French-English attention weights reveal word correspondences. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining alignment scores mathematically, to demonstrate that the model learns linguistically meaningful alignments without explicit supervision.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates the attention weight between each source-target word pair, revealing mostly diagonal alignment with some cross-language reordering.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior words with varying strength. (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention, to illustrate how a model relates different positions within the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red, and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing which prior words the model attends to.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention highlights relevant image regions per caption word. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs hard attention, or when explaining attention applied to non-sequential (spatial) data like images.
image_metadata.description: An image of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park with overlaid attention weight regions highlighted, showing which parts of the image the model attends to when generating each word of the caption 'A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park.'

image_metadata.caption: Global vs local attention: full-sequence vs windowed source alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. (2015) attention variants, after covering Bahdanau attention, to illustrate the spectrum from soft to hard attention.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in the Luong et al. architecture.

image_metadata.caption: NTM architecture: controller uses attention to read/write external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Neural Turing Machines as an application of attention to memory access, after students understand basic attention in seq2seq models.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine showing a controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing and how the controller produces a distribution over allowable integer shifts; use to prompt discussion of why different parameterizations matter.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing flow: content lookup, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After introducing each individual NTM addressing component; use as a synthesis diagram to show how all steps connect into a unified addressing pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions as output. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems with variable output spaces (e.g., sorting, TSP); contrast with standard attention to highlight the key architectural difference.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer architecture; introduce single-head attention first, then use this diagram to show how multi-head attention generalizes it.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, scaling, optional masking, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, concatenation, and linear projection.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder in detail; use to walk through each component layer by layer before discussing the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing a multi-head self-attention sublayer and a position-wise feed-forward sublayer, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL interleaves causal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer regarding positional sensitivity and how SNAIL addresses them; useful for comparing attention-only vs. hybrid sequential models.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and causal attention layers (for selective long-range access), used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution has local receptive field; self-attention accesses all positions globally.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SAGAN or self-attention in vision; use to motivate why convolution alone is insufficient for modeling global image structure.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the limited local receptive field of a convolutional operation (small highlighted region around a pixel) versus the global receptive field of self-attention (connections from one pixel to all other positions in the image).

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: image features as query, key, value via 1×1 convolutions. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), the attention map computation via softmax, and the final weighted output combined with the original feature map via a learned scale parameter.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — use it to ask 'which parts of the image do you think the attention map focuses on for a dog's eye?'
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer lesson, immediately after introducing the model as a black box, to motivate the internal structure
image_metadata.description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, styled as an 'opened' black box

image_metadata.caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translation out. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very beginning of a Transformer introduction to ground the architecture in a real-world task before opening the black box
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with an input sentence in one language entering and a translated sentence exiting

image_metadata.caption: Six stacked encoders feed into six stacked decoders in the Transformer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the black-box view, when explaining that the Transformer is composed of repeated encoder and decoder layers
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a stack of six encoders on the left and a stack of six decoders on the right, with arrows indicating data flow and connections between the two stacks

image_metadata.caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when drilling into a single encoder block after establishing the stack structure, to explain what each encoder actually computes
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with input flowing up through both

image_metadata.caption: Input words converted to 512-dimensional embedding vectors before encoding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from architecture overview to data flow, specifically when explaining how raw words become numerical inputs to the Transformer
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing input words being converted into embedding vectors (represented as colored columns of size 512) before entering the bottom encoder, with each word mapped to a fixed-size vector

image_metadata.caption: Each word vector flows its own path through encoder layers in parallel. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the encoder processes a full sequence, emphasizing parallelism as a key advantage over RNNs
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors flowing in parallel through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers of an encoder, with each word following its own path

image_metadata.caption: Concrete sentence tokens flowing through encoder self-attention and feed-forward layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when walking through a worked example of encoding, after introducing the encoder structure, to connect theory to a concrete sentence
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the encoder processing a short example sentence, showing how each token's vector representation passes through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers with concrete word labels

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', visualizing learned word relationships. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention conceptually, before the mathematical details, to motivate why attention is useful for capturing word relationships
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization heatmap showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending strongly to 'animal', with colored lines or shading indicating attention weights between the query word and all other words

image_metadata.caption: Query, Key, Value vectors derived from embeddings via learned weight matrices. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the first step of self-attention computation, after motivating why attention is useful, to ground the Q/K/V formalism in concrete matrix operations
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors are derived from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, with vector dimensions labeled (64 vs 512)

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.

image_metadata.caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.

image_metadata.caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.

image_metadata.caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
image_metadata.description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.

image_metadata.caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same input being processed through eight parallel sets of Q/K/V weight matrices, each producing a separate Z matrix (Z0 through Z7).

image_metadata.caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head QKV diagram to pose the 'how do we combine these?' problem before revealing the W^O projection solution.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the eight Z matrices problem, as the resolution step, and emphasize that W^O is also a learned parameter trained end-to-end.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the concatenation of all eight Z matrices followed by multiplication by weight matrix W^O to produce a single combined output matrix, completing the multi-headed attention mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Complete multi-headed self-attention pipeline from input to output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation diagram after explaining each individual step, or use as a reference when students need to review the complete multi-head attention mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-headed self-attention pipeline in one view: input embeddings → multiple Q/K/V projections → parallel attention computations → concatenation → W^O projection → final output.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads reveal which words 'it' attends to during encoding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-headed attention mechanics to demonstrate real-world utility — use it to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial by showing they capture different linguistic phenomena.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing which tokens different attention heads focus on when encoding the word 'it' in the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to other words.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads for 'it' — multi-head attention visualized. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual attention heads; use to motivate why we need multiple heads and to discuss the interpretability trade-off of richer representations
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, creating a complex, hard-to-interpret web of attention weights

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use the 4D example to build intuition before showing the full 512-dimensional version
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder, with a small 4-dimensional example illustrating the sinusoidal pattern of positional encoding values

image_metadata.caption: Actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding; use as a stepping stone before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap
image_metadata.description: Small color-coded grid showing actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding across several word positions, with values ranging from -1 to 1 displayed as colored cells

image_metadata.caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap across word positions. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss the property of generalizing to longer sequences than seen during training
image_metadata.description: Large color-coded heatmap grid where each row represents the positional encoding vector for one word position (up to ~20 positions) and each column represents one of 512 dimensions, with colors ranging from dark to light showing sinusoidal patterns across dimensions

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding from 'Attention Is All You Need' paper (interleaved). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the difference between the paper's positional encoding method and the Tensor2Tensor implementation; use to teach students to read primary sources alongside blog explanations
concepts: Attention Is All You Need paper
image_metadata.description: Reproduction of the positional encoding visualization from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sinusoidal pattern across positions and dimensions as a color-coded heatmap

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around self-attention sub-layer. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing residual connections and layer norm in the encoder; use to show the precise placement of Add & Norm before discussing why these are needed for deep network training
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection around the self-attention sub-layer and the subsequent layer normalization step, with vectors flowing through Add & Norm operations

image_metadata.caption: 2-layer Transformer with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining residual connections in a single layer; use to show how the pattern scales across the full model and to transition from encoder-only to encoder-decoder discussion
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Transformer with 2 stacked encoders and 2 stacked decoders, showing residual connections and layer normalization at each sub-layer in both encoder and decoder stacks

image_metadata.caption: Vector-level view of residual addition and layer norm after self-attention. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students need a deeper understanding of how residual connections work mechanically; use to show the actual vector arithmetic before moving to the decoder
image_metadata.description: Detailed vector-level diagram of the self-attention sub-layer with residual connection, showing input vectors being processed by self-attention, then added back to the original input vectors (residual), and passed through layer normalization

image_metadata.caption: First decoding step: encoder outputs K, V vectors fed to decoder. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the decoder side; use the animation to show the first decoding step and explain how encoder context is passed to the decoder
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer: the encoder processes the full input sequence, and the decoder begins generating the first output token, with attention vectors K and V flowing from the top encoder to each decoder layer

image_metadata.caption: Autoregressive decoding: each output token fed back as next input. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use to show the complete generation process and explain masked self-attention (attending only to previous positions) in the decoder
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, with each generated token being fed back as input to the decoder in the next step, illustrating the autoregressive generation process until an end token is produced

image_metadata.caption: Linear layer produces logits; softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how a Transformer generates a token at inference time, specifically after covering the decoder stack and before discussing training loss.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.

image_metadata.caption: One-hot encoding represents word 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how target words are represented during training, before explaining cross-entropy loss or comparing model output distributions to ground truth.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of one-hot encoding for a vocabulary, showing a vector where a single cell is set to 1 (representing the word 'am') and all other cells are 0, against the backdrop of the full output vocabulary.

image_metadata.caption: Six-word vocabulary: 'thanks' encoded as a one-hot vector. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing one-hot encoding during the training recap, especially when walking through the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
image_metadata.description: Example one-hot encoded vector for a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), illustrating how the word 'thanks' would be represented as a target label when training on the translation of 'merci'.

image_metadata.caption: Model output vs. target label: the gap the loss function minimizes. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function, specifically to illustrate why we need a metric to compare model output distributions to ground-truth labels.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the target one-hot label vector for the word 'thanks', illustrating the discrepancy the loss function must minimize.

image_metadata.caption: Target probability distributions across all decoding steps for 'i am a student'. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step sequence generation during training, after the single-word loss example, to generalize the loss concept to full sentence translation.
image_metadata.description: Grid of probability distributions showing what the model should output at each decoding step for the translation 'i am a student <eos>', with each column representing a time step and each row a vocabulary word, showing ideal peaked distributions at the correct words.

image_metadata.caption: Trained model produces confident peaked distributions at each decoding step. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after displaying the target distributions (image 5) to contrast ideal training targets with actual trained-model outputs, and when introducing greedy vs. beam search decoding strategies.
image_metadata.description: Grid of probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model for the translation 'i am a student <eos>', showing sharply peaked distributions near the correct words at each time step, contrasting with the flat distributions of an untrained model.

image_metadata.caption: VLMs handle diverse tasks from captioning to document understanding. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a VLM lesson to motivate why these models matter and what problems they solve before introducing technical details
image_metadata.description: A visual overview of VLM capabilities, likely showing icons or examples of tasks such as visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and web page analysis

image_metadata.caption: Vision Arena ranks VLMs via anonymous human preference voting. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to select or compare VLMs, especially to contrast human-preference evaluation against automated benchmark metrics
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Vision Arena leaderboard interface, showing a side-by-side anonymous model comparison UI where users submit an image and prompt and vote on preferred outputs

image_metadata.caption: Open VLM Leaderboard ranks models across benchmarks with size and license filters. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching model selection criteria, especially when students need to choose a VLM for a project with specific size or licensing constraints
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Open VLM Leaderboard showing a ranked table of vision language models with scores across multiple benchmark metrics, with filtering options for model size and license type

image_metadata.caption: VLM architecture: image encoder, projector, and text decoder stacked sequentially. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing VLM architecture, especially before or alongside explaining LLaVA's training procedure to ground the text description in a visual schema
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a typical vision language model showing the sequential pipeline: image encoder → multimodal embedding projector → text decoder, with annotations indicating which components are frozen vs trained during pretraining

image_metadata.caption: Projector aligns image features with text embeddings for the language decoder. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into how image and text representations are actually merged, especially when explaining the projector training stage
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram labeled 'Structure of a Typical Vision Language Model' showing how image features from the encoder are projected into the text embedding space via a multimodal projector and combined with text tokens before being fed to the language model decoder

image_metadata.caption: BLIP-2 architecture: frozen image encoder, Q-Former bridge, and frozen LLM. (Li et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing BLIP-2 to explain the modular three-stage architecture before discussing training objectives or benchmarks; also use it when contrasting BLIP-2's efficiency against larger models like Flamingo.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of BLIP-2 showing the three-component pipeline: a frozen pre-trained image encoder on the left, a lightweight 12-layer Querying Transformer (Q-Former) in the middle acting as a bridge, and a frozen large language model (LLM) on the right, with arrows indicating the flow of visual information through the system.

image_metadata.caption: Multimodal AI in healthcare: combining images, text, and signals. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for multimodal AI — specifically when arguing that some use cases are impossible with a single modality.
image_metadata.description: A visual showing multimodal AI applications in healthcare, likely depicting how different data types (medical images, text reports, audio) are combined for clinical tasks such as diagnosis, radiology, or patient monitoring.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V use cases illustrating multimodal reasoning and interaction. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on multimodal AI to build intuition and motivation before introducing technical content.
image_metadata.description: A showcase of GPT-4V use cases demonstrating multimodal capabilities, likely showing examples where the model processes images alongside text to answer questions, describe scenes, or perform reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V performing OCR and document processing on an image. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining classification as a multimodal task type, particularly to contrast fixed-class outputs with more flexible generative approaches.
image_metadata.description: An example of GPT-4V performing document processing or OCR, showing the model reading and interpreting text within an image, such as a scanned document or form.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CLIP's capabilities and the concept of zero-shot classification, before explaining the architecture that makes it possible.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating CLIP's zero-shot image classification capability, showing how text prompts (e.g., 'a photo of a dog') are matched against image embeddings to classify images without task-specific training.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP's dual-encoder architecture maps images and text to a shared space. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining CLIP's architecture in detail, after motivating why a shared embedding space is useful.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of OpenAI's CLIP model, showing the dual-encoder structure: an image encoder (ResNet or ViT) and a text encoder (Transformer), both projecting inputs into a shared embedding space.

image_metadata.caption: Classifier vs. language model objectives: fixed classes vs. flexible text generation. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP moved away from both classifier and language model objectives toward contrastive learning.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting a classifier objective (predicting one of N fixed classes) versus a language model objective (generating free-form text), illustrating their respective output spaces and limitations.

image_metadata.caption: Multiple valid captions for one image motivate contrastive over generative objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately before introducing contrastive learning, to build the intuition for why predicting exact captions is problematic.
image_metadata.description: An illustration showing multiple different human-written captions for the same image (from Flickr30K), demonstrating that a single image can be validly described in many ways.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP's contrastive training: maximize diagonal similarity in an N×N pair matrix. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the central visual when explaining CLIP's contrastive objective — use it to walk through the N×N matrix and the dual classification framing.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how CLIP's contrastive learning works: for a batch of N (image, text) pairs, an N×N similarity matrix is computed, and the model is trained to maximize similarity along the diagonal (correct pairs) and minimize it off-diagonal.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP training pseudocode: symmetric contrastive loss over image-text embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the similarity matrix diagram (image 8) for students ready to go deeper — ideal for those with a programming background or studying model implementation.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for CLIP's contrastive training loop, showing how image and text embeddings are computed, normalized, the similarity matrix is formed, and the symmetric cross-entropy loss is calculated.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive learning gives CLIP a 12x efficiency gain over language model objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the contrastive objective to reinforce its practical advantage with data — useful when students ask 'why does this matter?'
image_metadata.description: A chart or graph showing the training efficiency improvement of CLIP's contrastive objective compared to the language model objective baseline, illustrating the reported 12x efficiency gain.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP achieves strong zero-shot classification via contrastive learning efficiency. (Radford et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP uses contrastive loss instead of a language model objective, and when discussing zero-shot transfer performance benchmarks.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or performance comparison showing CLIP's image classification accuracy as a strong zero-shot baseline, likely compared to supervised models across multiple datasets, illustrating the 12x efficiency improvement from contrastive vs. language model objectives.

image_metadata.caption: unCLIP uses CLIP embeddings to condition image generation via a diffusion decoder. (Ramesh et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from CLIP as a classification/retrieval tool to its role enabling generative models, especially when explaining DALL-E 2's architecture.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2), showing how CLIP's joint image-text embedding space is used in a generative pipeline: a text encoder produces embeddings, a prior maps text to image embeddings, and a diffusion decoder generates images.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo combines a frozen vision encoder with a language model via cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing Flamingo's architecture, as the anchor diagram for all subsequent component-level explanations.
image_metadata.description: High-level architecture diagram of Flamingo showing its two main components: a frozen vision encoder (CLIP-like) that produces visual features, and a large language model (Chinchilla) augmented with cross-attention layers to condition text generation on visual inputs.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo trains on four datasets totaling 2.1B+ image-text pairs with tuned weights. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's training data strategy, particularly to contrast dataset scale with CLIP and to explain the role of dataset weights in the loss function.
image_metadata.description: Table or diagram summarizing Flamingo's 4 training datasets: ALIGN and LTIP (image-text pairs), VTP (video-text pairs), and an interleaved image-text dataset (LTIP/M3W), including dataset sizes and training weights.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo predicts next text tokens conditioned on both text and visual inputs. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo extends a standard language model to handle visual inputs, before introducing Perceiver Resampler and GATED XATTN-DENSE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how Flamingo's language model (Chinchilla) generates text tokens conditioned on both preceding text tokens and visual tokens, showing the modified autoregressive text generation process with visual conditioning.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP vs. Flamingo: contrasting architectures, objectives, and capabilities in vision-language AI.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation exercise after covering both CLIP and Flamingo, or as a review tool before moving to newer LMM architectures.
image_metadata.description: Comparison table or diagram contrasting CLIP and Flamingo across key dimensions: task type (classification/retrieval vs. text generation), architecture, training data, and capabilities, serving as a summary of both models.

image_metadata.caption: ImageBind aligns six modalities into one shared embedding space using images as anchor. (Girdhar et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing future directions in multimodal AI, specifically to illustrate how the field is moving toward unified representations across more than two modalities.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of ImageBind showing a unified embedding space where six modalities (images, text, audio, depth, thermal, IMU) are all aligned together using image as the binding modality, enabling cross-modal retrieval and reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: LaVIN uses lightweight adapters to connect frozen vision and language encoders.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing efficient multimodal training strategies, specifically to contrast with Flamingo's heavier train-from-scratch approach
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LaVIN, showing how a frozen pretrained language model and vision encoder are connected via lightweight adapter modules to enable instruction-following with minimal training from scratch

image_metadata.caption: Adapter architectures for LMMs reduce trainable parameters while preserving pretrained knowledge. (Source: Chunyuan Li, CVPR 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the landscape of efficient multimodal training methods, after introducing BLIP-2 as a motivating example of parameter efficiency gains
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing multiple adapter-based architectures for large multimodal models, showing how different works position adapter layers between frozen vision encoders and language models to reduce trainable parameters

image_metadata.caption: Further adapter designs for LMMs from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 2 as a continuation of the adapter landscape overview, before transitioning to multimodal output generation
image_metadata.description: Second diagram from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial showing additional adapter-based LMM architectures, likely detailing specific design choices such as where adapters are inserted and how modalities are aligned

image_metadata.caption: CM3 generates HTML markup as intermediate output, enabling text and image webpage synthesis.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the two strategies for multimodal output (text intermediate vs. multimodal tokens), use this to illustrate the text-based intermediate approach
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating CM3's approach to multimodal output generation, where the model produces HTML markup as an intermediate text representation that encodes both text content and image references, which are then compiled into full webpages

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V converts a data table image into LaTeX code, a text-intermediate multimodal output.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use alongside the CM3 diagram when explaining text-based intermediate outputs; this example grounds the concept with a familiar, practical tool (GPT-4V)
image_metadata.description: Screenshot or example output showing GPT-4V generating LaTeX code from an input image of a data table, demonstrating how a vision-language model can produce structured text markup that reconstructs the original visual content

image_metadata.caption: LMMs generate interleaved text and images using tagged multimodal tokens. (Koh et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the text-intermediate approach (CM3, GPT-4V), introduce this to contrast with the multimodal token strategy, as described by Caiming Xiong
image_metadata.description: Diagram from 'Generating Images with Multimodal Language Models' (Koh et al., 2023) showing how an LMM interleaves text token generation and image generation/retrieval in a single output stream using tagged multimodal tokens

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'jointly training with image and text' approach; use to explain how visual regions are embedded analogously to word tokens before entering the transformer.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text token embeddings and visual region embeddings (each composed of tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss which pre-training strategies matter and how researchers validate architectural decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, with/without pre-training).

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM uses image patches as prefix tokens with bidirectional encoder and causal decoder. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting SimVLM with VisualBERT; use to explain how the prefix LM paradigm differs from full bidirectional models and why it suits image-conditioned text generation.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a ResNet convolutional stage into prefix tokens fed into a bidirectional encoder, followed by a causal (autoregressive) text decoder that generates output text.

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to discuss why mixing data modalities and choosing the right pre-training objective are critical design decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants: different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM), with/without image-text data, and with/without text-only data.

image_metadata.caption: CM3 tokenizes images and HTML text into a unified causal masked sequence model. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing CM3 as an alternative multimodal approach; use to contrast discrete image tokenization (VQVAE-GAN) with patch-based methods and explain how web-scale HTML data enables multimodal learning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the CM3 causally-masked multimodal modeling approach, showing how HTML web pages with embedded images are tokenized (images via VQVAE-GAN into discrete tokens), masked, and processed by a causal language model to reconstruct masked spans.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM stays frozen during training and inference. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' paradigm; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain why freezing the LM preserves its language capabilities.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training pipeline showing only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) being updated while the language model parameters remain frozen, with image embeddings prepended as prefix to text; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image-text pairs.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen outperforms fine-tuned LM on VQA, showing value of preserving pre-trained knowledge. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion about why fine-tuning a pre-trained LM can be harmful and what the train-blind baseline reveals about LM prior knowledge.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right): Frozen (LM frozen), Frozen finetuned (LM fine-tuned), Frozen scratch (trained from scratch), and Frozen train-blind (image blacked out).

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix tokens via a small trainable network. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to highlight how CLIP's rich visual representations reduce the burden on the mapping network and enable training with very few parameters.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing: CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings → lightweight mapping network F (transformer or MLP) → k prefix embedding vectors → frozen GPT-2 language model generating captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words, showing visual-linguistic alignment. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's architecture; use as an intuition-building example to show that the learned prefix embeddings encode semantically meaningful visual concepts in language space.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', 'outdoor').

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing why pre-trained LM adaptation is preferable to training from scratch, especially in low-data regimes
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning performance metrics

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT self-ensemble module significantly boosts captioning metrics on MS COCO. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the importance of the self-ensemble module in VC-GPT and how to interpret standard image captioning evaluation metrics
image_metadata.description: Performance table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics

image_metadata.caption: MERLOT uses contrastive and temporal reordering objectives for video-language pretraining. (Zellers et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing video-based vision-language models and explaining how temporal structure in videos provides additional training signal beyond static images
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram of MERLOT training: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and temporal frame reordering task on YouTube video segments

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo performance improves with both model scale and number of few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how Flamingo leverages in-context learning at test time and why model scale matters for few-shot multimodal performance
image_metadata.description: Line or bar charts showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (e.g., 3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot, 4-shot, 8-shot, 32-shot) across vision-language benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing few-shot prompting versus fine-tuning paradigms, and when discussing the breadth of tasks a single vision-language model can handle
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) of different sizes against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning

image_metadata.caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives with split text decoders. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa and explaining how combining contrastive and captioning losses in one model captures both discriminative and generative vision-language representations
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of CoCa (Contrastive Captioner) showing a shared image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch

image_metadata.caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to recognition and understanding tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how CoCa handles diverse downstream tasks and the role of attention poolers as lightweight task adapters
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained model is adapted to downstream tasks (visual recognition, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries

image_metadata.caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss combination (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is implemented in practice.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including the dual-loss setup with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.

image_metadata.caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC decoding mechanism to contextualize its practical limitations — specifically when discussing the trade-off between no-training convenience and performance ceiling.
image_metadata.description: A results table comparing MAGiC image captioning performance against other methods on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised approaches and standard metrics (BLEU, CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: PICa n-shot VQA: image captions + few-shot examples prompt GPT-3. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to explain how vision tasks can be solved by converting images to text and leveraging large language models without fine-tuning.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students the architectural overview before diving into specific task examples.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation, with arrows depicting the multimodal prompting chain.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific image captioning application of Socratic Models to illustrate how VLM-to-LM chaining works step by step.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step illustration of the Socratic Models pipeline for image captioning: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, these fill a structured language prompt, and a causal LM generates the final caption.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models vs. ClipCap on COCO captioning: training-free performance gap. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the discussion with benchmark results and highlight the cost-performance trade-off of training-free approaches.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when demonstrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to illustrate how audio, video, and language models can be chained for complex activity understanding tasks.

lessons.title: Multi-Modal Pre-training
lessons.content:
# Multi-Modal Pre-training

A text-only language model learns by predicting the next token. A vision model learns by classifying pixels. **Multi-modal pre-training** is what happens when we stop treating these as separate worlds and instead train a model to *connect* them—so “a red stop sign” and an actual photo of one land in the same mental neighborhood.

There are two big families of objectives that make this work:

1) **Contrastive learning** (match image ↔ text)
2) **Generative next-token prediction** on **interleaved** sequences (predict text while looking at images/videos)

## 1) Contrastive pre-training: learning a shared space (CLIP-style)

The core idea: build two encoders—one for images, one for text—and train them so that the embedding of a caption is close to the embedding of its paired image, and far from other random pairings.

![CLIP's dual-encoder architecture maps images and text to a shared space. (Huyen Chip)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_005.png)

Why this is powerful: once image and text live in the same vector space, you can do “zero-shot classification” by comparing an image embedding to text prompts like “a photo of a {label}”.

A minimal sketch of the training signal:

```python
# Pseudocode: CLIP-style contrastive objective
img_z = image_encoder(images)      # (B, d)
txt_z = text_encoder(captions)     # (B, d)

# cosine similarity matrix between all image-text pairs in batch
logits = (normalize(img_z) @ normalize(txt_z).T) / temperature  # (B, B)

# correct pairs are on the diagonal
loss_i2t = cross_entropy(logits, targets=arange(B))
loss_t2i = cross_entropy(logits.T, targets=arange(B))
loss = (loss_i2t + loss_t2i) / 2
```

This style of pre-training scales well because the supervision is “free”: web-scale (image, text) pairs.

## 2) Interleaved training: treating images like things you can *talk through*

Contrastive models are great at retrieval and recognition, but they don’t automatically become good *conversational* agents. For that, many modern systems train a language model to generate text while conditioning on visual inputs.

The key trick is **interleaved training**: the model sees sequences like

> `<image> “This is a kitchen.” <image> “Now the camera pans to the stove…” “What is the person doing?”`

and learns to continue the text appropriately. This is closer to how we actually use multimodal assistants: multiple images, multiple turns, mixed with text.

## Flamingo: a language model that can ingest interleaved images + text

Flamingo’s headline capability is few-shot, in-context learning for vision-language tasks: you can prompt it with a couple of (image, question → answer) examples, then ask a new question.

![Flamingo engages in multi-turn visual conversations using few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al.)](/api/wiki-images/vision-language-models/images/huyenchip-2023-10-10-multimodal-html_013.png)

Architecturally, Flamingo is “bridge two strong specialists”: a pretrained vision encoder + a pretrained language model, connected so the LM can attend to visual features at the right moments in the token stream.

## BLIP-2: bootstrapping with frozen backbones (train less, reuse more)

A practical problem: end-to-end multimodal training is expensive. BLIP-2 shows a very pragmatic recipe:

- Keep a strong **frozen image encoder**
- Keep a strong **frozen LLM**
- Train a small “translator” module in between (a querying transformer) so the LLM can consume visual information

This often gets you impressive zero-shot behavior with far fewer trainable parameters than training everything from scratch.

## “Natively multimodal” (Gemini-style) vs “bolted-on” multimodal

A useful mental model:

- **Bolted-on**: take an LLM and attach a vision adapter/bridge so it can condition on images.
- **Natively multimodal**: pre-train the core model on multimodal tokens from the start, so multimodality isn’t an add-on—it’s part of the base distribution the model learns.

Both can work; the tradeoff is usually between training cost/complexity and how deeply multimodal reasoning is integrated.

## Summary

Multi-modal pre-training is about learning *alignment* between modalities and then using that alignment to *generate* useful outputs. Contrastive objectives teach “what matches what,” while interleaved next-token training teaches “how to talk and reason with what you see.”

## Recommended Reading
- [Multimodality and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs)](https://huyenchip.com/2023/10/10/multimodal.html) — Clear tour from CLIP to Flamingo to modern LMM design choices.
- [Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision (CLIP)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020) — The canonical contrastive pre-training recipe and why it scales.
- [Flamingo: a Visual Language Model for Few-Shot Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.14198) — Interleaved multimodal sequences and few-shot prompting behavior.
- [BLIP-2 (Transformers docs)](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model_doc/blip-2) — Practical architecture notes on bridging frozen vision encoders and frozen LLMs.
lessons.summary: Multi-modal pre-training connects modalities by either aligning their representations (contrastive learning) or training a language model to generate text conditioned on interleaved visual-text sequences. Systems like Flamingo emphasize interleaved, few-shot prompting behavior, while BLIP-2 shows how to get strong results by training only a small bridge between frozen vision and language backbones.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map, and revisit when explaining why the RL phase needs a KL penalty to stay close to the SFT checkpoint.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) pretraining, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and (3) RLHF with a reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after students understand the three-phase pipeline; use it when explaining why unconstrained reward maximization leads to reward hacking and how the KL term prevents degenerate outputs.
image_metadata.description: The mathematical objective function used in RLHF, showing the combined loss as a sum of the reward model score expectation and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy to stay close to the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based fixes for LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the gap between theoretical expectations and empirical results of RLHF; use it to prompt critical thinking about whether RL can reliably teach models to express uncertainty.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs 'know what they know' and that RL-based methods could force models to only assert information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF increases hallucination despite higher overall preference. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing RLHF benefits to immediately introduce nuance; ideal for discussions on evaluation metrics, reward hacking, and why optimizing for human preference does not guarantee factual correctness.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart or comparison graphic from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit higher hallucination rates than SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Full LLM training pipeline: pre-training through post-training alignment. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM training to orient students before introducing specific stages; return to it as a navigation aid when transitioning between pre-training and post-training topics.
image_metadata.description: A horizontal pipeline diagram showing the full LLM development and training workflow, from data collection through pre-training, continued pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and alignment/post-training stages, with annotations highlighting new methodologies covered in the article.

image_metadata.caption: MMLU scores for recent open-weight LLMs; higher is better. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface when discussing how to evaluate LLMs or when comparing Qwen 2 to other models; useful for teaching students to critically read benchmark tables and understand what MMLU measures.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing MMLU benchmark scores across the latest open-weight LLMs (including Qwen 2 variants and contemporaries), with model names on the x-axis and MMLU accuracy scores on the y-axis, sourced directly from official research papers.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen 2 pre-training techniques including two-stage continued pre-training. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching data-centric approaches to pre-training or when contrasting Qwen 2's pipeline with other models; ideal for a discussion on why data quality matters as much as quantity.
image_metadata.description: A structured summary table or diagram listing the key techniques used in Qwen 2 pre-training, including data filtering, data mixing, synthetic data generation via prior Qwen models, multi-task instruction data inclusion, and a two-stage pre-training approach (standard pre-training followed by long-context continued pre-training).

image_metadata.caption: Typical LLM development flow: pre-training → SFT → alignment. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce before explaining DPO or SFT in detail; also effective when students ask 'why can't you just use the base model?' to show the role of post-training stages.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the typical LLM development flow: starting from a base pre-trained model, moving through supervised fine-tuning (SFT), then alignment via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or direct preference optimization (DPO), resulting in a chat/instruction-following model.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen 2 post-training: offline then online DPO with rejection sampling. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching the difference between offline and online RL/DPO methods, or when discussing how preference datasets are constructed; pairs well with the DPO overview diagram (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: A summary diagram or table of Qwen 2 post-training techniques, covering the two-phase alignment approach: offline DPO on an existing dataset followed by online DPO with a reward model using rejection sampling, plus details on dataset construction combining human labeling and synthetic annotation.

image_metadata.caption: DPO alignment: training LLMs to prefer chosen over rejected responses. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing DPO to students, especially before or after a mathematical derivation; also useful as a reference when students encounter DPO mentioned in multiple model papers throughout the article.
image_metadata.description: A detailed overview diagram of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for LLM alignment, likely showing the training objective, the role of chosen vs. rejected responses, the reference model, and how the policy model is updated to prefer human-preferred outputs over rejected ones.
