image_metadata.caption: Apple AFM's 3-stage pre-training pipeline with data reweighting. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when comparing pre-training strategies across different organizations, or when teaching the concept of training curricula and how data composition shifts between pre-training stages.
image_metadata.description: A three-step pre-training pipeline diagram for Apple's AFM (Apple Foundation Models), showing: (1) core pre-training on 6.3 trillion tokens, (2) continued pre-training with down-weighted web-crawl data and up-weighted math/code data, and (3) a final context-extension or specialization stage.

image_metadata.caption: Knowledge distillation: student (3B) learns from teacher (6.4B) soft labels. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing knowledge distillation — use it to explain why distillation provides a richer training signal than standard cross-entropy on hard labels alone.
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing two training approaches: (a) standard pre-training using cross-entropy loss on training tokens, and (b) knowledge distillation where a 3B student model learns from both training tokens and soft labels from a 6.4B teacher model, with a convex combination loss (0.9 weight on teacher labels).

image_metadata.caption: AFM pre-training pipeline: core training, distillation, and context lengthening. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a wrap-up after covering each AFM pre-training stage, or as an overview before diving into individual stages to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Summary table or diagram listing the techniques used across the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) pre-training pipeline, including core pre-training, continued pre-training, and context lengthening stages.

image_metadata.caption: AFM post-training: iTeC combines SFT, DPO, IPO, and online RL. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching post-training alignment, especially to contrast single-algorithm approaches with Apple's committee-based iterative method.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) post-training pipeline, covering supervised fine-tuning (SFT), rejection sampling, preference tuning methods (DPO, IPO, online RL), and the iTeC committee-based iterative approach with a reward model.

image_metadata.caption: Gemma 2 pre-training: distillation for small models, scratch training for 27B. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing pre-training strategies across model families, or when discussing why knowledge distillation is preferred over simply scaling data for smaller models.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 pre-training techniques, highlighting knowledge distillation for smaller models (27B trained from scratch; smaller models distilled), training data scale, and the rationale that small models are often undertrained.

image_metadata.caption: Gemma 2 post-training: oversized reward model and WARP averaging. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing RLHF reward model design choices, or when contrasting Gemma 2's post-training with Apple's iTeC approach.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 post-training techniques, including SFT with teacher-generated responses (knowledge distillation during fine-tuning), RLHF with a reward model 10x larger than the policy model, and WARP model averaging.

image_metadata.caption: MMLU scores: Llama 3.1 405B approaches GPT-4 level performance. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Llama 3.1 to motivate why its training approach matters, or when teaching how benchmark results relate to model size and training choices.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart showing MMLU benchmark scores for multiple LLM models including Llama 3.1 variants (8B, 70B, 405B), GPT-4, and others, illustrating how Llama 3.1 405B approaches or matches frontier model performance.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 pre-training: 3 stages ending with high-quality annealing data. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching data curriculum strategies in pre-training, especially to illustrate the outsized impact of final-stage annealing on specific benchmarks.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 pre-training pipeline across three stages: initial pre-training on 15T tokens, continued pre-training with adjusted data mix (~800B tokens), and annealing on high-quality data (including GSM8K and MATH) for the final stage.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: iterative SFT+DPO rounds with rejection sampling. (Raschka/Meta)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching DPO-based alignment pipelines, especially to contrast with PPO/RLHF and to demonstrate why iterative rounds improve stability.
image_metadata.description: Annotated figure from the Llama 3.1 technical paper showing the iterative post-training procedure: multiple rounds of SFT and DPO, rejection sampling using a reward model, and incorporation of both human and synthetic data across rounds.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: SFT, DPO, rejection sampling, and model averaging. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary after covering Llama 3.1 post-training details, or to compare with AFM and Gemma 2 post-training approaches side by side.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 post-training techniques, covering SFT, rejection sampling, DPO, reward model training, and model averaging/merging applied to SFT, DPO, and reward models across training rounds.

image_metadata.caption: Pre-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the pre-training phase of LLM development, especially when contrasting how different organizations structure their training pipelines and what steps they share.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or visual overview showing pre-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, highlighting shared and distinct methods such as multi-stage pre-training, context lengthening, and annealing steps.

image_metadata.caption: Post-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing LLM alignment and post-training strategies, particularly to illustrate the diversity of approaches and prompt discussion about trade-offs between DPO and RLHF.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or visual overview showing post-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, covering methods such as rejection sampling, DPO, RLHF, and supervised fine-tuning stages.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau et al. (2014): Attention mechanism added to RNN encoder-decoder.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing attention mechanisms before covering transformers; use to contrast RNN-based attention with the self-attention in transformers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the attention mechanism added to a recurrent neural network (RNN) for sequence-to-sequence tasks, showing how attention weights connect encoder hidden states to decoder outputs for neural machine translation.

image_metadata.caption: Vaswani et al. (2017): Original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the central reference diagram when teaching the transformer architecture; revisit when discussing BERT (encoder-only) and GPT (decoder-only) as architectural derivatives.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the original Transformer model from 'Attention Is All You Need', showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right) with multi-head attention blocks, feed-forward layers, positional encoding, and residual connections.

image_metadata.caption: Devlin et al. (2018): BERT pre-training with masked language modeling and fine-tuning.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining encoder-style LLMs and the bifurcation between encoder (BERT) and decoder (GPT) architectures; use to contrast with GPT's unidirectional pretraining.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the BERT paper illustrating pre-training objectives (Masked Language Modeling and Next Sentence Prediction) and fine-tuning on downstream tasks, showing bidirectional context flow through the transformer encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Radford & Narasimhan (2018): GPT decoder-only architecture with pretraining and fine-tuning.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the BERT diagram when teaching the encoder vs. decoder architectural split; use as a foundation before introducing GPT-2, GPT-3, and InstructGPT.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the original GPT paper showing the decoder-only transformer architecture and the two-stage approach: unsupervised pretraining via next-word prediction followed by supervised fine-tuning on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Lewis et al. (2019): BART combines encoder-decoder for denoising sequence-to-sequence pretraining.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering BERT and GPT to illustrate how BART synthesizes both paradigms; useful when discussing trade-offs between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the BART paper comparing BART's encoder-decoder denoising pretraining with other models (BERT, GPT, etc.), showing how corrupted input text is reconstructed by the full sequence-to-sequence architecture.

image_metadata.caption: Dao et al. (2022): FlashAttention uses IO-aware tiling for fast, memory-efficient attention.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing efficiency improvements to the transformer; use to motivate why hardware-aware algorithm design matters for scaling LLMs.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the FlashAttention paper illustrating the IO-aware tiled computation approach for computing exact attention efficiently, contrasting memory access patterns between standard attention and FlashAttention on GPU hardware.

image_metadata.caption: Geiping & Goldstein (2022): BERT trained on one GPU in 24h reveals efficiency trade-offs.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing scaling laws and compute budgets; use to challenge students' intuitions about the relationship between model size, training time, and performance.
image_metadata.description: Results figure from the Cramming paper showing performance (e.g., GLUE scores) of a BERT-style model trained on a single GPU for 24 hours, compared against baselines, with analysis of training efficiency trade-offs.

image_metadata.caption: Lialin et al. (2022): Taxonomy of 40+ parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing fine-tuning strategies for LLMs; use as a reference map when students encounter specific PEFT methods like LoRA or adapters in later readings.
image_metadata.description: Taxonomy diagram from the PEFT survey categorizing over 40 parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods into groups such as prefix tuning, adapters, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA), with comparisons of their properties.

image_metadata.caption: Hoffmann et al. (2022): Chinchilla scaling law — training tokens matter as much as model size.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching scaling laws; use to prompt discussion on why many contemporary LLMs were undertrained and how this insight changed subsequent model development.
image_metadata.description: Figures from the Chinchilla paper showing the relationship between model size, number of training tokens, and loss, illustrating the linear scaling law and demonstrating that Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) outperforms GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens).

image_metadata.caption: Ouyang et al. (2022): InstructGPT three-step RLHF pipeline for aligning GPT-3.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM alignment and RLHF; use as the primary reference diagram before discussing Constitutional AI and other alignment approaches.
image_metadata.description: Three-step pipeline diagram from the InstructGPT paper showing: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs, (2) reward model training from human preference rankings, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align GPT-3.

image_metadata.caption: Constitutional AI: self-supervised harmlessness training via written principles. (Anthropic, 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Constitutional AI as an alternative to RLHF, specifically to contrast human-feedback-based alignment with principle-based AI self-supervision.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Constitutional AI training pipeline, showing how a set of written principles (a 'constitution') guides a self-critique and revision loop to produce a harmless AI system without direct human supervision at each step.

image_metadata.caption: The three-step RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, and PPO optimization. (Stiennon et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on RLHF or instruction-tuning; use as a reference diagram when explaining InstructGPT or comparing alignment techniques.
image_metadata.description: Wide-format diagram depicting the three-step RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) procedure: (1) supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations, (2) training a reward model from human preference comparisons, and (3) optimizing the language model policy using PPO against the reward model.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention mechanism. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention; use to walk through each computational step before presenting the mathematical formulation
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel attention heads, including linear projections, scaled dot-product attention blocks, concatenation, and final linear transformation

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show early when introducing the Transformer architecture as a whole; reference back when discussing individual components like attention or positional encoding to anchor them in the full model
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention and feed-forward layers, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers, plus input/output embeddings and positional encoding

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap, L=32, d=128. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the need for positional encoding due to permutation invariance of self-attention; use to connect the mathematical formula to its visual pattern before discussing learned alternatives
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating wave patterns across positions and dimensions with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white)

image_metadata.caption: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) applied to query/key vectors. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after covering sinusoidal encoding; show when explaining how RoPE encodes relative positions and why it improves context length extrapolation compared to absolute positional encodings
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how 2D rotation matrices are applied to query and key vectors in pairs of dimensions, with rotation angles proportional to position index i and dimension-specific frequencies

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL training with segment recurrence. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the context segmentation problem of vanilla Transformers; use the side-by-side comparison to motivate why Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism is needed
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training in vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches hidden states from previous segments and extends the effective attention span across segment boundaries

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer with memory and compressed memory tiers. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after Transformer-XL as a natural extension; use to explain the tradeoff between temporal range and memory cost, and to motivate the compression loss objectives
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory tiers: a standard memory buffer of recent past activations and a compressed memory buffer of older activations, with compression functions reducing older memories to save space while retaining information

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions in Compressive Transformer. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Compressive Transformer architecture; use as empirical support that compression preserves useful information and that the model attends to both recent and older compressed memories
image_metadata.description: Bar chart showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) across memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right), demonstrating that compressed memories (older positions) receive comparable attention to recent memories

image_metadata.caption: SPALM combining short-term hidden states with long-term k-NN memory. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing non-differentiable external memory augmentation; use to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and to explain how retrieval-augmented language models work
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM (Sparse Pointer Attention Language Model) showing how short-term memory (past hidden states) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce context-aware token representations

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches training Memorizing Transformer from scratch. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the Memorizing Transformer to highlight its practical advantage; use to motivate why fine-tuning with memory augmentation is a viable and efficient strategy
image_metadata.description: Line graph comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory added, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves similar performance to training from scratch

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds linear distance-based bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when comparing positional encoding strategies; use to contrast with RoPE and sinusoidal encoding, emphasizing ALiBi's simplicity and its ability to generalize to longer sequences at inference
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) modifies attention scores by subtracting a linear positional bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions, with different slopes per attention head

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.

image_metadata.caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
image_metadata.description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically after explaining why standard self-attention is O(L²) and before deriving the Linformer projection math.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) line plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders computation to avoid the full attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, specifically to illustrate why reordering (φ(Q)(φ(K)ᵀV)) is cheaper than ((QKᵀ)V).
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computing the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reordering operations so the kernel feature map φ is applied first, enabling linear-time computation by avoiding the explicit attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism, when discussing why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error plots for the Performer model: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing orthogonal features have lower approximation error; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower error for softmax kernel approximation.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm for stable RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers for RL, specifically to explain why vanilla Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how GTrXL's two modifications address this.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm after residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with layer norm reordered to before the sub-layer (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using pre-norm, designed for stable RL training.

image_metadata.caption: VLMs handle diverse tasks from captioning to document understanding. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a VLM lesson to motivate why these models matter and what problems they solve before introducing technical details
image_metadata.description: A visual overview of VLM capabilities, likely showing icons or examples of tasks such as visual question answering, image captioning, document understanding, and web page analysis

image_metadata.caption: Vision Arena ranks VLMs via anonymous human preference voting. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to select or compare VLMs, especially to contrast human-preference evaluation against automated benchmark metrics
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Vision Arena leaderboard interface, showing a side-by-side anonymous model comparison UI where users submit an image and prompt and vote on preferred outputs

image_metadata.caption: Open VLM Leaderboard ranks models across benchmarks with size and license filters. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching model selection criteria, especially when students need to choose a VLM for a project with specific size or licensing constraints
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of the Open VLM Leaderboard showing a ranked table of vision language models with scores across multiple benchmark metrics, with filtering options for model size and license type

image_metadata.caption: VLM architecture: image encoder, projector, and text decoder stacked sequentially. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing VLM architecture, especially before or alongside explaining LLaVA's training procedure to ground the text description in a visual schema
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a typical vision language model showing the sequential pipeline: image encoder → multimodal embedding projector → text decoder, with annotations indicating which components are frozen vs trained during pretraining

image_metadata.caption: Projector aligns image features with text embeddings for the language decoder. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into how image and text representations are actually merged, especially when explaining the projector training stage
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram labeled 'Structure of a Typical Vision Language Model' showing how image features from the encoder are projected into the text embedding space via a multimodal projector and combined with text tokens before being fed to the language model decoder

image_metadata.caption: BLIP-2 architecture: frozen image encoder, Q-Former bridge, and frozen LLM. (Li et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing BLIP-2 to explain the modular three-stage architecture before discussing training objectives or benchmarks; also use it when contrasting BLIP-2's efficiency against larger models like Flamingo.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of BLIP-2 showing the three-component pipeline: a frozen pre-trained image encoder on the left, a lightweight 12-layer Querying Transformer (Q-Former) in the middle acting as a bridge, and a frozen large language model (LLM) on the right, with arrows indicating the flow of visual information through the system.

image_metadata.caption: Multimodal AI in healthcare: combining images, text, and signals. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the motivation for multimodal AI — specifically when arguing that some use cases are impossible with a single modality.
image_metadata.description: A visual showing multimodal AI applications in healthcare, likely depicting how different data types (medical images, text reports, audio) are combined for clinical tasks such as diagnosis, radiology, or patient monitoring.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V use cases illustrating multimodal reasoning and interaction. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on multimodal AI to build intuition and motivation before introducing technical content.
image_metadata.description: A showcase of GPT-4V use cases demonstrating multimodal capabilities, likely showing examples where the model processes images alongside text to answer questions, describe scenes, or perform reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V performing OCR and document processing on an image. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining classification as a multimodal task type, particularly to contrast fixed-class outputs with more flexible generative approaches.
image_metadata.description: An example of GPT-4V performing document processing or OCR, showing the model reading and interpreting text within an image, such as a scanned document or form.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP enables zero-shot image classification via shared text-image embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CLIP's capabilities and the concept of zero-shot classification, before explaining the architecture that makes it possible.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating CLIP's zero-shot image classification capability, showing how text prompts (e.g., 'a photo of a dog') are matched against image embeddings to classify images without task-specific training.

image_metadata.caption: Classifier vs. language model objectives: fixed classes vs. flexible text generation. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP moved away from both classifier and language model objectives toward contrastive learning.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting a classifier objective (predicting one of N fixed classes) versus a language model objective (generating free-form text), illustrating their respective output spaces and limitations.

image_metadata.caption: Multiple valid captions for one image motivate contrastive over generative objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately before introducing contrastive learning, to build the intuition for why predicting exact captions is problematic.
image_metadata.description: An illustration showing multiple different human-written captions for the same image (from Flickr30K), demonstrating that a single image can be validly described in many ways.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP's contrastive training: maximize diagonal similarity in an N×N pair matrix. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the central visual when explaining CLIP's contrastive objective — use it to walk through the N×N matrix and the dual classification framing.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how CLIP's contrastive learning works: for a batch of N (image, text) pairs, an N×N similarity matrix is computed, and the model is trained to maximize similarity along the diagonal (correct pairs) and minimize it off-diagonal.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP training pseudocode: symmetric contrastive loss over image-text embeddings. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the similarity matrix diagram (image 8) for students ready to go deeper — ideal for those with a programming background or studying model implementation.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for CLIP's contrastive training loop, showing how image and text embeddings are computed, normalized, the similarity matrix is formed, and the symmetric cross-entropy loss is calculated.

image_metadata.caption: Contrastive learning gives CLIP a 12x efficiency gain over language model objectives. (Huyen Chip)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the contrastive objective to reinforce its practical advantage with data — useful when students ask 'why does this matter?'
image_metadata.description: A chart or graph showing the training efficiency improvement of CLIP's contrastive objective compared to the language model objective baseline, illustrating the reported 12x efficiency gain.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP achieves strong zero-shot classification via contrastive learning efficiency. (Radford et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why CLIP uses contrastive loss instead of a language model objective, and when discussing zero-shot transfer performance benchmarks.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or performance comparison showing CLIP's image classification accuracy as a strong zero-shot baseline, likely compared to supervised models across multiple datasets, illustrating the 12x efficiency improvement from contrastive vs. language model objectives.

image_metadata.caption: unCLIP uses CLIP embeddings to condition image generation via a diffusion decoder. (Ramesh et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from CLIP as a classification/retrieval tool to its role enabling generative models, especially when explaining DALL-E 2's architecture.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2), showing how CLIP's joint image-text embedding space is used in a generative pipeline: a text encoder produces embeddings, a prior maps text to image embeddings, and a diffusion decoder generates images.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo combines a frozen vision encoder with a language model via cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing Flamingo's architecture, as the anchor diagram for all subsequent component-level explanations.
image_metadata.description: High-level architecture diagram of Flamingo showing its two main components: a frozen vision encoder (CLIP-like) that produces visual features, and a large language model (Chinchilla) augmented with cross-attention layers to condition text generation on visual inputs.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo trains on four datasets totaling 2.1B+ image-text pairs with tuned weights. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's training data strategy, particularly to contrast dataset scale with CLIP and to explain the role of dataset weights in the loss function.
image_metadata.description: Table or diagram summarizing Flamingo's 4 training datasets: ALIGN and LTIP (image-text pairs), VTP (video-text pairs), and an interleaved image-text dataset (LTIP/M3W), including dataset sizes and training weights.

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo predicts next text tokens conditioned on both text and visual inputs. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo extends a standard language model to handle visual inputs, before introducing Perceiver Resampler and GATED XATTN-DENSE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how Flamingo's language model (Chinchilla) generates text tokens conditioned on both preceding text tokens and visual tokens, showing the modified autoregressive text generation process with visual conditioning.

image_metadata.caption: Perceiver Resampler compresses variable visual features into 64 fixed visual tokens. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Flamingo handles variable numbers of image/video features, specifically to illustrate the role of Perceiver Resampler before discussing GATED XATTN-DENSE.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of Flamingo's Perceiver Resampler module, showing how variable-length visual features from the vision encoder (images or video frames) are compressed into a fixed set of 64 visual output tokens via cross-attention with learned latent queries.

image_metadata.caption: GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inject visual context into frozen LM layers via gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Perceiver Resampler to complete the picture of how visual tokens are integrated into text generation, emphasizing the ablation result showing its necessity.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of Flamingo's GATED XATTN-DENSE layers inserted between frozen Chinchilla LM layers, showing how cross-attention allows LM layers to attend to visual tokens, with a gating mechanism controlling the contribution of visual information.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP vs. Flamingo: contrasting architectures, objectives, and capabilities in vision-language AI.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation exercise after covering both CLIP and Flamingo, or as a review tool before moving to newer LMM architectures.
image_metadata.description: Comparison table or diagram contrasting CLIP and Flamingo across key dimensions: task type (classification/retrieval vs. text generation), architecture, training data, and capabilities, serving as a summary of both models.

image_metadata.caption: ImageBind aligns six modalities into one shared embedding space using images as anchor. (Girdhar et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing future directions in multimodal AI, specifically to illustrate how the field is moving toward unified representations across more than two modalities.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of ImageBind showing a unified embedding space where six modalities (images, text, audio, depth, thermal, IMU) are all aligned together using image as the binding modality, enabling cross-modal retrieval and reasoning.

image_metadata.caption: LaVIN uses lightweight adapters to connect frozen vision and language encoders.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing efficient multimodal training strategies, specifically to contrast with Flamingo's heavier train-from-scratch approach
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of LaVIN, showing how a frozen pretrained language model and vision encoder are connected via lightweight adapter modules to enable instruction-following with minimal training from scratch

image_metadata.caption: Adapter architectures for LMMs reduce trainable parameters while preserving pretrained knowledge. (Source: Chunyuan Li, CVPR 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the landscape of efficient multimodal training methods, after introducing BLIP-2 as a motivating example of parameter efficiency gains
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing multiple adapter-based architectures for large multimodal models, showing how different works position adapter layers between frozen vision encoders and language models to reduce trainable parameters

image_metadata.caption: Further adapter designs for LMMs from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 2 as a continuation of the adapter landscape overview, before transitioning to multimodal output generation
image_metadata.description: Second diagram from Chunyuan Li's CVPR 2023 tutorial showing additional adapter-based LMM architectures, likely detailing specific design choices such as where adapters are inserted and how modalities are aligned

image_metadata.caption: CM3 generates HTML markup as intermediate output, enabling text and image webpage synthesis.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the two strategies for multimodal output (text intermediate vs. multimodal tokens), use this to illustrate the text-based intermediate approach
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating CM3's approach to multimodal output generation, where the model produces HTML markup as an intermediate text representation that encodes both text content and image references, which are then compiled into full webpages

image_metadata.caption: GPT-4V converts a data table image into LaTeX code, a text-intermediate multimodal output.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use alongside the CM3 diagram when explaining text-based intermediate outputs; this example grounds the concept with a familiar, practical tool (GPT-4V)
image_metadata.description: Screenshot or example output showing GPT-4V generating LaTeX code from an input image of a data table, demonstrating how a vision-language model can produce structured text markup that reconstructs the original visual content

image_metadata.caption: LMMs generate interleaved text and images using tagged multimodal tokens. (Koh et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the text-intermediate approach (CM3, GPT-4V), introduce this to contrast with the multimodal token strategy, as described by Caiming Xiong
image_metadata.description: Diagram from 'Generating Images with Multimodal Language Models' (Koh et al., 2023) showing how an LMM interleaves text token generation and image generation/retrieval in a single output stream using tagged multimodal tokens

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'jointly training with image and text' approach; use to explain how visual regions are embedded analogously to word tokens before entering the transformer.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text token embeddings and visual region embeddings (each composed of tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss which pre-training strategies matter and how researchers validate architectural decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, with/without pre-training).

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM uses image patches as prefix tokens with bidirectional encoder and causal decoder. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting SimVLM with VisualBERT; use to explain how the prefix LM paradigm differs from full bidirectional models and why it suits image-conditioned text generation.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a ResNet convolutional stage into prefix tokens fed into a bidirectional encoder, followed by a causal (autoregressive) text decoder that generates output text.

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to discuss why mixing data modalities and choosing the right pre-training objective are critical design decisions.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants: different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM), with/without image-text data, and with/without text-only data.

image_metadata.caption: CM3 tokenizes images and HTML text into a unified causal masked sequence model. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing CM3 as an alternative multimodal approach; use to contrast discrete image tokenization (VQVAE-GAN) with patch-based methods and explain how web-scale HTML data enables multimodal learning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the CM3 causally-masked multimodal modeling approach, showing how HTML web pages with embedded images are tokenized (images via VQVAE-GAN into discrete tokens), masked, and processed by a causal language model to reconstruct masked spans.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM stays frozen during training and inference. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' paradigm; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain why freezing the LM preserves its language capabilities.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training pipeline showing only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) being updated while the language model parameters remain frozen, with image embeddings prepended as prefix to text; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image-text pairs.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen outperforms fine-tuned LM on VQA, showing value of preserving pre-trained knowledge. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion about why fine-tuning a pre-trained LM can be harmful and what the train-blind baseline reveals about LM prior knowledge.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right): Frozen (LM frozen), Frozen finetuned (LM fine-tuned), Frozen scratch (trained from scratch), and Frozen train-blind (image blacked out).

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix tokens via a small trainable network. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to highlight how CLIP's rich visual representations reduce the burden on the mapping network and enable training with very few parameters.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing: CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings → lightweight mapping network F (transformer or MLP) → k prefix embedding vectors → frozen GPT-2 language model generating captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words, showing visual-linguistic alignment. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's architecture; use as an intuition-building example to show that the learned prefix embeddings encode semantically meaningful visual concepts in language space.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', 'outdoor').

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals per layer. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing cross-attention fusion mechanisms as an alternative to prefix-based approaches; use to explain how gates B_vis and B_lan allow the model to selectively weight visual vs. linguistic signals.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualGPT showing a visual encoder feeding into a GPT-style decoder via a cross-attention mechanism with a Self-Resurrecting Activation Unit (SRAU) that uses complementary gates B_vis and B_lan to balance visual and linguistic information at each decoder layer.

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing why pre-trained LM adaptation is preferable to training from scratch, especially in low-data regimes
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning performance metrics

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how VC-GPT avoids catastrophic forgetting by using separate cross-attention layers rather than injecting visual features directly into GPT2
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a self-ensemble module combining language decoder logits and vision-language fused logits, with GPT2 as the language decoder

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT self-ensemble module significantly boosts captioning metrics on MS COCO. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the importance of the self-ensemble module in VC-GPT and how to interpret standard image captioning evaluation metrics
image_metadata.description: Performance table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics

image_metadata.caption: MERLOT uses contrastive and temporal reordering objectives for video-language pretraining. (Zellers et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing video-based vision-language models and explaining how temporal structure in videos provides additional training signal beyond static images
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram of MERLOT training: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and temporal frame reordering task on YouTube video segments

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo connects CLIP and a frozen LM via Perceiver Resampler and gated cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Flamingo as a case study in efficiently connecting pretrained vision and language models, especially before discussing the gated cross-attention mechanism in detail
image_metadata.description: High-level architecture overview of Flamingo showing a pretrained vision encoder (CLIP), a Perceiver Resampler producing fixed visual tokens, and gated cross-attention layers interleaved with frozen LM layers, accepting interleaved image-text input

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer preserves pretrained LM behavior at initialization. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific mechanism Flamingo uses to safely inject visual information into a frozen LM, particularly the role of the learned gate initialized to zero
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how visual tokens attend to language hidden states with a learned tanh gate that initializes to zero to preserve pretrained LM behavior

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo performance improves with both model scale and number of few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how Flamingo leverages in-context learning at test time and why model scale matters for few-shot multimodal performance
image_metadata.description: Line or bar charts showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (e.g., 3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot, 4-shot, 8-shot, 32-shot) across vision-language benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing few-shot prompting versus fine-tuning paradigms, and when discussing the breadth of tasks a single vision-language model can handle
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) of different sizes against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning

image_metadata.caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives with split text decoders. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa and explaining how combining contrastive and captioning losses in one model captures both discriminative and generative vision-language representations
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of CoCa (Contrastive Captioner) showing a shared image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch

image_metadata.caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to recognition and understanding tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how CoCa handles diverse downstream tasks and the role of attention poolers as lightweight task adapters
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how CoCa's pretrained model is adapted to downstream tasks (visual recognition, VQA, image captioning) using task-specific attention poolers with different numbers of learnable queries

image_metadata.caption: CoCa pseudo-code: dual contrastive and captioning loss training. (Yu & Wang et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how CoCa differs from contrastive-only or captioning-only models, especially to illustrate how the weighted loss combination (λ_cap=2.0, λ_con=1.0) is implemented in practice.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block showing the CoCa (Contrastive Captioners) architecture and training procedure, including the dual-loss setup with contrastive and captioning objectives, weighted combination, and the encoder-decoder structure.

image_metadata.caption: MAGiC captioning results on COCO and Flickr30k vs. supervised baselines. (Su et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the MAGiC decoding mechanism to contextualize its practical limitations — specifically when discussing the trade-off between no-training convenience and performance ceiling.
image_metadata.description: A results table comparing MAGiC image captioning performance against other methods on COCO and Flickr30k benchmarks, broken down by supervised vs. unsupervised approaches and standard metrics (BLEU, CIDEr, SPICE, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: PICa n-shot VQA: image captions + few-shot examples prompt GPT-3. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing PICa to explain how vision tasks can be solved by converting images to text and leveraging large language models without fine-tuning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how PICa performs n-shot Visual Question Answering at inference time: an image is converted to a caption/tags, then combined with n few-shot example (image-caption, question, answer) triplets as a prompt fed into GPT-3.

image_metadata.caption: PICa-Full vs. PICa-Base on OK-VQA: impact of example selection and ensembling. (Yang et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the two PICa improvements to give students concrete evidence of how each design choice contributes to the +8.6 point SoTA improvement on OK-VQA.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing PICa-Base (random in-context examples) vs. PICa-Full (similar example selection + multi-query ensembling) against other methods on the OK-VQA knowledge-based VQA benchmark.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models: language as interface composing VLM, LM, and ALM. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Socratic Models explanation to give students the architectural overview before diving into specific task examples.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Socratic Models framework showing how multiple pretrained models (VLM, LM, ALM) exchange information through language as an intermediate representation, with arrows depicting the multimodal prompting chain.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models captioning: VLM fills prompt slots, LM generates caption. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the specific image captioning application of Socratic Models to illustrate how VLM-to-LM chaining works step by step.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step illustration of the Socratic Models pipeline for image captioning: VLM predicts place/object/image-type categories, these fill a structured language prompt, and a causal LM generates the final caption.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models vs. ClipCap on COCO captioning: training-free performance gap. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Socratic Models captioning pipeline to ground the discussion with benchmark results and highlight the cost-performance trade-off of training-free approaches.
image_metadata.description: Table comparing image captioning performance (CIDEr, SPICE, CLIP-S metrics) of Socratic Models against ClipCap and other baselines on 100 random COCO test examples, with training-free vs. trained model distinctions.

image_metadata.caption: Socratic Models for egocentric video: chaining ALM, VLM, and LM via language. (Zeng et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when demonstrating the flexibility of Socratic Models beyond image captioning, particularly to illustrate how audio, video, and language models can be chained for complex activity understanding tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Socratic Models pipeline for egocentric video understanding: audio is processed by ALM (speech-to-text), video frames by VLM (image-to-text), outputs are combined into a structured prompt, and an LM generates activity summaries and answers questions.

lessons.title: Diffusion Models for Image Generation
lessons.content:
# Diffusion Models for Image Generation

Diffusion models feel almost like a magic trick you can explain to a friend.

1. Take a real image.
2. Add a tiny bit of Gaussian noise.
3. Repeat until it becomes pure static.

Now the trick: if you can learn how to *undo* each tiny noising step, you can start from random noise and “walk back” into a brand-new image.

![Forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) Markov chain in DDPM. (Ho et al., 2020; adapted from Weng)](/api/wiki-images/diffusion-models/images/lilianweng-posts-2021-07-11-diffusion-models_002.png)

## 1) The forward process: a controlled destruction

In **DDPM**-style diffusion, the forward process is fixed (no learning). It’s a Markov chain:

\[
q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1-\beta_t}\,x_{t-1},\, \beta_t I)
\]

- \(\beta_t\) is a small variance you choose (a **noise schedule**), typically increasing with \(t\).
- After many steps, \(x_T\) is basically standard Gaussian noise.

A key convenience: you can sample \(x_t\) directly from \(x_0\) in closed form:
\[
x_t = \sqrt{\bar\alpha_t}\,x_0 + \sqrt{1-\bar\alpha_t}\,\varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon\sim\mathcal{N}(0,I)
\]
where \(\alpha_t=1-\beta_t\) and \(\bar\alpha_t=\prod_{i=1}^t \alpha_i\).

## 2) The reverse process: learn to denoise

The reverse chain is what we learn:
\[
p_\theta(x_{t-1}\mid x_t)
\]

In practice, we train a neural net (often a U-Net) to predict the noise \(\varepsilon\) that was added. Once you can estimate \(\varepsilon\), you can compute a good estimate of the clean direction and step from \(x_t\) to \(x_{t-1}\).

![Training algorithm: predict noise ε at random time step t, minimize MSE. (Ho et al., 2020; adapted from Hugging Face)](/api/wiki-images/diffusion-models/images/huggingface-co-blog-annotated-diffusion_003.png)

### The training objective (the “surprisingly simple” part)

Sample a random timestep \(t\), corrupt \(x_0\) into \(x_t\), and train:
\[
\min_\theta \; \mathbb{E}\big[\lVert \varepsilon - \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t) \rVert^2\big]
\]

That’s it: **noise prediction with MSE**.

```python
# pseudo-code for DDPM training
x0 = sample_real_image()
t = randint(1, T)
eps = randn_like(x0)
xt = sqrt(alpha_bar[t]) * x0 + sqrt(1 - alpha_bar[t]) * eps

eps_hat = model(xt, t)          # predict noise
loss = mse(eps_hat, eps)
loss.backward(); opt.step()
```

## 3) From DDPM to Stable Diffusion: do it in latent space

Running diffusion directly on pixels is expensive: the model must denoise huge tensors for hundreds/thousands of steps.

**Latent diffusion** (popularized by **Stable Diffusion**) compresses images with an autoencoder into a smaller **latent** representation, runs diffusion there, then decodes back to pixels. You keep the “diffusion magic,” but at a fraction of the compute.

## 4) Text conditioning + classifier-free guidance

To make text-to-image work, the denoiser needs access to the prompt. A common approach is **cross-attention**: image/latent features act like “queries,” and text embeddings provide “keys/values,” letting the model pull in the right semantic details while denoising.

Then comes a practical knob: **classifier-free guidance (CFG)**.

You train the same model sometimes with the prompt, sometimes with an empty prompt. At sampling time you combine both predictions:
- unconditional prediction: “what would I draw with no prompt?”
- conditional prediction: “what would I draw for this prompt?”

Cranking the guidance scale pushes samples to match the prompt more strongly (often sharper/more literal), but too high can reduce diversity or introduce artifacts.

## 5) ControlNet and Diffusion Transformers (DiT)

Two modern extensions are worth knowing:

- **ControlNet**: add extra trainable branches so you can condition on spatial signals (edges, pose, depth) while keeping a strong pretrained denoiser mostly intact.

![ControlNet adds trainable branches to a frozen U-Net backbone. (Zhang et al., 2023; adapted from Weng)](/api/wiki-images/diffusion-models/images/lilianweng-posts-2021-07-11-diffusion-models_018.png)

- **Diffusion Transformer (DiT)**: replace the U-Net with a Transformer that operates on patches/tokens, scaling nicely with model size and data.

![DiT processes latent patches through transformer blocks with adaLN conditioning. (Peebles & Xie, 2023; adapted from Weng)](/api/wiki-images/diffusion-models/images/lilianweng-posts-2021-07-11-diffusion-models_019.png)

## Summary

Diffusion models generate images by learning to reverse a gradual noising process: training reduces to predicting the added noise at random timesteps. Latent diffusion makes this efficient enough for high-res generation, while cross-attention, classifier-free guidance, and add-ons like ControlNet enable strong text and structure control.

## Recommended Reading
- [What are Diffusion Models?](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2021-07-11-diffusion-models/) — Clear end-to-end explanation of DDPMs, schedules, objectives, and modern extensions.
- [The Annotated Diffusion Model](https://huggingface.co/blog/annotated-diffusion) — Step-by-step DDPM implementation with practical details.
- [Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2006.11239) — The original DDPM paper.
- [Score-Based Generative Modeling through Stochastic Differential Equations (blog)](https://yang-song.net/blog/2021/score/) — Intuition for the “score” view and Langevin-style sampling.
lessons.summary: Diffusion models learn to generate images by reversing a gradual Gaussian noising process, reducing training to predicting noise at random timesteps. Latent diffusion plus text conditioning (cross-attention) and guidance techniques power modern systems like Stable Diffusion, with extensions like ControlNet and DiT improving control and scalability.
lessons.video_title: DDPM - Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models | Paper Explained

image_metadata.caption: DiT processes latent patches through transformer blocks with adaLN conditioning. (Peebles & Xie, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining U-Net-based diffusion models, when contrasting transformer-based alternatives, to help students understand the architectural differences and the scalability motivation for DiT.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Diffusion Transformer (DiT) showing how noisy latent patches are tokenized and processed through transformer blocks with adaptive layer norm (adaLN) for timestep and class conditioning, followed by a linear decoder to predict noise or output patches.

image_metadata.caption: DDPM paper by Ho et al. (2020), the basis for this annotated implementation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on diffusion models to establish the academic source and motivate why this specific paper is being annotated
image_metadata.description: The DDPM (Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models) paper title/cover page, showing the original paper by Ho et al. that introduced the DDPM framework

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of generative model families, including diffusion models. (Weng, 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a diffusion models lesson to establish prior knowledge and motivate why a new class of models is needed.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram showing the landscape of generative models, branching into categories including GANs, VAEs, Flow-based models, and Diffusion models, with their key characteristics and relationships illustrated.

image_metadata.caption: Forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) Markov chain in DDPM. (Ho et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the DDPM framework, before presenting the mathematical formulation of q(x_t|x_{t-1}) and p_theta(x_{t-1}|x_t).
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the Markov chain for the forward and reverse diffusion process: left-to-right shows progressive noise addition from x0 to xT (forward), right-to-left shows iterative denoising from xT back to x0 (reverse), with annotated transition distributions q and p_theta.

image_metadata.caption: DDIM achieves lower FID than DDPM with fewer sampling steps. (Song et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing DDIM to DDPM to motivate why deterministic implicit sampling is preferred for fast generation, especially when compute is limited.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing FID scores on CIFAR10 and CelebA datasets as a function of the number of sampling steps, comparing DDIM (eta=0, cyan) and DDPM (sigma-hat, orange), demonstrating DDIM's superior sample quality at fewer steps.

image_metadata.caption: ControlNet adds trainable zero-convolution branches to frozen U-Net encoder layers. (Zhang et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining ControlNet after introducing U-Net, to illustrate how the architecture is modified to accept spatial conditioning inputs while preserving pretrained weights.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of ControlNet showing the original locked U-Net on one side and a trainable copy of the encoder connected via zero-convolution layers, illustrating how external conditioning signals (e.g., edge maps, depth maps) are injected into the U-Net encoder layers through the sandwiched zero-convolution mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) processes in a diffusion model. (Ho et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the two-process framework, before presenting any equations, so students have an intuitive visual anchor for q(x_t|x_{t-1}) and p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t)
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the forward (noising) and reverse (denoising) diffusion processes, showing a sequence of images transitioning from a clean image x0 through progressively noisier states x1...xT and back, with arrows labeled for the forward process q and reverse process p

image_metadata.caption: Training algorithm: predict noise ε at random time step t, minimize MSE. (Ho et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after deriving the simplified MSE objective, to bridge theory and implementation; use it to walk through each line of the training loop before showing actual code
image_metadata.description: A pseudocode block showing the training algorithm for a diffusion model: sample a real image x0, sample a random time step t, sample Gaussian noise ε, compute the noisy image x_t, and minimize MSE between true noise ε and predicted noise ε_θ(x_t, t)

image_metadata.caption: U-Net architecture with skip connections used as the diffusion model backbone. (Ronneberger et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the neural network component of the diffusion model, after explaining the autoencoder analogy, to ground the architectural choice in a visual before discussing attention and time embeddings
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the U-Net architecture showing the encoder (downsampling) path on the left, bottleneck in the middle, and decoder (upsampling) path on the right, with skip connections between corresponding encoder and decoder layers

image_metadata.caption: Sample cat images (x₀) from the data distribution before any noise is added.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the forward diffusion process and image preprocessing steps, to give students a concrete sense of the starting point before demonstrating how noise is progressively added
image_metadata.description: A grid of cat images from ImageNet used as the real data distribution x0, showing the clean, uncorrupted input images before any noise is applied in the forward diffusion process

image_metadata.caption: Cat image after applying forward diffusion at a single time step t.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after presenting the forward diffusion function implementation, as a sanity-check example before visualizing multiple time steps
image_metadata.description: A single cat image shown after applying the forward diffusion process at a specific time step t, demonstrating the noisy image x_t at one particular noise level as a verification step

image_metadata.caption: Forward diffusion: cat image progressively corrupted by Gaussian noise over T time steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the single time-step verification, when explaining the noise schedule and cumulative variance ᾱ_t, to illustrate how different β schedules affect the rate of information destruction
image_metadata.description: A sequence of cat images showing the same image at progressively increasing noise levels across multiple time steps (e.g., t=0, t=50, t=100, ..., t=T), illustrating how the forward diffusion process gradually destroys image structure

image_metadata.caption: Noisy images at various time steps, motivating the noise-prediction training objective.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show just before introducing the loss function, to motivate why predicting the noise ε is the training objective — students can see what the network must 'undo'
image_metadata.description: Another visualization of multiple noisy cat images at different time steps, likely showing a different noise schedule or a different set of time step samples compared to the previous figure, used to illustrate the forward process before defining the loss function

image_metadata.caption: Sampling algorithm: iteratively denoise from pure noise x_T to generate image x_0. (Ho et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from training to inference, to explain how the trained model is used to generate new samples; contrast with the training algorithm to highlight the differences
image_metadata.description: A pseudocode block showing the sampling (inference) algorithm for a diffusion model: start from pure Gaussian noise x_T, then iteratively apply the learned reverse process p_θ(x_{t-1}|x_t) for t=T down to t=1, adding noise at each step except the last

image_metadata.caption: T-shirt generated by the trained diffusion model on 28×28 Fashion MNIST dataset.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the end of the tutorial as the culminating result, after explaining training and sampling; use it to discuss limitations (low resolution) and potential improvements
image_metadata.description: A generated Fashion MNIST image (likely a T-shirt) produced by the trained diffusion model, showing the final output of the reverse diffusion process after training on the 28x28 Fashion MNIST dataset

image_metadata.caption: Diffusion model learning to reverse noise on 2D swiss roll data. (Sohl-Dickstein et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the reverse diffusion process to give students a concrete geometric intuition before tackling high-dimensional image examples.
image_metadata.description: A visualization of a diffusion model applied to 2D swiss roll data, showing the gradual transformation of the data distribution through forward diffusion steps and the learned reverse process reconstructing the original swiss roll shape.

image_metadata.caption: DDPM training and sampling algorithms in pseudocode. (Ho et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after deriving the simplified ELBO/loss objective, to demonstrate how the math translates into a practical training loop and sampling procedure.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side pseudocode algorithms for DDPM training (Algorithm 1) and DDPM sampling (Algorithm 2), detailing the exact steps including noise prediction, loss computation, and iterative denoising with the learned model.

image_metadata.caption: Linear vs. cosine beta schedules during diffusion training. (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing improvements to DDPM, specifically when explaining why a cosine schedule outperforms a linear one for model log-likelihood.
image_metadata.description: A plot comparing linear beta scheduling (Ho et al., 2020) versus cosine-based beta scheduling (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021) over training timesteps, showing how the cosine schedule maintains more signal at the ends of the diffusion trajectory.

image_metadata.caption: NLL comparison of improved DDPM vs. other generative models. (Nichol & Dhariwal, 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the improved DDPM techniques (learned variance, cosine schedule) to validate their effectiveness with quantitative results.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing negative log-likelihood (NLL) in bits/dim across multiple likelihood-based generative models including improved DDPM, original DDPM, and other baselines on standard image benchmarks.

image_metadata.caption: Classifier-guided sampling algorithms for DDPM and DDIM. (Dhariwal & Nichol, 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing conditional generation with diffusion models, after explaining unconditional DDPM/DDIM, to contrast guided vs. unguided sampling.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode algorithms for classifier-guided diffusion sampling with both DDPM and DDIM, showing how gradient signals from a separately trained classifier are incorporated into the reverse diffusion update step to condition generation.

image_metadata.caption: Progressive distillation halves sampling steps each iteration. (Salimans & Ho, 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing distillation-based methods for faster diffusion sampling, after students understand DDIM, to explain how step count can be halved iteratively.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the progressive distillation process, showing how a teacher model using N steps is distilled into a student model using N/2 steps iteratively, halving the required sampling steps each round.

image_metadata.caption: Standard training vs. progressive distillation algorithms, changes highlighted in green. (Salimans & Ho, 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the progressive distillation diagram to give students the algorithmic details, especially useful for students who want to implement or reproduce the method.
