image_metadata.description: Side-by-side pseudocode comparison of Algorithm 1 (standard diffusion model training) and Algorithm 2 (progressive distillation training), with green highlights marking the specific changes introduced in the distillation procedure.

image_metadata.caption: Consistency models map any trajectory point directly to its clean-data origin. (Song et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing consistency models, immediately after defining the consistency function f: (x_t, t) → x_epsilon, to help students visualize why the self-consistency constraint enables fast sampling.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a diffusion trajectory from clean data x_epsilon to noisy data x_T, with arrows illustrating how the consistency function f maps any point on the trajectory back to the origin x_epsilon, demonstrating the self-consistency property.

image_metadata.caption: Ablation results for consistency models: LPIPS + Heun solver + N=18 is optimal. (Song et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining both CD and CT training objectives, when discussing practical implementation choices and hyperparameter sensitivity in consistency models.
image_metadata.description: A table or chart comparing consistency model performance across different configurations, varying distance metrics (LPIPS vs others), ODE solvers (Heun vs others), and number of discretization steps N, showing FID or similar quality scores for both consistency distillation (CD) and consistency training (CT).

image_metadata.caption: Rate-distortion tradeoff showing perceptual vs. semantic compression stages. (Rombach & Blattmann et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the LDM section, before describing the autoencoder architecture, to motivate why operating in latent space is a principled design choice rather than just a computational shortcut.
image_metadata.description: A rate-distortion curve plotting compression rate (bits per dimension) on the x-axis against distortion on the y-axis, with annotated regions showing where perceptual compression and semantic compression occur, illustrating that most image bits encode perceptual details rather than semantic content.

image_metadata.caption: LDM architecture: encoder compresses to latent, U-Net denoises, decoder reconstructs. (Rombach & Blattmann et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the full LDM pipeline, after motivating latent-space diffusion with the rate-distortion plot, to give students a concrete architectural reference.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Latent Diffusion Model (LDM) showing: an encoder E compressing image x to latent z, a U-Net denoising model operating on z with cross-attention for conditioning inputs (text, semantic maps, etc.), and a decoder D reconstructing the image from the denoised latent.

image_metadata.caption: Cascaded diffusion pipeline generates high-resolution images through sequential upsampling stages. (Ho et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing cascaded diffusion models as a strategy for scaling up generation resolution, to contrast with single-model approaches and motivate the need for conditioning augmentation between stages.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram showing multiple diffusion models arranged in sequence at increasing image resolutions (e.g., 64x64 → 256x256 → 1024x1024), where each model upsamples and refines the output of the previous model, illustrating the cascaded diffusion approach.

image_metadata.caption: unCLIP architecture: CLIP prior maps text to image embeddings for guided diffusion. (Ramesh et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining unCLIP/DALL-E 2, after introducing CLIP, to help students trace how a text prompt flows through the system to produce a final image.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of unCLIP (DALL-E 2) showing the two-stage pipeline: a prior network that maps CLIP text embeddings to CLIP image embeddings, followed by a diffusion decoder that generates images conditioned on the image embedding, with CLIP encoders shown for both text and image inputs.

image_metadata.caption: U-Net encoder-decoder with skip connections; backbone of most diffusion model denoisers. (Ronneberger, 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the model architecture section, before discussing modifications like cross-attention or ControlNet, as the baseline architecture that students need to understand first.
image_metadata.description: The original U-Net architecture diagram showing a symmetric encoder-decoder structure with downsampling (left) and upsampling (right) paths, skip connections (gray arrows) between corresponding encoder and decoder feature maps, with channel counts labeled on top and spatial dimensions on the left.

image_metadata.caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus regions vs. low-res periphery.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of an attention lesson to build intuition before introducing any equations — ask students 'what are you looking at first and why?'
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit with colored bounding boxes highlighting different regions — a yellow box on a pointy ear and red boxes on other facial features — illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Words attend to other words with varying strength in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, before introducing seq2seq models, to extend the analogy from images to language.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weights visualized between words, showing how one word (e.g., 'eating') attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths indicated by shading or line thickness.

image_metadata.caption: Seq2seq encoder-decoder: all source info compressed into one vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the limitations of vanilla seq2seq before attention — ask students 'what happens to early words by the time the decoder finishes?'
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder (seq2seq) architecture unrolled in time, showing RNN encoder processing 'she is eating a green apple' into a fixed-length context vector, which the RNN decoder uses to generate the Chinese translation character by character.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck problem; walk through each component step-by-step — encoder states, score function, softmax, weighted sum, decoder input.
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention mechanism within an encoder-decoder model, showing bidirectional RNN encoder hidden states, alignment score computation, softmax over attention weights, weighted sum to form context vector, and decoder RNN receiving the context vector at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the attention weight formula to demonstrate what trained attention weights look like in practice — ask students to identify reordering patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; cell brightness indicates attention weight, revealing which source words the model attends to when generating each target word.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to context words (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention as distinct from encoder-decoder attention — contrast with the sentence-level attention example to highlight that source and target are the same sequence.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and surrounding words are shaded in blue with varying intensity proportional to their attention activation level, showing intra-sentence dependencies.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention over image regions for caption generation. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs. hard attention, or when demonstrating attention in computer vision tasks — ask students which image regions they expect the model to attend to for each word.
image_metadata.description: Image captioning example showing a photo of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with attention weight overlays highlighting which image regions the model focuses on when generating specific caption words, demonstrating soft attention over CNN feature maps.

image_metadata.caption: Global vs. local attention: full sequence vs. windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the Luong et al. attention variants after Bahdanau — use the side-by-side layout to directly compare scope of attention at each decoding step.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (left, attending to all source positions) and local attention (right, attending to a window of source positions around a predicted alignment point) in Luong et al.'s model.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending attention concepts beyond NLP to memory-augmented networks — ask students how the attention vector over memory rows parallels attention over encoder hidden states.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine (NTM) showing the controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing mechanisms (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting distribution s_t.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically after introducing the concept of convolutional shift and before discussing sharpening.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over shift positions and a direct parametric distribution.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content, interpolation, shift, and sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a summary diagram after explaining each individual NTM addressing step, or as an overview before diving into each component.
image_metadata.description: Full flow diagram of the Neural Turing Machine addressing mechanism, showing the sequential steps: content-based addressing, interpolation with previous weights, location-based shift convolution, and sharpening to produce final attention weights w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: decoder attention directly selects input positions. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing Pointer Networks as a solution to problems where output elements are positions in the input (e.g., sorting, TSP), contrasting with standard seq2seq attention.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence and a decoder RNN using attention over encoder hidden states to output pointers (indices) back into the input sequence rather than fixed vocabulary tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer's core attention mechanism; show left panel first for single-head attention, then right panel to explain multi-head extension.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and scaling; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residuals. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder architecture; use to walk through each sub-layer and the role of residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder with masked self-attention and cross-attention layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the encoder; highlight the masked attention layer and cross-attention to encoder, contrasting with the encoder's structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding, masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer normalization, and final linear+softmax output.

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or as an overview at the start of a Transformer lesson to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer for sequential/RL tasks and how SNAIL addresses them by combining convolution and attention.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for selective retrieval), used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution accesses local regions; self-attention accesses the entire image.
image_metadata.when_to_show: When motivating the use of self-attention in vision models (SAGAN); use to contrast local convolutional receptive fields with global attention reach.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local region) versus self-attention (entire image/sequence), illustrating the difference in context access.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: Q/K/V via 1x1 convolutions over image features. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching how attention mechanisms transfer from NLP to computer vision; use to show the direct analogy between Transformer attention and SAGAN's spatial attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing image feature maps branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f, g, h corresponding to query, key, value), attention map computation via softmax, and weighted sum to produce self-attention feature maps added back to input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention-enabled global coherence. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h) to give students a concrete visual payoff — use it to ask 'which parts of the image do you think the attention map focuses on for a dog's eye?'
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 generated images from the Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) model, showing diverse object classes such as dogs, birds, and other animals with fine-grained details and coherent global structure.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer internals: encoder stack and decoder stack with connections. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the Transformer as a black box, to transition students from the abstract input/output view to the internal component structure.
image_metadata.description: High-level diagram of the Transformer architecture showing the encoding component (stack of encoders) and decoding component (stack of decoders) with connections between them, resembling an 'opened up' black box view.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer as a black box: input sentence in, translated sentence out. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a Transformer introduction to motivate the architecture with a real-world task before revealing internal components.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Transformer as a black box for machine translation, with a source-language sentence as input and a target-language translation as output, labeled with 'Encoders' and 'Decoders' blocks.

image_metadata.caption: Six stacked encoders (left) and six stacked decoders (right) in the Transformer. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining that the Transformer uses multiple identical encoder and decoder layers, to reinforce the stacking concept and the number six from the original paper.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the stacked encoder and decoder architecture, showing six encoder layers stacked vertically on the left and six decoder layers stacked vertically on the right, with arrows indicating data flow between them.

image_metadata.caption: Single encoder: self-attention layer feeds into a feed-forward network. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the internal structure of a single encoder, before explaining self-attention or feed-forward networks in detail.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer encoder block showing two sub-layers: a Self-Attention layer at the bottom and a Feed-Forward Neural Network layer on top, with arrows showing data flow upward through both layers.

image_metadata.caption: Single decoder: self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting the decoder structure with the encoder, specifically to introduce the cross-attention mechanism that connects encoder and decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single Transformer decoder block showing three sub-layers: Self-Attention, Encoder-Decoder Attention (cross-attention), and a Feed-Forward Neural Network, with arrows indicating data flow including input from the encoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: Each input word is converted to a 512-dimensional embedding vector. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how raw text is prepared for the Transformer, before explaining how vectors flow through encoder layers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing word embedding conversion: input words (e.g., 'You', 'are', 'welcome') are each converted into 512-dimensional embedding vectors represented as colored bars, which then enter the bottom encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Each word flows its own path through the encoder; self-attention creates cross-path dependencies. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how multiple tokens are processed simultaneously in the encoder, and to set up the distinction between self-attention (has cross-position dependencies) and feed-forward (fully parallel).
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors (one per input word) flowing in parallel through the self-attention layer and then the feed-forward layer of a single encoder, with separate paths for each word position.

image_metadata.caption: A short example sentence entering the encoder, preparing for self-attention walkthrough. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show just before diving into the step-by-step self-attention calculation, to establish the example sentence that will be used throughout the detailed walkthrough.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a short example sentence being processed through the encoder, with vectors for each word position flowing through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers, used to set up the detailed self-attention explanation.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: 'it' attends strongly to 'animal' to resolve coreference. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention at a high level, before the mathematical details, to give students an intuitive understanding of what the mechanism achieves.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in action: the word 'it' in a sentence is highlighted, with attention lines connecting it to other words (especially 'animal'), showing which words the model attends to when encoding 'it'.

image_metadata.caption: Each word embedding is projected into Query, Key, and Value vectors (dim=64). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the detailed self-attention explanation, as the first step in the Q/K/V computation walkthrough, before showing how scores are calculated.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the creation of Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors for each input word embedding by multiplying with three learned weight matrices (WQ, WK, WV), showing the dimensional reduction from 512 to 64.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the scoring step of self-attention, before discussing scaling or softmax, to ground the dot-product formula in a concrete word-level example.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Scaling by √d_k and softmax normalization of attention scores. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the dot-product scoring diagram, when explaining why scores are scaled and how softmax produces a valid probability distribution over positions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the self-attention score computation to show division by √64 (scaling step), followed by a softmax operation that normalizes scores into a probability distribution, and then multiplication of each value vector by its softmax weight.

image_metadata.caption: Weighted sum of value vectors produces self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram to complete the full self-attention pipeline, emphasizing how the final output vector encodes context from the entire sequence.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final steps of self-attention: weighted value vectors (each value vector multiplied by its softmax score) are summed to produce the output vector z1 for the first word position.

image_metadata.caption: Input matrix X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V to form Q, K, V. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the step-by-step word-level diagrams, when transitioning to the practical matrix implementation to explain how transformers achieve computational efficiency.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-form diagram showing input embedding matrix X being multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all positions at once.

image_metadata.caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Q/K/V matrix construction diagram as the culminating summary, and revisit when students encounter the formula in the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper.
image_metadata.description: Single condensed formula diagram showing the complete self-attention matrix calculation: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, combining all six steps into one matrix expression.

image_metadata.caption: Eight parallel attention heads each produce a separate Z matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-headed attention, after students understand single-head self-attention, to illustrate why eight separate computations are performed and what they each produce.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating multi-headed attention by showing the same input being processed through eight parallel sets of Q/K/V weight matrices, each producing a separate Z matrix (Z0 through Z7).

image_metadata.caption: Eight attention head outputs Z0–Z7 must be condensed for the next layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head QKV diagram to pose the 'how do we combine these?' problem before revealing the W^O projection solution.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing eight separate Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) from eight attention heads, highlighting the challenge of having multiple output matrices when the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Concatenated attention heads projected by W^O into single output matrix. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the eight Z matrices problem, as the resolution step, and emphasize that W^O is also a learned parameter trained end-to-end.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the concatenation of all eight Z matrices followed by multiplication by weight matrix W^O to produce a single combined output matrix, completing the multi-headed attention mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Complete multi-headed self-attention pipeline from input to output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a consolidation diagram after explaining each individual step, or use as a reference when students need to review the complete multi-head attention mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-headed self-attention pipeline in one view: input embeddings → multiple Q/K/V projections → parallel attention computations → concatenation → W^O projection → final output.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads reveal which words 'it' attends to during encoding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-headed attention mechanics to demonstrate real-world utility — use it to motivate why multiple heads are beneficial by showing they capture different linguistic phenomena.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing which tokens different attention heads focus on when encoding the word 'it' in the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to other words.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the complexity of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about how different heads specialize.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, making the combined picture cluttered and hard to interpret.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use this before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap to build intuition with a simple 4D example.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder. Each word's embedding is shown as a colored vector, with a corresponding positional encoding vector added to it, illustrated with a 4-dimensional example.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after explaining that positional encodings are added to embeddings; use as a stepping stone before the large 512-dimension heatmap.
image_metadata.description: Small heatmap visualization of positional encoding values for a short sequence with 4-dimensional embeddings. Each row represents a word position and each column a dimension, color-coded to show values between -1 and 1.

image_metadata.caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap — sinusoidal patterns visible. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss how the sinusoidal structure allows generalization to unseen sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Large heatmap showing positional encodings for many sequence positions (rows) across 512 dimensions (columns). Color-coded from -1 to 1, revealing sinusoidal wave patterns that vary in frequency across dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Original paper's interleaved sine/cosine positional encoding heatmap. (Vaswani et al., via Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the original paper's positional encoding formula; use alongside the Tensor2Tensor version to highlight that multiple valid implementations exist.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap of positional encodings from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sine/cosine encoding pattern across positions and dimensions, slightly different from the Tensor2Tensor implementation.

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection and layer normalization in a Transformer encoder layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining encoder sub-layer structure; use to show why residual connections matter for training deep networks and how they are implemented in the Transformer.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection and layer normalization step. The input vector is shown bypassing the self-attention sub-layer and being added back to the sub-layer's output before layer norm is applied.

image_metadata.caption: 2-layer Transformer stack with residual connections and layer norm shown. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual encoder residuals; use to generalize the pattern to the full encoder-decoder stack and reinforce the modular, repeated structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, each sub-layer showing residual connections and layer normalization. Arrows show data flow through the stack, illustrating how both encoder and decoder stacks apply Add & Norm.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students need a detailed view of the encoder sub-layer; use to trace the exact path of a vector through self-attention, addition, and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Detailed diagram of a single encoder sub-layer showing the self-attention mechanism with residual connection and layer normalization. Input vectors flow into self-attention, the output is added to the original input (residual), then layer norm is applied.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder generating the first output token autoregressively. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the decoder side; use this animation first to show the overall decoding process before explaining masked self-attention and encoder-decoder attention in detail.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer. The encoder processes the full input sequence, then the decoder begins generating output one token at a time, with the first output token being produced.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder generating output tokens step-by-step using encoder K/V. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use to show the full multi-step generation process and explain how encoder outputs are reused and why decoder self-attention must be masked.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, with the decoder generating successive output tokens. Illustrates how each new token is fed back as input to the decoder and how attention vectors K and V from the encoder are reused at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Linear layer produces logits; softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer generates output tokens — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to actual word predictions.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.

image_metadata.caption: One-hot encoding represents each vocabulary word as a sparse vector. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, before discussing loss functions or comparing model outputs to target labels.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 in the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.

image_metadata.caption: Six-word vocabulary one-hot target for translating 'merci' → 'thanks'. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the training objective — specifically when illustrating what the 'correct answer' looks like in vector form before introducing the loss function.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active (1) for a specific word, used as the target label when training to translate 'merci' into 'thanks'.

image_metadata.caption: Model output vs. target distribution: loss measures their difference. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why cross-entropy is needed and what 'minimizing loss' means geometrically.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (spread across vocabulary words) versus the target one-hot label distribution (all probability on 'thanks') for the single-word translation example 'merci' → 'thanks', illustrating the discrepancy the loss function must minimize.

image_metadata.caption: Target probability distributions across all decoding steps for a sentence. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the single-word loss example to generalize the concept to multi-step sequence generation and explain how loss is accumulated across all output positions.
image_metadata.description: Multiple probability distribution panels showing what the model should output at each decoding step for the translation 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', with each panel representing one time step and the target word having high probability.

image_metadata.caption: Trained model produces near-certain distributions on correct output words. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after discussing the loss function and training process, as a 'goal state' visualization, and use it to introduce greedy vs. beam search decoding strategies.
image_metadata.description: Probability distribution panels showing what a well-trained Transformer model actually produces at each decoding step for 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', with near-certain probability mass on the correct word at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention over words: dot(Q,K) → weights → weighted sum of V (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the attention equation to map each term (Q, K, V, scores, weights, output) to a visual step-by-step pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A word-sequence attention illustration: one query vector is compared (via dot products) against key vectors for each word to produce attention weights (softmax implied), which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding an attended output representation.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled dot-product attention computation graph (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students move from the intuitive attention idea to the efficient matrix-form implementation, especially before coding QKᵀ/softmax/V steps.
image_metadata.description: Block diagram of scaled dot-product attention: inputs Q, K, V flow through a matrix multiplication QKᵀ, scaling by 1/sqrt(dk), optional masking, softmax to obtain attention probabilities, and a final multiplication by V to produce the attention output.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads then concat and project (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention, to motivate multiple heads and to explain tensor shapes (split into heads, then concat).
image_metadata.description: Multi-head attention schematic: Q, K, V are linearly projected into multiple heads (separate subspaces), each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently, the head outputs are concatenated, and a final linear projection produces the layer output.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder block with self-attention and FFN (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from attention as an operation to the full Transformer layer/stack, or when debugging why residuals/norm are needed for stable training.
image_metadata.description: Transformer encoder architecture diagram: token embeddings combined with positional encodings feed stacked encoder blocks containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, each wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding patterns across dimensions (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after presenting the PE(pos,i) formula to connect the equation to an interpretable signal pattern across dimensions.
image_metadata.description: Plot illustrating sinusoidal positional encoding across positions and hidden dimensions, showing alternating sine/cosine components with different wavelengths depending on the dimension index.

image_metadata.caption: Heatmap of sinusoidal positional encodings over position and dimension (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why different dimensions use different wavelengths and how nearby dimensions relate (e.g., dim 1 vs 2 phase shift, dim 2 vs 3 longer period).
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of positional encodings with position on one axis and hidden dimension on the other; color indicates the added value per (pos, dim), revealing phase shifts and increasing wavelengths across dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer learning-rate warm-up followed by decay (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students encounter unstable early training or are configuring an optimizer; use it to justify warm-up steps and how to choose warm-up length.
image_metadata.description: Learning-rate schedule plot for Transformer warm-up: learning rate increases during an initial warm-up phase and then decays afterward according to a predefined schedule.

image_metadata.caption: Attention maps by layer and head reveal learned token alignment (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after training a toy task to validate whether the model learned the desired mapping; use it to discuss head diversity and layer-wise refinement.
image_metadata.description: Grid of attention maps for a sequence-to-sequence task: rows correspond to Transformer layers and columns to attention heads; each subplot is a SeqLen×SeqLen matrix showing where each token attends, with strong mass near the flipped-index target and some spread to nearby positions.

image_metadata.caption: CIFAR-100 set with one anomalous image among same-class images (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing the dataset sampling procedure or model, to ensure students understand the task definition and what the model must detect.
image_metadata.description: Example set for anomaly detection built from CIFAR-100 images: a small collection of images where most belong to the same class and one image is from a different class (the anomaly), illustrating the unordered-set input format.

image_metadata.caption: Attention maps by layer/head highlighting the outlier item (UvA Deep Learning Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: After students understand self-attention math, show this to practice reading attention matrices and to ask: which heads attend to the suspected outlier and why might different layers specialize differently?
image_metadata.description: Grid of attention heatmaps from a Transformer used for set anomaly detection, organized by layer and head (e.g., Layer 1–4, Head 1–4). Each heatmap shows attention probabilities between the 10 set elements (9 similar images and one outlier), with several heads placing strong attention on the last element (the anomaly candidate).

image_metadata.caption: Layer/head attention patterns: outlier-focused vs. averaging heads (UvA Deep Learning Course).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing interpretability or debugging: have learners compare heads/layers, identify averaging vs. outlier-focused heads, and predict when the model might fail if attention does not isolate the anomaly.
image_metadata.description: Second grid of Transformer attention heatmaps for the same set anomaly detection task, showing contrasting head behaviors: some heads strongly attend to the last image (outlier), while later-layer heads assign very low attention to it; other heads distribute attention more evenly, resembling a weighted average over all set elements.

lessons.title: Text-to-Video & Beyond
lessons.content:
# Text-to-Video & Beyond

An image model learns *what* things look like. A video model has to learn *what stays the same while things change*.

That extra constraint—**temporal consistency**—is why text-to-video feels like a qualitative jump. It’s not just “more pixels.” It’s a model that must keep identities, lighting, and geometry stable while motion unfolds.

## From text-to-image to text-to-video: what actually changes?

Most modern text-to-video systems start from the diffusion recipe you already know: begin with noise, iteratively denoise, and condition on text.

The twist is the data tensor. Instead of an image `H×W×C`, you now denoise a **video volume** `T×H×W×C`.

That creates two immediate problems:

1. **Compute explodes**: attention over space-time is much larger than attention over space.
2. **Flicker is the default**: if each frame is “free to improvise,” you get a slideshow.

So video diffusion models add **temporal attention** (and/or temporal convolutions) to let information flow across frames: “this pixel region is the same character as last frame; keep it consistent, just move it.”

![Space-time separable block combining spatial and temporal attention layers (Source: Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/text-to-video/images/lilianweng-posts-2024-04-12-diffusion-video_004.png)

A common engineering trick is to *factor* the problem: do strong spatial modeling (like image models) and add just enough temporal machinery to coordinate motion.

## Scaling up: patches in space-time (Sora-style intuition)

A different design choice is: stop thinking in “frames,” and think in **spacetime patches**.

Compress the video into a latent (via a video VAE), chop it into patch tokens, and run a transformer over those tokens—very similar to how LLMs run over text tokens. This makes it easier to train on mixed data: images (1-frame videos) and videos of many shapes and lengths.

![Sora's Diffusion Transformer processes video as spacetime patch tokens (Source: Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/text-to-video/images/lilianweng-posts-2024-04-12-diffusion-video_005.png)

The big conceptual shift: the model isn’t “generating frame 17.” It’s denoising a *chunk of spacetime* while keeping global coherence.

## A tiny mental model: why temporal attention reduces flicker

Here’s the simplest way to picture it:

- Without temporal links, the model can satisfy the prompt independently per frame.
- With temporal attention, each denoising step can *copy, align, and update* features across time.

Pseudo-code sketch (not real implementation):

```python
# x: [T, H, W, C] noisy latent video
# text: prompt embedding

for step in diffusion_steps:
    h = spatial_unet(x, cond=text)          # per-frame spatial reasoning
    h = temporal_attention(h)               # cross-frame identity/motion binding
    x = denoise_update(x, h, step)
```

That `temporal_attention(h)` is where “the same dog” becomes a persistent object instead of a re-sampled dog every frame.

## Beyond video: 3D generation as “consistency across viewpoints”

Video consistency is about time. **3D consistency** is about camera motion.

### NeRF: a scene as a function you can query

A **NeRF** represents a scene as a continuous function: give it a 3D point and a viewing direction, and it returns color + density. Render an image by shooting rays through space and integrating what the function says along each ray.

This is beautiful—and slow—because you’re doing lots of neural queries per pixel.

### 3D Gaussian Splatting: make the scene out of millions of tiny “sprays”

**3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)** keeps the “continuous radiance field” feel, but swaps the representation: instead of a neural network queried along rays, you store the scene as many explicit **3D Gaussians** (position, covariance/shape, color, opacity) and render them with a fast, differentiable rasterizer.

![A single rasterized Gaussian with border — the core rendering primitive (Source: Hugging Face)](/api/wiki-images/3d-gaussian-splatting/images/huggingface-co-blog-gaussian-splatting_002.png)

Scale that up to millions of Gaussians and you get photoreal scenes with real-time-ish rendering.

![7 million fully opaque Gaussians reconstruct a photorealistic bicycle scene (Source: Hugging Face)](/api/wiki-images/3d-gaussian-splatting/images/huggingface-co-blog-gaussian-splatting_004.png)

The unifying idea: whether it’s time (video) or viewpoint (3D), the model is being punished for inconsistency.

## World models: when video generation becomes “simulation”

A **world model** is a generative model that learns a compressed representation of an environment’s dynamics—so you can *predict* what happens next, or even train an agent inside the model’s “dream.”

Text-to-video models start to look world-model-ish when they can:

- maintain objects over long horizons,
- respect rough physics and causality,
- and stay coherent under camera motion.

They’re not perfect simulators—but the direction is clear: better temporal consistency → better implicit understanding of how the world evolves.

## Summary

Text-to-video extends diffusion by adding mechanisms (especially temporal attention) that bind frames into a coherent sequence rather than independent images. The same “consistency pressure” shows up in 3D (across viewpoints) via NeRFs and 3D Gaussian Splatting, and it points toward world models: generative systems that don’t just render, but approximate dynamics.

## Recommended Reading

- [Diffusion Models for Video Generation](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2024-04-12-diffusion-video/) — Clear tour of video diffusion design choices and temporal consistency.
- [Video generation models as world simulators](https://openai.com/research/video-generation-models-as-world-simulators) — Patch-based spacetime tokenization and scaling intuition.
- [NeRF: Representing Scenes as Neural Radiance Fields for View Synthesis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.08934) — The core “scene as a function” idea.
- [Introduction to 3D Gaussian Splatting](https://huggingface.co/blog/gaussian-splatting) — Practical, visual explanation of Gaussians + differentiable rasterization.
lessons.summary: Text-to-video models add temporal attention and space-time structure to diffusion so frames stay consistent while motion evolves. Extending the same idea to viewpoint consistency yields 3D methods like NeRF and 3D Gaussian Splatting, and together they hint at world models that approximate environment dynamics.
lessons.video_title: 3D Gaussian Splatting for Real-Time Radiance Field Rendering (Paper Explained)

image_metadata.caption: 7 million fully opaque Gaussians reconstruct a photorealistic bicycle scene. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when motivating the technique's quality and scale, after explaining individual Gaussians, to illustrate what millions of Gaussians can reconstruct.
image_metadata.description: A photorealistic rendered image of a bicycle scene reconstructed using approximately 7 million fully opaque Gaussian splats, showing fine detail and scene complexity.

image_metadata.caption: Space-time separable block combining spatial and temporal attention layers (Ho et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Imagen Video pipeline overview, when explaining how each diffusion model in the cascade processes video data internally.
image_metadata.description: A detailed block diagram of a single space-time separable block in Imagen Video. Shows the internal structure with 2D spatial convolution, temporal convolution, spatial attention, and temporal attention sub-modules arranged in sequence, with residual connections.

image_metadata.caption: Sora's Diffusion Transformer processes video as spacetime patch tokens (Brooks et al., 2024)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from U-Net-based video diffusion models to transformer-based approaches, to highlight the architectural contrast and motivate why Sora chose DiT.
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of Sora showing its Diffusion Transformer (DiT) design. Illustrates how video and image inputs are compressed into a latent space, tokenized into spacetime patches, processed by transformer blocks with conditioning on text embeddings, and decoded back to pixel space.

image_metadata.caption: STUNet inflates a text-to-image U-Net with temporal convolution and attention. (Bar-Tal et al. 2024)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how pretrained image diffusion U-Nets are extended to video via temporal layer insertion — ideal for comparing with Video LDM's approach and explaining factorized convolution.
image_metadata.description: Three-panel architecture diagram of Lumiere's Space-Time U-Net (STUNet): (a) the full U-Net with spatial and temporal downsampling/upsampling paths, (b) a convolution-based block with factorized space-time convolution appended to pretrained layers, and (c) an attention-based block with temporal attention appended at the coarsest level.

image_metadata.caption: Text2Video-Zero generates videos from a frozen image diffusion model. (Khachatryan et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing training-free adaptation methods, especially to contrast with fine-tuning approaches and to explain how motion information can be injected into latent codes without retraining.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline overview of Text2Video-Zero showing how a pretrained text-to-image diffusion model generates temporally consistent video frames without any training, using motion-enriched latent initialization and cross-frame attention replacing standard self-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Triangle rasterization: the classical primitive analogous to Gaussian splatting. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing Gaussian splatting, when establishing the analogy between triangle rasterization and Gaussian rasterization.
image_metadata.description: A single rasterized triangle on screen, illustrating the concept of triangle rasterization in traditional computer graphics as an analogy for Gaussian splatting.

image_metadata.caption: A single rasterized Gaussian with border — the core rendering primitive. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately when introducing the Gaussian as the rendering primitive, before discussing its parameters, to ground the parameter discussion in a visible example.
image_metadata.description: A single 2D Gaussian splat rendered on screen with a visible border outline, showing its elliptical shape and soft opacity falloff from center to edge.

image_metadata.caption: Three composited Gaussians — building blocks of a full 3DGS scene. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the single Gaussian image, when scaling up from one primitive to many, to bridge the gap between a single splat and a full scene.
image_metadata.description: Three overlapping 2D Gaussian splats rendered together on screen, demonstrating how multiple Gaussians composite to form a combined image.

image_metadata.caption: 3D Gaussian ellipsoids in space before projection onto the image plane. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the 3D-to-2D projection step, to clarify that Gaussians exist as 3D ellipsoids that are then splatted onto the screen.
image_metadata.description: A 3D visualization of ellipsoids in space, representing 3D Gaussians before projection, showing their varying sizes, orientations, and positions in the scene.

image_metadata.caption: SfM point cloud from COLMAP — the initialization for 3D Gaussian Splatting training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the training pipeline explanation, when describing how SfM output is converted into initial Gaussians before optimization begins.
image_metadata.description: A sparse 3D point cloud of a scene, as produced by Structure from Motion (SfM) using COLMAP, showing scattered colored points representing estimated 3D positions.

image_metadata.caption: NeRF: Representing scenes as continuous volumetric functions. (Mildenhall et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on implicit neural representations or novel view synthesis to establish the paper's contribution
image_metadata.description: The NeRF paper title page or abstract figure showing the core concept of neural radiance fields for novel view synthesis

image_metadata.caption: NeRF pipeline: 5D coordinates → MLP → density & color → rendered image. (Mildenhall et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the NeRF pipeline architecture, especially when introducing how 5D coordinates map to color and density outputs
image_metadata.description: SVG pipeline diagram showing the NeRF method: 5D input coordinates (x,y,z,θ,φ) fed into a fully-connected network, outputting volume density and view-dependent color, then composited via volume rendering into a final image

image_metadata.caption: Camera rays sample 5D coordinates; volume rendering accumulates color per pixel. (Mildenhall et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how NeRF uses volume rendering to synthesize images, particularly when explaining the connection between camera rays and network queries
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how camera rays are cast through a scene, with sample points along each ray queried as 5D coordinates (position + viewing direction) to synthesize a novel view

image_metadata.caption: NeRF MLP: position inputs density; direction inputs view-dependent color. (Mildenhall et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the NeRF network architecture, especially to highlight why viewing direction is injected later in the network than spatial position
image_metadata.description: SVG diagram showing the fully-connected MLP network architecture used in NeRF, illustrating how spatial coordinates (x,y,z) and viewing direction (θ,φ) are input at different stages to produce volume density σ and RGB color output

image_metadata.caption: mip-NeRF casts conical frustums (right) vs. NeRF's thin rays (left) to reduce aliasing. (Barron et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of NeRF and improvements in mip-NeRF, or when teaching anti-aliasing strategies in neural rendering
image_metadata.description: Comparison image showing rays vs. conical frustums for sampling in NeRF vs. mip-NeRF, illustrating how mip-NeRF casts anti-aliased conical frustums instead of infinitesimally thin rays to reduce aliasing at multiple scales

image_metadata.caption: Occupancy Networks predict binary inside/outside occupancy from 3D coordinates. (Mescheder et al., CVPR 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing implicit neural representations as precursors to NeRF; use to contrast with explicit 3D representations (voxels, meshes) before explaining signed distance functions
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing Occupancy Networks architecture: a neural network with 5 ResNet blocks taking a feature vector and 3D point as input and predicting binary occupancy (inside/outside classification) for implicit surface representation

image_metadata.caption: IM-NET's implicit MLP decoder enables GAN-based 3D shape generation. (Chen & Zhang, CVPR 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing IM-NET to Occupancy Networks to show parallel development of implicit representations; useful when discussing how implicit functions enable generative modeling
image_metadata.description: Visualization of 3D shapes generated using a GAN where IM-NET serves as the implicit decoder, showing diverse generated shape outputs from a 6-layer MLP that predicts binary occupancy given a feature vector and 3D coordinate

image_metadata.caption: DeepSDF reconstructs the Stanford bunny via a learned signed distance function. (Park et al., CVPR 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the architectural lineage from DeepSDF to NeRF; highlight the skip-connection design as a direct precursor to NeRF's network architecture
image_metadata.description: Rendering of the Stanford bunny reconstructed through a learned signed distance function (SDF), showing smooth, high-quality surface geometry produced by DeepSDF's 8-layer MLP with skip connections that outputs continuous signed distance values

image_metadata.caption: PIFu projects 3D points to pixel-aligned features for single-view reconstruction. (Saito et al., ICCV 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When bridging from implicit surface methods to NeRF extensions; specifically reference when introducing PixelNeRF to show how pixel-aligned features enable generalization across scenes
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating PIFu's pipeline: 3D points are projected onto image feature maps to extract pixel-aligned features, which are then used to regress both color and SDF values, enabling detailed single-view human reconstruction

image_metadata.caption: Neural Volumes decodes a latent code into a 3D volume for differentiable rendering. (Facebook Reality Labs)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from implicit surface methods to NeRF; use to show that volume rendering for view synthesis predates NeRF and to explain what NeRF improves upon
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Neural Volumes pipeline: a latent code is decoded by a neural network into a 3D volume of density and color, which is then rendered into a new image via volume rendering, showing the encode-decode-render flow

image_metadata.caption: NeRF encodes a scene as MLP weights trained on posed images. (Mildenhall et al., ECCV 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: As the primary introductory diagram when first explaining NeRF; show before the pipeline figure to establish the overall learning setup before detailing the rendering procedure
image_metadata.description: Overview diagram of the NeRF setup showing: multiple input images with known camera poses used to train an MLP that stores the entire scene as network weights, mapping 3D coordinates and view direction to density and color

image_metadata.caption: NeRF renders pixels by integrating density and color along viewing rays. (Mildenhall et al., ECCV 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after the NeRF setup diagram; use to walk through each rendering step in detail, pausing to explain positional encoding and the volume rendering integral
image_metadata.description: Detailed pipeline diagram of NeRF's rendering procedure: rays are cast from a camera, 3D points are sampled along each ray, positional encoding (Fourier features) is applied, the MLP outputs density and color, and numerical integration accumulates these into a final pixel color

image_metadata.caption: NSVF uses a sparse octree with vertex features to accelerate NeRF rendering. (Liu et al., NeurIPS 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing NeRF performance improvements; use to explain how spatial data structures reduce the number of MLP queries needed and why this speeds up both training and rendering
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Neural Sparse Voxel Fields (NSVF): a sparse octree organizes the scene, feature embeddings on voxel vertices are trilinearly interpolated for sampled 3D points, and a smaller MLP outputs density and color — shown as a generalization encompassing both Neural Volumes and NeRF

image_metadata.caption: DNRF embeds a morphable face model into NeRF for parametric 4D avatar control. (Gafni et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When covering NeRF extensions to dynamic scenes and human avatars; contrast with Nerfies' deformation MLP approach to illustrate the spectrum from data-driven to model-based priors
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing Dynamic Neural Radiance Fields (DNRF) for 4D facial avatar reconstruction, showing how a deformable face model (morphable model) is integrated as a strong inductive bias into the NeRF pipeline to enable parametric control over facial dynamics

image_metadata.caption: NeRD decomposes scenes into reflectance and spherical harmonics illumination per scene. (Boss et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how NeRF-based methods can be extended to support relighting by disentangling geometry, reflectance, and illumination; use panel d to anchor the discussion of per-scene illumination inference.
image_metadata.description: Multi-panel diagram of NeRD (Neural Reflectance Decomposition) showing how the method decomposes scenes into reflectance components and learns a global illumination model (spherical harmonics) per scene, with panel d specifically illustrating the illumination model across training scenes.

image_metadata.caption: GRAF adds shape and appearance latent codes to NeRF for generative 3D synthesis. (Schwarz et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from standard NeRF to generative variants; use to contrast scene-specific NeRF with latent-conditioned models that can synthesize novel instances.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating GRAF's architecture, showing how latent codes for shape and appearance are injected into a conditional NeRF model, enabling generative 3D-aware image synthesis and inference via GAN-style training.

image_metadata.caption: OSFs enable composing scenes with objects at arbitrary positions. (Guo et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing compositional neural scene rendering; use the left/position example to motivate why object-centric representations are needed over monolithic scene NeRFs.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF demonstrating Object Scattering Functions (OSFs) composing a scene with objects at arbitrary positions, showing how object-centric neural representations can be repositioned within a scene while maintaining realistic appearance.

image_metadata.caption: OSFs support realistic relighting of composed scenes via Monte Carlo rendering. (Guo et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 3 to complete the two-axis demonstration of OSF capabilities (position and lighting); useful when contrasting OSFs with simpler composition methods like GIRAFFE.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF demonstrating Object Scattering Functions (OSFs) composing a scene with varying lighting conditions, showing how the object-centric neural representation supports realistic relighting of composed scenes.

image_metadata.caption: iNeRF inverts a NeRF model to estimate camera pose via iterative rendering. (Yen-Chen et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing applications of NeRF beyond view synthesis, particularly pose estimation; use to show how a synthesis model can be 'inverted' via gradient-based optimization.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF teaser for iNeRF showing a NeRF model being used inversely: given an observed image, a pose optimizer iteratively refines camera pose estimates by minimizing the difference between NeRF-rendered and observed images.

image_metadata.caption: Agent-environment interaction loop: state, action, reward cycle. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RL introduction, before defining formal terms, to ground the intuition of the learning loop.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the agent-environment interaction loop: an agent observes state and reward from the environment, then takes an action, which feeds back into the environment.

image_metadata.caption: RL algorithm taxonomy: model-based vs. model-free, value vs. policy. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing basic RL concepts and before diving into specific algorithms, as a roadmap students can return to throughout the course.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram categorizing RL algorithms into model-based vs model-free, and within model-free into value-based, policy-based, and actor-critic approaches, with example algorithms placed in each category.

image_metadata.caption: MDP agent-environment interaction with timestep notation. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MDPs formally, after students understand the intuitive loop, to connect intuition to the mathematical framework.
image_metadata.description: The classic Sutton & Barto diagram of the MDP agent-environment interaction cycle, showing the agent receiving state S_t and reward R_t at each timestep and outputting action A_t.

image_metadata.caption: Work-day MDP example: states, actions, and rewards illustrated. (randomant.net)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the formal MDP definition to provide an accessible worked example before moving to mathematical derivations.
image_metadata.description: A fun illustrative MDP example using a typical work day: states include 'home', 'office', 'lunch', etc., with actions and transitions shown as arrows, and rewards annotated on transitions.

image_metadata.caption: Bellman backup diagrams for V(s) and Q(s,a) value functions. (Lilian Weng)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when deriving Bellman equations, using the diagram to trace through the one-step lookahead and explain the expectation over next states.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams illustrating Bellman expectation equations for both state-value V(s) and action-value Q(s,a) functions, showing how current values are computed from successor states and rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Backup diagrams contrasting Q-learning and SARSA update targets. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD control methods to visually contrast how SARSA and Q-learning differ in their update targets before presenting the pseudocode.
image_metadata.description: Backup diagrams comparing Q-learning (off-policy) and SARSA (on-policy) TD control, showing the difference in which next action is used for the update target.

image_metadata.caption: Q-learning (off-policy) vs. SARSA (on-policy) backup diagrams compared. (Sutton & Barto 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show side-by-side when students struggle to articulate the difference between SARSA and Q-learning, pointing to the branching structure to explain the update rule.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side backup diagrams for Q-learning and SARSA, explicitly labeling the greedy max selection in Q-learning versus the policy-sampled next action in SARSA.

image_metadata.caption: DQN algorithm with experience replay and frozen target network. (Mnih et al. 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching DQN after explaining why naive Q-learning with neural networks is unstable, using each step of the pseudocode to explain how replay and frozen targets address instability.
image_metadata.description: The full DQN algorithm pseudocode from Mnih et al. 2015, including experience replay buffer initialization, epsilon-greedy action selection, minibatch sampling, and periodic frozen target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: TD(λ) backup depth interpolates between TD and Monte-Carlo methods. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing TD(λ) after students understand both 1-step TD and MC, to illustrate how λ interpolates between the two extremes.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing backup depth across TD(λ), showing how λ-return interpolates between one-step TD (shallow backup) and full Monte-Carlo (deep backup) using exponentially decaying weights.

image_metadata.caption: MC, TD, and DP backup diagrams: depth and width compared. (David Silver)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a synthesis diagram after covering all three methods, or use it as a preview at the start to give students a conceptual map of where each method sits.
image_metadata.description: Three backup diagrams side by side comparing Monte-Carlo (full episode depth), TD learning (one-step depth), and Dynamic Programming (full-width one-step), illustrating the depth and width of each method's update.

image_metadata.caption: Parallel ES-RL: workers share seeds to reconstruct noise cheaply. (Salimans et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Evolution Strategies as an alternative to MDP/TD-based RL, specifically when explaining how ES scales efficiently via parallelism and why shared random seeds reduce communication cost.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart showing a parallel Evolution Strategies RL algorithm where a master node distributes random seeds to multiple parallel workers, each worker evaluates a perturbed policy in the environment, and returns scalar rewards back to the master for parameter updates. Workers share random seeds to reconstruct Gaussian noise without transmitting full gradient vectors.

image_metadata.caption: 19×19 Go board: liberties and eyes define stone survival rules.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the AlphaGo Zero case study, before discussing the neural network architecture or MCTS, to ensure students understand the environment the RL agent operates in.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a 19x19 Go board showing the placement of black and white stones, illustrating key game concepts: liberties (open intersection points adjacent to a stone group), eyes (enclosed liberties needed for a group to stay alive), and the positional superko rule.

image_metadata.caption: AlphaGo Zero self-play loop: MCTS refines policy each step. (Silver et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how AlphaGo Zero trains purely from self-play, especially to contrast with supervised learning from human expert data used in the original AlphaGo, and to illustrate how MCTS and neural networks interact iteratively.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the AlphaGo Zero self-play training loop: the current neural network policy plays games against itself via MCTS, generating training data (board states, MCTS policy targets, game outcomes), which are used to update the network weights, and the improved network is then used for the next round of self-play.

image_metadata.caption: General policy gradient form; Ψ varies by algorithm. (Schulman et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this immediately after deriving the policy gradient theorem, before discussing specific algorithms, to anchor all subsequent methods to a common formula
image_metadata.description: Mathematical expression showing the general form of the policy gradient, with expectation over state-action distributions under policy π, combining log probability of action and a function Ψ representing various return estimators

image_metadata.caption: A3C uses async updates; A2C synchronizes all actors before updating.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from A3C to A2C to highlight the single architectural change (coordinator/synchronization) that distinguishes the two methods
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architectural diagram comparing A3C (asynchronous) and A2C (synchronous) showing multiple parallel actors, their interaction with a global network, and the role of a coordinator in A2C that synchronizes gradient updates before applying them

image_metadata.caption: DDPG algorithm with soft target updates and exploration noise. (Lillicrap et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the motivation for DDPG (continuous actions, off-policy learning), so students can map each algorithmic step to the conceptual components already discussed
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the DDPG algorithm detailing initialization of critic and actor networks with random weights, replay buffer setup, soft target network updates, exploration noise addition, and the actor-critic update steps per episode

image_metadata.caption: D4PG extends DDPG with distributional critic and N parallel actors. (Barth-Maron et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after DDPG algorithm is understood; use to walk through each of the three D4PG improvements one by one, pointing to the corresponding pseudocode lines
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the D4PG algorithm showing distributed actors feeding a shared replay buffer, distributional critic updates using N-step returns, and priority-weighted sampling, extending DDPG with distributional and distributed components

image_metadata.caption: MADDPG: centralized critics, decentralized actors per agent. (Lowe et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-agent RL challenges; use the diagram to explain why a centralized critic stabilizes training even when agents act independently at test time
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of MADDPG showing multiple agents each with their own actor (using local observations) and a centralized critic (using global observations and all agents' actions), illustrating the centralized training with decentralized execution paradigm

image_metadata.caption: PPG alternates policy and auxiliary phases to decouple training. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the parameter-sharing trade-off in actor-critic methods; use the two-phase structure to show how PPG resolves the conflict between policy and value objectives
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the PPG (Phasic Policy Gradient) algorithm showing two alternating training phases: a policy phase updating the policy network with PPO, and an auxiliary phase updating the value network with distillation loss to prevent policy drift

image_metadata.caption: PPG outperforms PPO in sample efficiency on Procgen. (Cobbe et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the PPG algorithm diagram to validate the claim that phase separation improves sample efficiency; prompt students to identify which environments benefit most
image_metadata.description: Bar chart or learning curve comparing mean normalized performance of PPG versus PPO across multiple Procgen benchmark environments, showing PPG achieving higher sample efficiency

image_metadata.caption: SAC algorithm with entropy regularization and fixed temperature α. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the maximum entropy RL objective; walk through each update step to show how the entropy term modifies standard actor-critic updates
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm showing entropy-regularized policy optimization with soft Q-function updates, policy update via reparameterization, and fixed temperature parameter α balancing reward and entropy

image_metadata.caption: SAC with auto-tuned temperature α via dual gradient descent. (Haarnoja et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the fixed-temperature SAC algorithm; highlight the single added line updating α and explain why this resolves the brittleness of manual temperature selection across different tasks
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode for SAC with automatically adjusted temperature, adding a gradient-based update rule for α that minimizes the difference between current entropy and a target entropy threshold, removing the need for manual temperature tuning

image_metadata.caption: TD3 algorithm pseudocode showing twin critics, delayed updates, and policy smoothing. (Fujimoto et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining each of TD3's three core improvements (clipped double Q-learning, delayed updates, target policy smoothing) so students can see how all three are integrated into a single coherent algorithm.
image_metadata.description: Pseudocode/algorithm box for TD3 (Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient), detailing initialization, actor-critic network setup, replay buffer, and the training loop including target policy smoothing with clipped noise, twin critic updates, and delayed actor/target network updates.

image_metadata.caption: IMPALA architecture: parallel actors feed a central learner with off-policy correction.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing IMPALA's motivation for scalability, before diving into V-trace math, so students first grasp the high-level system design and understand why the behavior and training policies diverge.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of IMPALA showing multiple actor machines generating experience trajectories in parallel and sending them to a central learner, which updates policy and value function parameters and periodically broadcasts updated weights back to actors.

image_metadata.caption: CleanRL ppo.py vs. OpenAI Baselines PPO: average episodic returns over training steps.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students how to validate a new RL implementation — use it to discuss the importance of reproducing baseline results before claiming novelty.
image_metadata.description: Line chart showing average episodic returns over training steps for CleanRL's ppo.py compared against OpenAI Baselines' PPO across multiple classic control or MuJoCo environments, plotted against environment steps.
