image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visualization of attention patterns for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird models, each showing a combination of local (sliding window), global (special tokens), and in Big Bird's case, random attention connections overlaid on a sequence.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention matrix: queries attend only within same hash bucket. (Kitaev et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (complement to Image 10), when students need to see the resulting attention matrix structure or when discussing the computational savings of Reformer.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap-style visualization of the LSH attention matrix showing how queries and keys are grouped into buckets (indicated by colored blocks along the diagonal), with attention restricted to within-bucket pairs, resulting in a block-sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention: four steps — bucketing, sorting, chunking, attention. (Kitaev et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; use before Image 9 to build up from the process to the resulting matrix structure, or when students ask how LSH is implemented in practice.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step process diagram of LSH attention showing four sequential stages: (1) bucketing queries and keys via random projections, (2) sorting by bucket assignment, (3) chunking into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention within chunks.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically when explaining how low-rank approximations reduce O(L²) attention to O(Lk) and why this matters for long sequences.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Linformer architecture diagram showing linear projection layers E_i and F_i added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k. (Right) Log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer vs. standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders matrix ops to avoid the L×L attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, particularly to illustrate why reordering operations (right-to-left vs left-to-right) changes computational complexity.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel computation order diagram: (Left) Standard softmax attention showing the expensive O(L²) matrix multiplication order (Q·K^T first, then softmax, then multiply V). (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) showing the reordered computation (φ(K)^T·V first) that enables linear-time attention via kernel approximation.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ algorithm, specifically to justify why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos features for approximating softmax attention.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel approximation error comparison for Performer: (Left) Bar/line chart comparing approximation error of i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features across different settings. (Right) Chart comparing approximation error of sin/cos random features vs. positive random features, showing positive features yield lower error.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers applied to reinforcement learning, specifically to explain why standard Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how gating and layer norm reordering address this.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) Standard Transformer-XL with layer norm in original position, (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm), and (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using reordered layer norm, enabling stable RL training.

lessons.title: Multi-Modal Agents & Tool Use
lessons.content:
# Multi-Modal Agents & Tool Use

A language model that can *talk about* a website is useful. A model that can **see** the website, **click** the right button, **read** the confirmation, and **recover** when something changes is a different beast.

That’s the core idea behind **multi-modal agents**: systems that combine vision + language + actions (tools) in a feedback loop, so they can operate inside digital environments like browsers, desktop apps, or mobile UIs.

## From “describe” to “do”: what makes something an agent?

A plain VLM answers: “The button says *Submit*.”

An agent goes further:

1. **Observe**: screenshot / DOM / logs
2. **Reason**: decide what to do next
3. **Act**: click, type, call an API, run code
4. **Observe again**: did the UI change as expected?

This loop matters because GUIs are noisy: popups appear, buttons move, network errors happen. Agents need **visual feedback** to stay grounded.

## Visual grounding: connecting words to pixels

To act reliably, the agent must map language like “click the blue ‘Continue’ button” to a concrete target on screen. This is **visual grounding**.

A helpful mental model: the vision side turns the image into a set of features; the language side turns the instruction into features; then the model learns to align them so “Continue button” points to the right region.

A classic way to get this alignment is contrastive image–text training (think: matching captions to images). Once you can score “how well does this text match this image?”, you can do things like zero-shot classification or pick the best candidate UI element.

## How modern VLMs fuse vision + language

Under the hood, many VLMs follow a simple pattern: convert images into token-like embeddings, then let a language model attend to them.

Two common design ideas:

- **Treat image patches/regions like tokens** so the transformer can mix them with text.
- **Bridge a pretrained vision encoder and a pretrained language model**, so you don’t have to train everything from scratch.

This is what enables “interleaved” inputs like: *image, text, image, text…*—which is exactly what GUI interaction feels like over time.

## Tool use: when the model shouldn’t “think”, it should *call*

Even strong models are unreliable at exact arithmetic, calendar math, or fetching fresh facts. Tool use turns those into deterministic steps.

A practical agent prompt often separates:

- **Thought/plan** (private or brief)
- **Tool call** (structured)
- **Tool result** (fed back)
- **Final answer / next action**

![Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Adapted from Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/chain-of-thought/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-03-15-prompt-engineering_006.png)

### Minimal “tool loop” pseudocode

```python
state = {"goal": goal, "history": []}

while True:
    obs = get_screenshot_and_dom()
    action = model.plan(obs, state)  # may return a tool call or a UI action

    if action.type == "tool":
        result = tools[action.name](**action.args)
        state["history"].append((action, result))
    else:
        execute_ui_action(action)  # click/type/scroll
        state["history"].append(action)

    if done(state, obs):
        break
```

The key is that **observations are first-class**. The model isn’t just generating text—it’s steering a process.

## Multi-modal reasoning: chain-of-thought, but grounded

**Chain-of-thought prompting** helps models solve multi-step problems by writing intermediate steps. In multimodal settings, those “steps” often alternate between:

- interpreting the screen (“the checkbox is unchecked”)
- deciding an action (“click checkbox”)
- verifying via feedback (“now it’s checked”)

For harder tasks, you can go beyond a single linear chain and explore multiple candidate plans.

![Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Adapted from Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/chain-of-thought/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-03-15-prompt-engineering_002.png)

## GUI agents in the real world: what breaks, and what helps

Common failure modes:

- **Ambiguous targets** (“Sign in” appears twice)
- **Layout drift** (responsive design, A/B tests)
- **Hidden state** (scroll position, collapsed menus)
- **Long horizons** (20+ steps)

Mitigations that work well:

- Ask for **explicit grounding**: “return bounding box / element id you will click.”
- Use **verification steps**: after acting, describe what changed.
- Store **memory**: keep a compact history of key UI states and decisions.

## Summary

Multi-modal agents turn VLMs from “describe what you see” into “act based on what you see,” by closing the loop between observation, reasoning, tool calls, and visual feedback. The practical art is grounding language to UI targets and using tools for the parts that should be precise or external.

## Recommended Reading

- [Prompt Engineering](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-03-15-prompt-engineering/) — Clear patterns for decomposition, tool use, and reasoning prompts.
- [Vision Language Models](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2022-06-09-vlm/) — A tour of how models fuse image and text representations.
- [Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.11903) — Why intermediate steps unlock multi-step reasoning.
- [Learning Transferable Visual Models From Natural Language Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2103.00020) — How image–text alignment enables flexible visual understanding.
lessons.summary: Multi-modal agents combine vision-language understanding with tool calls and UI actions in a feedback loop, enabling reliable interaction with GUIs and other digital environments. The core challenges are visual grounding, multi-step multimodal reasoning, and using tools for precision and external information.
lessons.video_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models (Paper Explained)

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-pass prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly after covering CoT and self-consistency, to illustrate the conceptual leap to tree-based exploration.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps with evaluation and backtracking.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code instead of verbal reasoning steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing programming-language-based prompting methods (PAL/PoT), especially to contrast with CoT and motivate why symbolic execution is more reliable for math.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs interface with external tools at the token level, before or alongside Toolformer to compare approaches.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LLM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve model performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the TALM self-play mechanism to provide quantitative justification for why iterative tool-use training works.
image_metadata.description: A performance graph showing model accuracy across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use training examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching Toolformer as a case study in self-supervised tool augmentation, after introducing TALM to highlight the shared self-play philosophy and differences in implementation.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram of Toolformer's training process: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via in-context learning, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for calculator, search, and calendar. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to ground the abstract training steps in a concrete annotated example, especially when discussing how different tool types are handled uniformly.
image_metadata.description: A concrete text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls — embedding calculator, Wikipedia search, and calendar tool calls inline within natural text, with results inserted, demonstrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.caption: Transformers as the central model-definition hub across ML frameworks. (HuggingFace)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this when introducing the Transformers library for the first time, especially when explaining why a student should learn Transformers rather than a framework-specific API. Also useful when discussing MLOps tooling choices.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the Transformers library as a central 'pivot' or hub connecting model definitions to downstream frameworks. It likely depicts Transformers at the center with spokes or arrows pointing outward to training frameworks (Axolotl, Unsloth, DeepSpeed, FSDP, PyTorch-Lightning), inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TGI), and adjacent modeling libraries (llama.cpp, mlx).

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion: text prompt in, generated image out. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on Stable Diffusion to anchor the discussion in a familiar, tangible example before introducing architecture
image_metadata.description: A text-to-image example showing a text prompt input on the left and the resulting AI-generated image output on the right, illustrating the basic input/output interface of Stable Diffusion

image_metadata.caption: img2img: Stable Diffusion accepts both image and text inputs. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing text-to-image to contrast the two modes and prompt discussion about how image information is incorporated alongside text
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating image-to-image (img2img) generation in Stable Diffusion, showing both an input image and a text prompt feeding into the system to produce a modified output image

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion: text encoder feeds numeric representations to image generator. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first explaining that Stable Diffusion is not monolithic — use as a scaffold before drilling into each component individually
image_metadata.description: A high-level system diagram showing two main blocks: a 'Text Understanding' component (text encoder) on the left receiving a text prompt, and an 'Image Generator' component on the right producing the output image, with an arrow showing information flow between them

image_metadata.caption: Image generator internals: information creator then decoder. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the high-level architecture diagram to zoom into the image generator and introduce the concept of latent space processing
image_metadata.description: A diagram expanding the image generator block into two sub-stages: (1) Image Information Creator and (2) Image Decoder, with text embeddings flowing in from the text encoder on the left

image_metadata.caption: Image information creator: UNet + scheduler iterate in latent space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why increasing steps improves quality, or when students ask what the 'steps' parameter does in generation interfaces
image_metadata.description: A diagram highlighting the Image Information Creator component, showing it operates iteratively over multiple steps (the 'steps' parameter) in latent space, with a UNet and scheduler inside, before passing output to the image decoder

image_metadata.caption: Three neural networks in Stable Diffusion: CLIPText, UNet, Autoencoder. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary diagram after introducing each component individually, or as a reference when students need to connect component names to their roles
image_metadata.description: A complete architecture diagram labeling all three neural network components of Stable Diffusion: CLIPText (text encoder), UNet (diffusion in latent space), and Autoencoder Decoder (pixel image painter), with their connections shown

image_metadata.caption: Tensor shapes through Stable Diffusion: 77×768 → 4×64×64 → 3×512×512. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students are ready for technical depth — ideal when discussing why latent diffusion is faster than pixel-space diffusion, or when connecting to code implementations
image_metadata.description: A detailed tensor-level architecture diagram showing the exact data shapes flowing between components: text embeddings (77×768), noise latent tensor (4×64×64) into UNet+Scheduler, processed latent (4×64×64) into Autoencoder Decoder, and final image (3×512×512)

image_metadata.caption: Diffusion: random noise latent iteratively refined using text embeddings. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of diffusion — use to explain why the process is called 'diffusion' and how text guides the denoising trajectory
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step diffusion process diagram showing how a random noise latent array is progressively refined over multiple steps using text embeddings and the UNet, with each step producing a less noisy latent until the final array is decoded into an image

image_metadata.caption: UNet applied repeatedly: each step refines the latent toward the prompt. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the UNet's role in the diffusion loop, especially to clarify the relationship between the number of steps and the UNet's repeated application
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple UNet processing steps in sequence, with each step taking the current latent and text embeddings as input and outputting a refined latent, illustrating the repeated application of the UNet across diffusion steps

image_metadata.caption: Denoising steps visualized: structure emerges from noise step by step. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the key intuition-building moment when teaching diffusion — particularly effective when students ask 'what is actually happening at each step?' or when discussing the steps parameter
image_metadata.description: A visual sequence of decoded latent arrays at multiple diffusion steps (e.g., steps 1, 2, 4, 10, 20, 50), showing the progression from pure visual noise to a recognizable, detailed image, demonstrating how structure emerges during denoising

image_metadata.caption: Forward diffusion: clean image (step 0) to pure noise (step 4). — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when first explaining how diffusion models are trained — show it before discussing the noise predictor to establish what the model is learning to reverse
image_metadata.description: A sequence of 5 images showing a photograph progressively transformed from clean (step 0) to pure noise (step 4), illustrating the forward diffusion process used to generate training examples

image_metadata.caption: One training example: original image + noise = noisy image at step t. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the concept of forward diffusion and before explaining the noise predictor architecture, to bridge 'how noise is added' with 'what the model learns'
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single forward diffusion training example: an original image plus a generated noise sample combined to produce a noisy image, with the noise amount labeled, forming one (noisy_image, noise, step) training tuple

image_metadata.caption: Many noisy training examples train the U-Net noise predictor. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use after explaining individual training examples to show how they aggregate into a full training regime; ideal before discussing the training loss or optimization step
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple training examples fed into a U-Net noise predictor, illustrating how a dataset of (noisy image, noise level) pairs is used to train the model to predict the noise component

image_metadata.caption: U-Net training step: predict noise, compute loss, update weights. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present when transitioning from 'what training data looks like' to 'how the model is optimized'; useful for students with ML background to connect diffusion to familiar training paradigms
image_metadata.description: A diagram of a single U-Net training step: a noisy image and timestep are input, the U-Net predicts noise, the predicted noise is compared to the actual noise via a loss function, and gradients update the model weights

image_metadata.caption: One denoising step: subtract predicted noise to recover a cleaner image. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the 'generating images' section, immediately after training is explained, to show how the trained model is used in the opposite direction
image_metadata.description: A diagram of one denoising step during inference: a noisy image and timestep are fed into the trained noise predictor, which outputs a predicted noise slice that is subtracted from the noisy image to produce a slightly cleaner image

image_metadata.caption: Chained denoising steps progressively reconstruct an image from noise. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the single denoising step diagram to extend the concept to a full generation loop; useful for motivating later discussion of faster samplers like DDIM
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a second denoising step chained after the first, illustrating that the output of one denoising step becomes the input of the next, progressively reducing noise across multiple iterations

image_metadata.caption: Full image generation: iterative denoising from pure noise to final image. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining individual denoising steps; ideal for summarizing the generation process before introducing text conditioning or latent diffusion
image_metadata.description: A full end-to-end image generation diagram showing the complete reverse diffusion pipeline: starting from pure random noise, applying many sequential denoising steps via the noise predictor, and arriving at a final generated image

image_metadata.caption: Autoencoder compresses images to latent space where diffusion operates. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from basic diffusion to Stable Diffusion's specific architecture; use to motivate why latent diffusion is faster before showing the full latent diffusion pipeline
image_metadata.description: A diagram of an autoencoder showing an input pixel image compressed by an encoder into a smaller latent representation, then reconstructed back to pixel space by a decoder, with the latent space highlighted as the site of diffusion

image_metadata.caption: Forward diffusion applied to compressed latents, not raw pixels. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the autoencoder diagram to demonstrate how forward diffusion is adapted to latent space; bridges the autoencoder concept with the diffusion training process
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the forward diffusion process applied to latent representations rather than pixel images: an image is encoded to a latent, noise is progressively added to the latent across timesteps, generating latent-space training examples

image_metadata.caption: Complete LDM architecture: forward/reverse latent diffusion with text conditioning. — Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as the master architecture diagram after all individual components have been introduced; ideal for synthesis discussions or as a reference when students ask how text prompts influence generation
image_metadata.description: A two-path diagram (mirroring Figure 3 from the LDM paper) showing the forward process (image → encoder → noisy latents) and reverse process (noisy latents → denoising U-Net → decoder → generated image), with conditioning inputs (text prompts) feeding into the U-Net

image_metadata.caption: Stable Diffusion architecture: latent diffusion with text conditioning. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram when introducing the full Stable Diffusion pipeline, after students understand basic diffusion, to show how pixel-space diffusion is replaced by latent-space diffusion with conditioning.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of Latent Diffusion (Stable Diffusion) showing the forward diffusion process on compressed latents via an autoencoder encoder, the noise predictor (UNet) operating in latent space, the reverse process via the autoencoder decoder, and the text conditioning pipeline feeding into the noise predictor.

image_metadata.caption: ClipText encodes text prompts into token embeddings for image conditioning. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how text prompts are converted into a form the UNet can use; ideal after introducing the overall architecture and before explaining cross-attention.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a Transformer language model (ClipText, a GPT-based model) taking a text prompt as input and producing token embeddings, which are then fed into the image generation process as conditioning vectors.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP trains on 400M image-caption pairs crawled from the web. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the CLIP training explanation, before introducing the contrastive loss, to establish what kind of data CLIP learns from.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of a sample image-caption dataset used to train CLIP, showing paired examples of images and their descriptive text captions, representing the 400-million-pair web-crawled training corpus.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 1: encode image and caption, compare embeddings via cosine similarity. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the first step in a three-step walkthrough of CLIP training, immediately after presenting the dataset diagram.
image_metadata.description: Step 1 of CLIP training: an image and its caption are each passed through their respective encoders (image encoder and text encoder) to produce embeddings, which are then compared using cosine similarity — shown to be low at the start of training.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 2: update encoders so matching image-caption embeddings become similar. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as step 2 in the CLIP training walkthrough, directly after the initial encoding and similarity measurement step.
image_metadata.description: Step 2 of CLIP training: the image and text encoders are updated via backpropagation so that the embeddings of a matched image-caption pair become more similar, illustrating the gradient update step.

image_metadata.caption: CLIP step 3: trained encoders align matching image-text pairs in embedding space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final step of the CLIP training walkthrough to summarize what the trained model achieves and why negative examples are necessary.
image_metadata.description: Step 3 of CLIP training: after repeated training across the dataset with large batch sizes, the encoders produce aligned embeddings where semantically matching image-text pairs have high cosine similarity, and negative (non-matching) pairs have low similarity.

image_metadata.caption: UNet inputs: noisy latent, timestep, and text token embeddings in latent space. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from explaining CLIP embeddings to explaining how those embeddings are used inside the UNet; bridges the text encoder and the denoising network.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the inputs to the Stable Diffusion UNet noise predictor: a noisy latent image, a timestep value, and encoded text token embeddings — all operating in latent space rather than pixel space.

image_metadata.caption: Training dataset: noisy latents paired with text embeddings and target noise. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the noise predictor is trained with text conditioning, to ground the abstract conditioning concept in a concrete data format.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the training dataset for the text-conditioned noise predictor, showing triplets of (noisy latent image, encoded text embedding, target noise) used to train the UNet to predict noise conditioned on text.

image_metadata.caption: Baseline UNet noise predictor: noisy latent and timestep inputs, no text. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing the text-conditioned UNet to establish the baseline; use as a 'before' in a before/after comparison when explaining cross-attention addition.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a diffusion UNet without text conditioning, showing its inputs (noisy latent image and timestep) and output (predicted noise), with internal ResNet blocks visible — serving as a baseline before text conditioning is added.

image_metadata.caption: UNet internal ResNet block layers before text-conditioning attention is added. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when diving into UNet internals, after the high-level UNet diagram, to prepare students for understanding where attention layers are inserted for text conditioning.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the internal layers of the UNet noise predictor with ResNet blocks, illustrating the sequential processing steps within the encoder and decoder paths of the UNet before attention layers are added.

image_metadata.caption: Text-conditioned UNet: cross-attention layers inject text embeddings between ResNet blocks. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the text-free UNet, when explaining how text prompts influence image generation — use it to highlight the structural addition of cross-attention layers and why ResNet blocks alone cannot process text.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a UNet noise predictor modified for text conditioning, showing ResNet blocks interleaved with cross-attention layers that receive text embeddings as input, with labeled inputs (noisy latent + timestep + text tokens) and outputs (predicted noise).

image_metadata.caption: Step-by-step data flow through text-conditioned UNet layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the static architecture diagram (Image 1), when students need to understand the sequential processing order — particularly useful when discussing why text influence accumulates across network depth.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step walkthrough diagram of the text-conditioned UNet noise predictor, illustrating the sequential flow of data through alternating ResNet and cross-attention layers, showing how text token representations are repeatedly merged into the evolving latent representation at each stage.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT fuses text and image region embeddings into a shared BERT encoder. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'joint text-image training' paradigm; use to contrast how image regions are embedded analogously to word tokens in BERT.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualBERT showing how text tokens and visual region embeddings (each summing tokenized features f_o, segmentation embedding f_s, and position embedding f_p) are concatenated and fed into a BERT-style transformer with self-attention layers.

image_metadata.caption: VisualBERT ablation on NLVR shows impact of each training component. (Li et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining VisualBERT's training objectives; use to discuss how researchers isolate the contribution of each design choice.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for VisualBERT on the NLVR benchmark, showing performance metrics across different model configurations (e.g., with/without visual grounding objectives, pre-training stages).

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM encodes image patches as bidirectional prefix; text is decoded autoregressively. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing SimVLM; use to contrast bidirectional vs. causal attention and explain why images are encoded as prefix tokens.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of SimVLM showing image patches processed by a bidirectional prefix encoder (cross-attention) and a text decoder with causal (unidirectional) attention, illustrating the prefix language model design.

image_metadata.caption: SimVLM ablation on VQA: PrefixLM and mixed data yield best results. (Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining SimVLM's training setup; use to reinforce why both the objective choice and data diversity matter in vision-language pre-training.
image_metadata.description: Ablation study table for SimVLM on VQA, comparing performance across variants such as different pre-training objectives (PrefixLM vs. span corruption vs. naive LM) and data mixtures (image-text only vs. combined with text-only C4 data).

image_metadata.caption: CM3 applies causal masking to interleaved text and image tokens from HTML pages. (Aghajanyan et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting CM3 with simpler joint-training models; use to explain how masking strategy enables both generation and infilling in a multimodal context.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the causally-masked multimodal modeling (CM3) approach, showing how HTML-structured web content with interleaved text and image tokens is processed with a causal masking strategy that allows both generation and infilling.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen trains only the vision encoder; the LM remains fixed, enabling few-shot VQA. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the 'learned image embedding as frozen LM prefix' approach; use to contrast with full fine-tuning and explain the motivation for freezing LM parameters.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: (left) Frozen model training where only the vision encoder (NF-ResNet-50) is updated to produce image embeddings compatible with a frozen GPT-style LM; (right) test-time pipeline showing few-shot prompting with image embeddings prepended to text.

image_metadata.caption: Frozen LM outperforms fine-tuned variant on VQA; blind baseline shows LM prior strength. (Tsimpoukelli et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining the Frozen architecture; use to spark discussion on why fine-tuning the LM hurts performance and what the blind baseline reveals about LM priors.
image_metadata.description: Two bar charts comparing performance of Frozen model variants (Frozen, Frozen-scratch, Frozen-finetuned, Frozen-train-blind) on VQAv2 (left) and OKVQA (right), trained on Conceptual Captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap maps CLIP embeddings to GPT-2 prefix vectors via a lightweight network. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When comparing ClipCap to Frozen; use to show how a mapping network replaces direct vision encoder training and discuss the trade-off between transformer vs. MLP mapping.
image_metadata.description: Pipeline diagram of ClipCap showing CLIP vision encoder producing image embeddings, a lightweight mapping network (transformer or MLP) translating them into k prefix vectors in GPT-2 embedding space, and a frozen GPT-2 generating captions.

image_metadata.caption: ClipCap's image prefix embeddings decode to semantically relevant words. (Mokady et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining ClipCap's mapping network; use as an intuition-building example to show that learned visual prefixes are not arbitrary but carry semantic meaning.
image_metadata.description: Visualization showing that ClipCap's learned image prefix embeddings, when decoded back to text, produce words semantically related to the image content (e.g., an image of a dog yields prefix tokens interpretable as 'dog', 'animal', etc.).

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT uses gated cross-attention (SRAU) to balance visual and linguistic signals. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing cross-attention fusion mechanisms; use to contrast with prefix-based approaches and explain how complementary gates prevent catastrophic forgetting of linguistic knowledge.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VisualGPT showing a visual encoder feeding into a GPT-style decoder via cross-attention, with the self-resurrecting activation unit (SRAU) gating mechanism that balances visual information and pre-trained linguistic knowledge using complementary gates B_vis and B_lan.

image_metadata.caption: VisualGPT outperforms baselines with only 0.1%–1% training data. (Chen et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SRAU gating mechanism to illustrate why balancing linguistic and visual signals matters empirically, especially in low-data regimes
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing VisualGPT against baseline models on MS COCO and Conceptual Captions datasets at 0.1% and 1% training data fractions, showing captioning metrics across data-scarce conditions

image_metadata.caption: VC-GPT fuses CLIP-ViT and GPT-2 via cross-attention with self-ensemble. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing VC-GPT to illustrate the architectural solution to catastrophic forgetting before presenting the self-ensemble logit combination formula
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of VC-GPT showing CLIP-ViT visual encoder feeding into cross-attention layers, a language decoder (GPT-2), and a self-ensemble module combining vision-language fused logits with language-only logits

image_metadata.caption: Self-ensemble module significantly boosts VC-GPT captioning performance. (Luo et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the self-ensemble formula to demonstrate its empirical importance via the 'VC-GPT w/o SE' ablation row
image_metadata.description: Results table comparing VC-GPT variants (with/without self-ensemble) against end-to-end image captioning baselines on MS COCO test set using CIDEr, BLEU, METEOR, and SPICE metrics

image_metadata.caption: MERLOT learns spatial and temporal video-language alignment via two objectives. (Zellers et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing MERLOT to contrast image-only vs. video-based pretraining and to explain why temporal reordering is a useful self-supervised objective
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram: left panel shows contrastive frame-caption matching training in MERLOT; right panel shows a joint vision-language transformer trained with MLM loss and a temporal frame reordering task on shuffled video segments

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo injects visual features into a frozen LM via Perceiver and cross-attention. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the Flamingo section to establish the overall architecture before diving into the gated cross-attention details
image_metadata.description: High-level overview diagram of Flamingo showing interleaved image/text inputs processed by a frozen vision encoder and Perceiver resampler, with cross-attention layers inserted between frozen LM layers to inject visual features

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo's tanh-gated cross-attention layer enables stable visual integration. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the Flamingo overview to explain the specific mechanism preventing destabilization of the pretrained LM during multimodal training
image_metadata.description: Detailed architecture diagram and pseudo-code of Flamingo's gated cross-attention-dense layer, showing how a tanh-gated cross-attention output is added to the frozen LM's feed-forward output with a learnable scalar gate initialized to zero

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo performance scales with both model size and few-shot examples. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Flamingo's few-shot capabilities to illustrate that both model scale and number of examples matter, and to motivate why large-scale pretraining is valuable
image_metadata.description: Line plots showing Flamingo performance scaling with model size (3B, 9B, 80B parameters) and number of few-shot examples (0-shot to 32-shot) across multiple vision-language benchmarks

image_metadata.caption: Flamingo matches fine-tuned SoTA on many tasks using only few-shot prompting. (Alayrac et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining Flamingo's training to contextualize the claim that it outperforms fine-tuned SoTA on 6/16 tasks without fine-tuning, prompting discussion of generalization vs. specialization
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive results table comparing Flamingo variants (0-shot, 4-shot, 32-shot, fine-tuned) against state-of-the-art fine-tuned baselines across 16 vision-language tasks including VQA, captioning, and visual reasoning

image_metadata.caption: CoCa jointly trains contrastive and captioning objectives in one architecture. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing CoCa to contrast its from-scratch dual-objective training with the frozen-component approaches of Flamingo and VC-GPT
image_metadata.description: Architecture overview of CoCa showing a unimodal image encoder, a unimodal text decoder for contrastive loss, and a multimodal text decoder with cross-attention for captioning loss, trained jointly from scratch

image_metadata.caption: CoCa uses task-specific attention poolers to adapt to diverse downstream tasks. (Yu & Wang et al. 2022)

lessons.title: Document Understanding & Multi-Modal RAG
lessons.content:
# Document Understanding & Multi-Modal RAG

If you’ve ever tried “normal” RAG on a PDF and felt betrayed, you’ve met the core problem: many documents aren’t *text*, they’re *layouts*. The meaning lives in **where** things are (columns, headers, footnotes), and sometimes in **what they look like** (checkboxes, stamps, chart axes). Multi-modal RAG is the idea of doing retrieval and grounding over *document images* (or layout-aware representations), not just plain strings.

## Why text-only RAG breaks on documents
A typical RAG pipeline embeds chunks of text and retrieves the nearest ones for a query. That works great for articles and markdown.

But documents introduce failure modes:
- **Layout carries semantics**: “Total” next to a number in the bottom-right is different from “Total” in a line item.
- **Tables aren’t sentences**: row/column structure matters more than word order.
- **Charts are visual**: the answer might be a bar height or a legend mapping.

So we need retrieval that can “see” the page.

## Document understanding: text + 2D position (+ pixels)
Classic document AI models treat a page as more than a token sequence. They combine:
- **text tokens** (from OCR or a text layer)
- **bounding boxes** (2D coordinates for each token)
- sometimes **image features** (pixels/patches)

This is the key leap: instead of only learning “word A attends to word B”, the model also learns “this token is *above* that token” or “these tokens form a block.”

A canonical example is the LayoutLM family:
- **LayoutLM**: learns from text + layout (and can incorporate visual cues), but expects OCR tokens + boxes as input.
- **LayoutLMv2**: strengthens the multi-modal story by integrating image features during pretraining and using spatial-aware attention biases.

## OCR-free document understanding (and why it’s exciting)
OCR is useful, but it’s also a brittle dependency: it can be slow, language-specific, and error-prone on scans.

**OCR-free** models aim to answer questions or extract fields directly from the document image, learning an implicit “reading” capability. In practice, many modern DocVQA systems use this style: you provide an image and a question, and the model returns an answer.

That makes multi-modal RAG simpler: you can retrieve *pages* (as images) and let the model read them, rather than building a perfect OCR pipeline first.

## Visual document retrieval: the “R” in multi-modal RAG
Multi-modal RAG needs a retriever that can index and search documents as visual objects.

Conceptually:
1. Convert each page (or region) into a **visual embedding** (often from a vision-language model).
2. Embed the user query into the same space.
3. Retrieve the top-*k* pages/regions.
4. Feed those retrieved images (plus question) to a document QA / VLM reader.

This is where systems like **ColPali** fit in: they’re designed for *document image retrieval*—finding the right page/region even when the clue is layout-y (“the table with Q3 revenue”) rather than a clean text match.

## A minimal “DocVQA-style” inference loop
Even without building full RAG, it helps to see the *reader* interface. Many document QA pipelines look like this:

```python
from transformers import pipeline
from PIL import Image

pipe = pipeline(
    "document-question-answering",
    model="naver-clova-ix/donut-base-finetuned-docvqa",
)

image = Image.open("your-document.png")
question = "What is the purchase amount?"

print(pipe(image=image, question=question)[0]["answer"])
```

In multi-modal RAG, the only difference is that `your-document.png` is not “the one document you already have”—it’s one of the retrieved pages.

## Multi-modal RAG vs text RAG (quick comparison)

| Aspect | Text RAG | Multi-modal document RAG |
|---|---|---|
| What you index | text chunks | page images and/or layout-aware regions |
| What retrieval matches | semantic text similarity | cross-modal similarity (query ↔ page/region) |
| What the reader consumes | text context | images (+ question), sometimes with layout signals |
| Common failure | misses table/layout meaning | misses tiny text, needs good page/region retrieval |

*Table informed by common RAG and document QA pipelines (adapted from Pinecone + Hugging Face docs).* 

## Summary
Multi-modal RAG extends RAG to visually rich documents by retrieving *pages/regions* using visual (cross-modal) embeddings and grounding generation in document QA models that understand layout and pixels. The big win is robustness on tables, forms, and charts—places where “just chunk the text” quietly loses the plot.

## Recommended Reading
- [Document Question Answering (DocVQA) task page](https://huggingface.co/tasks/document-question-answering) — Practical overview + inference pipeline for document QA.
- [LayoutLM documentation](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model_doc/layoutlm) — How text + 2D layout is represented and used.
- [LayoutLMv2 documentation](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/model_doc/layoutlmv2) — Deeper multi-modal pretraining and spatial-aware attention.
- [Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paper](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401) — The core idea of grounding generation with retrieved non-parametric memory.
lessons.summary: Multi-modal RAG brings retrieval-augmented generation to visual documents by retrieving relevant pages/regions using cross-modal embeddings and answering with layout- and image-aware document QA models, often without relying on OCR.

lessons.title: Fine-Tuning Multi-Modal Models
lessons.content:
# Fine-Tuning Multi-Modal Models

A vision-language model (VLM) is like a bilingual brain: one “language” is pixels, the other is text. Pretraining teaches it general visual concepts and general language. **Fine-tuning** is how you teach it your *dialect*: radiology phrasing, invoice layouts, microscope artifacts, your label taxonomy, your answer style.

The catch: VLMs are big, and full fine-tuning is expensive. The modern trick is to *adapt* them with tiny add-ons.

## What “multi-modal fine-tuning” really changes

Most VLMs have (1) a **vision encoder** (turns an image into embeddings), (2) a **language model** (predicts text), and (3) a **connector** (projects vision embeddings into the LM’s space).

Fine-tuning can target different parts:
- **Connector-only**: cheapest; often enough for “new camera / new document style” shifts.
- **LM adapters (LoRA)**: teaches new domain language + how to talk about what it sees.
- **Vision-side tuning**: more compute; useful when the visual features themselves are out-of-domain (e.g., X-rays vs natural images).

In practice, many domain wins come from tuning the LM (and sometimes the connector) while keeping the vision backbone mostly frozen.

## LoRA for VLMs: the “low-rank steering wheel”

**LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)** freezes the original weights and learns a small update 
\(\Delta W\) that’s factorized into two skinny matrices. Instead of learning a full \(d\times d\) update, you learn \(d\times r\) and \(r\times d\) where \(r\ll d\).

Two practical heuristics that matter a lot:
- Apply LoRA broadly (not just a couple attention matrices) if you want strong adaptation.
- Tune **rank `r`** and **alpha**; a common rule of thumb is `alpha ≈ 2*r`.

## QLoRA: LoRA + 4-bit base model

If LoRA is “small updates,” **QLoRA** is “small updates on a compressed base.” You load the pretrained model in **4-bit** (typically NF4 + double quantization), keep it frozen, and train LoRA adapters on top.

![QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization with LoRA adapters to reduce fine-tuning memory. (Dettmers et al.; image from Eugene Yan)](/api/wiki-images/evaluation-benchmarks/images/eugeneyan-writing-llm-patterns_022.webp)

The trade: big memory savings, but training can run slower due to quantization-related overhead.

## Data prep: make the model’s job unambiguous

Multi-modal datasets fail in boring ways. A good example format for VQA-style tuning is:
- **image**: path or bytes
- **prompt**: explicit instruction + question
- **answer**: short, deterministic when possible

Key tips:
- Normalize answer style (units, casing, abbreviations).
- Include “I can’t tell from the image” examples if that’s a valid outcome.
- For documents: keep resolution consistent; avoid aggressive JPEG artifacts.

## Minimal PEFT recipe (QLoRA-style)

```python
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training

bnb = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
    "your-vlm-text-backbone-or-vlm",
    quantization_config=bnb,
    device_map="auto",
)
base = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(base)

lora = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj","k_proj","v_proj","o_proj"],
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)
model = get_peft_model(base, lora)
```

(For a full VLM, you’ll use the model’s multimodal forward pass and a collator that batches images + text.)

## Evaluation: don’t fly blind

For VQA, start with simple, legible metrics:
- **Exact match / accuracy** for closed-set answers.
- **Relaxed match** (e.g., numeric tolerance, unit normalization) for measurements.
- A small **human review set** for “is this clinically/operationally acceptable?”

If you change prompts, adapters, or preprocessing, rerun the same eval slice to catch regressions.

## Deployment: adapters are a feature, not a burden

A nice property of LoRA: you can ship one base model and swap adapters per domain/customer.
- Store adapters separately (small files).
- Optionally merge adapters into weights for simpler serving (at the cost of flexibility).
- Treat prompts + preprocessing as versioned artifacts alongside the adapter.

## Summary

Fine-tuning multi-modal models is mostly about *teaching the language model how to talk about your images*—without paying the full cost of retraining everything. LoRA and QLoRA make this practical: small, swappable adapters on top of frozen (even 4-bit) base models.

## Recommended Reading
- [LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685) — Core idea: low-rank weight updates for efficient adaptation.
- [Making LLMs even more accessible with bitsandbytes, 4-bit quantization and QLoRA](https://huggingface.co/blog/4bit-transformers-bitsandbytes) — Practical view of 4-bit loading + adapter training.
- [PEFT Quantization Guide](https://huggingface.co/docs/peft/main/en/developer_guides/quantization) — How to set up k-bit training with LoRA in the HF stack.
- [Practical Tips for Finetuning LLMs Using LoRA](https://magazine.sebastianraschka.com/p/practical-tips-for-finetuning-llms) — Heuristics on rank/alpha, layer coverage, and overfitting.
lessons.summary: LoRA and QLoRA let you adapt vision-language models to specialized domains by training small adapter weights (optionally on a 4-bit frozen base), achieving strong domain performance with minimal GPU memory and deployable, swappable adapters.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after LoRA is understood, when discussing how to fine-tune very large models under GPU memory constraints; useful for comparing memory footprints.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how 4-bit or 8-bit quantized models can still be fine-tuned, particularly when discussing RLHF use cases where multiple adapters (reward model, value policy) are trained on a single quantized base model.
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF illustrating the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) mechanism, showing how small trainable adapter matrices are added on top of a frozen base model, with the animation likely depicting the forward pass through frozen weights and the parallel low-rank update path.

image_metadata.caption: LLM-powered autonomous agent system overview with planning, memory, and tools. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall framework; return to it as a reference when introducing each individual component.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge and decision tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the ReAct framework; use the side-by-side format to contrast reasoning-augmented vs. Act-only approaches and discuss why Thought steps improve performance.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion framework: actor, evaluator, and self-reflection feedback loop. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Reflexion after ReAct; use the cycle diagram to explain how the memory buffer accumulates reflections and how this differs from standard RL reward signals.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator, Self-Reflection model, and Memory buffer, with trajectories feeding back as verbal reinforcement signals for iterative improvement.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion results on AlfWorld and HotpotQA; hallucination dominates failures. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to ground the theory in experimental results; prompt students to analyze why hallucination dominates over inefficient planning as a failure mode.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trial iterations.

image_metadata.caption: Chain of Hindsight fine-tuning produces incrementally improved outputs from feedback. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when covering self-reflection mechanisms beyond prompting; contrast with Reflexion to show a training-time vs. inference-time approach to agent self-improvement.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning process, where a model receives a sequence of past outputs annotated with feedback ranked by quality, and learns to produce incrementally better outputs conditioned on that history.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation distills RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing advanced self-improvement mechanisms; contrast with CoH and Reflexion to show a third paradigm where the improvement process itself is learned from historical trajectories.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD), showing how multi-episode learning histories from source RL policies are used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning, enabling in-context reinforcement learning.

image_metadata.caption: AD vs. ED, source policy, and RL² on memory/exploration tasks with binary reward. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to validate the approach empirically; use to discuss the role of context window length in enabling in-context RL and the trade-offs between different distillation methods.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, Expert Distillation (ED), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments with A3C, watermaze with DQN), measuring learning efficiency with binary rewards.

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory component section; use the human analogy to motivate why agent memory is structured the way it is before introducing technical implementations like vector stores.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical categorization diagram of human memory types: sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), mapped to their LLM agent analogues.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed trade-off. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing external memory implementation; use to explain why approximate methods are preferred over exact search and how to evaluate the accuracy-latency trade-off in production systems.
image_metadata.description: A recall@10 performance comparison chart of multiple approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) / MIPS algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) plotted against query speed, showing the accuracy-speed trade-off.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)

image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision logic for API selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)

image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, reflection, and planning enable simulated human behavior. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from tool-use to memory and planning components; use it to anchor discussion of how persistent memory and self-reflection enable complex, human-like agent behavior over extended time horizons.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via reflection, and uses a planning module to produce actions in a simulated social environment with 25 virtual characters.

image_metadata.caption: 4-shot 2-class image classification: support set and query example.

image_metadata.description: A visual example of 4-shot 2-class image classification showing two categories (e.g., dogs vs. cats) each with 4 labeled support images and one query image to classify

image_metadata.caption: Siamese CNN: twin weight-sharing branches compute embedding similarity.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing metric-based meta-learning methods, specifically before explaining how the network is trained on pairs and generalizes to unseen classes
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a convolutional Siamese neural network showing two identical CNN branches processing a pair of images, sharing weights, and computing an L1 distance between embeddings to produce a similarity score

image_metadata.caption: Matching Networks: attention over support set embeddings for classification.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Siamese networks to contrast fixed embeddings with context-dependent embeddings, and when explaining how attention replaces nearest-neighbor lookup
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of Matching Networks showing an embedding function f for the query, embedding function g with bidirectional LSTM for support set items, attention-weighted sum over support labels to produce a prediction

image_metadata.caption: Relation Network: learned relation module scores query-support embedding pairs.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Matching Networks to illustrate the progression from fixed to learned distance functions, emphasizing the two-module design
